topics.title: Methods of Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods of Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Non-Linear System?

A **non-linear control system** is a dynamical system whose mathematical model contains at least one non-linear component. Formally, it is a system that does **not** satisfy the principle of **superposition** (i.e., the homogeneity and additivity conditions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):**
> A system is said to be non-linear if it cannot be described by a linear differential equation of the form:
> $$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$$
> where $A$ and $B$ are constant matrices and the state vector $x$ and input $u$ appear only to the **first power**, and are not multiplied together or passed through non-linear functions.

### 1.2 Common Types of Non-Linearities

| S.No. | Type | Example (Physical Form) | Mathematical Form |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Saturation** | Op-amp output limit | $y = \tanh(kx)$ |
| 2 | **Dead-Zone** | Motor stiction zone | $y = 0$ for $\vert x \vert \le d$ |
| 3 | **Relay / On-Off** | Thermostat switch | $y = M \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$ |
| 4 | **Hysteresis** | Magnetic relay, backlash | Multi-valued mapping |
| 5 | **Coulomb Friction** | Dry friction in bearings | $y = F_c \cdot \text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ |
| 6 | **Square-Law** | Pneumatic / hydraulic flow | $y = k x^2$ |
| 7 | **Time-Optimal (Bang-Bang)** | Missile rudder control | $y = \pm M$ |

### 1.3 Why Special Methods of Analysis?

> [!NOTE]
> Linear systems are governed by a rich, closed-form theory (Laplace Transforms, Eigenvalues, Bode, Root-Locus). For non-linear systems, **no single universal analytical tool** exists. We must choose a method based on:
> - **Order** of the system (2nd order → phase plane, higher → describing function / Lyapunov)
> - **Type** of non-linearity (symmetric, asymmetric, time-varying)
> - **Objective** (stability, limit cycle prediction, transient response)

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Boat on a River" Picture:**
> Imagine steering a boat on a river. A **linear system** is like a river with constant width, depth, and current — once you set your rudder angle, the response is predictable and proportional.
> A **non-linear system** is a river with waterfalls, whirlpools, and rocks. The boat's behaviour depends on *where* it is (state-dependent), and tiny changes in steering at one point can lead to wildly different outcomes at another (sensitivity to initial conditions). The "Methods of Analysis" are the navigational tools we use to safely chart this turbulent river.

### 1.5 The Five Principal Methods of Analysis

The KTU 2024 syllabus for **Module 1 — Methods of Analysis** is built around **four primary analytical tools**:

1. **Phase Plane Method** (geometric, restricted to 2nd-order systems)
2. **Describing Function Method** (quasi-linear harmonic approximation)
3. **Linearization** (Jacobian / small-signal approximation)
4. **Lyapunov's Indirect (First) Method** (linearization-based stability test)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Method 1 — Phase Plane Analysis

The phase plane is a 2-D graphical state-space in which the trajectory of a 2nd-order system is plotted. The axes are the two state variables.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- Given $\ddot{x} = f(x, \dot{x})$, introduce $x_1 = x$ and $x_2 = \dot{x}$.
- The system is reduced to two first-order equations:
  $$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = f(x_1, x_2)$$
- Eliminating time $t$ yields the **phase trajectory equation**:
  $$\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{f(x_1, x_2)}{x_2}$$
- **Singular Points (Equilibria)** are where $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$ simultaneously.

**Classification of Singular Points** (via Jacobian eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$):

| Type of Equilibrium | Eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2}$ | Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stable Node | Real, negative, distinct | Trajectories converge in |
| Unstable Node | Real, positive, distinct | Trajectories diverge out |
| Saddle Point | Real, opposite signs | Trajectories diverge along one axis |
| Stable Focus (Spiral) | Complex, $\text{Re}<0$ | Inward spiralling |
| Unstable Focus | Complex, $\text{Re}>0$ | Outward spiralling |
| Center (Vortex) | Purely imaginary | Closed orbits |
| Stable Degenerate Node | Real, equal, negative | Cusp-shaped convergence |

### 2.2 Method 2 — Describing Function (DF) Analysis

**Key Idea:** Replace a non-linear element $N(\cdot)$ by a *quasi-linear* gain $N(A, \omega)$ that depends on input amplitude $A$ and frequency $\omega$. Then apply Nyquist-style techniques.

**Assumptions for validity:**
- System is low-pass (filters out higher harmonics)
- Non-linearity is *odd symmetric* and *memoryless*
- Only the **fundamental component** of the output is retained

**Describing Function Definition:**
$$N(A) = \frac{Y_1}{A} e^{j\phi_1} = \frac{1}{\pi A} \int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\psi) \sin(\psi + \phi) \, d\psi$$

For a single-input, single-output, odd, memoryless non-linearity excited by $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$:
$$N(A) = \frac{1}{\pi A} \int_{0}^{2\pi} y(A \sin\psi) \sin\psi \, d\psi$$

**Limit Cycle Prediction Equation:**
$$1 + N(A) G(j\omega) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The intersection of the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ with the locus $-\tfrac{1}{N(A)}$ predicts a **limit cycle**. Stable if the encirclement condition is satisfied.

### 2.3 Common Describing Functions (Memoryless Non-Linearities)

For input $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$:

| Non-linearity | Output $y(t)$ | Describing Function $N(A)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ideal Relay | $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x)$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ |
| Relay with Dead-Zone ($d$) | $\pm M$ if $\vert x \vert > d$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{A}\right)^2}$, for $A \ge d$ |
| Saturation (slope $k$, limit $M$) | $y = kx$ for $\vert x \vert \le M/k$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{M}{kA}\right) + \dfrac{M}{kA}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{M}{kA}\right)^2}\right]$ |
| Dead-Zone ($d$) | $0$ for $\vert x \vert \le d$ | $N(A) = k - \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{d}{A}\right) + \dfrac{2kd}{\pi A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{d}{A}\right)^2}$ |
| Coulomb Friction | $y = F_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{4F_c}{\pi A}$ |

### 2.4 Method 3 — Linearization (Jacobian / Small-Signal)

For a non-linear system $\dot{x} = f(x, u)$ near an equilibrium $x^*$, $u^*$:

$$f(x^*, u^*) = 0$$

Taylor expand and keep only first-order terms:
$$\dot{x} \approx \underbrace{\left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right|_{x^*, u^*}}_{A}(x - x^*) + \underbrace{\left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial u}\right|_{x^*, u^*}}_{B}(u - u^*)$$

Define $\tilde{x} = x - x^*$, $\tilde{u} = u - u^*$:
$$\dot{\tilde{x}} = A \tilde{x} + B \tilde{u}$$

### 2.5 Method 4 — Lyapunov's Indirect Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Theorem (Indirect Method):**
> - If all eigenvalues of $A$ have **negative real parts** → origin is **asymptotically stable**.
> - If at least one eigenvalue has a **positive real part** → origin is **unstable**.
> - If eigenvalues lie on the $j\omega$-axis (with no positive real parts) → **no conclusion** (non-linear terms decide stability).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Method | Typical Industry Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Phase Plane | Servo-motor control, robotic manipulator joints (2-DOF) |
| Describing Function | Predicting oscillations in relay-controlled thermostatic ovens, autopilot limit cycles |
| Linearization | Trim-point analysis of aircraft, equilibrium analysis of chemical reactors |
| Lyapunov Indirect | Quick stability verdict of inverted pendulum, maglev suspension systems |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Describing Function for an Ideal Relay

**Given:** Non-linearity is an ideal relay (relay with zero dead-zone).
$$y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & x(t) > 0 \\ -M, & x(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Input:** $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$.

**Output Fourier Series:**
$$y(t) = \frac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t)\right]$$

By odd symmetry, $a_0 = 0$ and $a_n = 0$ for all $n$. The fundamental sine coefficient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\psi) \sin\psi \, d\psi \\
&= \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} M \sin\psi \, d\psi + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} (-M) \sin\psi \, d\psi\right] \\
&= \frac{M}{\pi}\left[-\cos\psi\right]_{0}^{\pi} + \frac{-M}{\pi}\left[-\cos\psi\right]_{\pi}^{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{M}{\pi}\left[(-\cos\pi) - (-\cos 0)\right] + \frac{-M}{\pi}\left[(-\cos 2\pi) - (-\cos\pi)\right] \\
&= \frac{M}{\pi}\left[(1) - (-1)\right] + \frac{-M}{\pi}\left[(-1) - (1)\right] \\
&= \frac{2M}{\pi} + \frac{2M}{\pi} = \frac{4M}{\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Describing Function:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N(A) &= \frac{\sqrt{a_1^2 + b_1^2}}{A}\angle \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a_1}{b_1}\right) = \frac{b_1}{A} \\
N(A) &= \frac{4M}{\pi A} \quad \text{(real, frequency-independent)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an ideal relay, $N(A)$ is purely real and amplitude-dependent only. This is the simplest case and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

### 3.2 Derivation: Describing Function for Relay with Dead-Zone

**Given:** Dead-zone width $d$, output magnitude $M$.
$$y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & x > d \\ 0, & \vert x \vert \le d \\ -M, & x < -d \end{cases}$$

**Switching angles:** $x(t) = A\sin\psi = d \Rightarrow \psi_1 = \sin^{-1}(d/A)$ and $\psi_2 = \pi - \psi_1$.

**Fundamental sine coefficient:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\psi)\sin\psi\,d\psi \\
&= \frac{2}{\pi}\int_{\psi_1}^{\pi-\psi_1} M \sin\psi\,d\psi \quad \text{(by symmetry over half period)} \\
&= \frac{2M}{\pi}\left[-\cos\psi\right]_{\psi_1}^{\pi-\psi_1} \\
&= \frac{2M}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\pi-\psi_1) + \cos\psi_1\right] \\
&= \frac{2M}{\pi}\left[\cos\psi_1 + \cos\psi_1\right] \\
&= \frac{4M}{\pi}\cos\psi_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\cos\psi_1 = \cos\!\left(\sin^{-1}(d/A)\right) = \sqrt{1 - (d/A)^2}$:

$$
\boxed{\; N(A) = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2}, \qquad A \ge d \;}
$$

For $A < d$: $N(A) = 0$ (input never exceeds the dead-zone).

### 3.3 Derivation: Phase Trajectory Equation for a 2nd-Order System

**Given:** $\ddot{x} + f(x, \dot{x}) = 0$.

**Step 1:** Define state variables:
$$x_1 = x, \qquad x_2 = \dot{x}$$

**Step 2:** Form the state equations:
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -f(x_1, x_2)$$

**Step 3:** Eliminate time using chain rule:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dx_2}{dt} &= -f(x_1, x_2) \\
\frac{dx_1}{dt} &= x_2
\end{aligned}
\quad \Longrightarrow \quad
\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{-f(x_1, x_2)}{x_2}
$$

**Step 4:** Integration yields the family of phase trajectories. Singular points are where:
$$\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{0}{0} \quad \text{(indeterminate)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(x_1, x_2) = 0 \text{ and } x_2 = 0$$

### 3.4 Worked Example: Phase Plane of a Van der Pol Oscillator

**System:** $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$, where $\mu > 0$ is the non-linear damping coefficient.

**State equations:**
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + \mu(1-x_1^2)x_2$$

**Singular Point:** Setting both derivatives to zero gives $(x_1, x_2) = (0, 0)$.

**Jacobian at origin:**
$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \mu \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Eigenvalues:** $\lambda^2 - \mu\lambda + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \dfrac{\mu \pm \sqrt{\mu^2 - 4}}{2}$.

| Case | $\mu$ value | Eigenvalues | Behaviour at origin |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mu < 2$ | Complex, $\text{Re} = \mu/2 > 0$ | Unstable focus |
| 2 | $\mu = 2$ | Real, equal, positive | Unstable degenerate node |
| 3 | $\mu > 2$ | Real, positive, distinct | Unstable node |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Van der Pol oscillator exhibits a **stable limit cycle** for any $\mu > 0$. Although the origin is unstable, every trajectory spirals outward and is "captured" onto a closed orbit of radius $\approx 2$. This is a classic KTU viva question.

### 3.5 Limit Cycle Stability Criterion (Kochenburger)

When the describing function locus $-\tfrac{1}{N(A)}$ intersects the Nyquist plot $G(j\omega)$ at point $P$:

- **Stable limit cycle** if the locus $-\tfrac{1}{N(A)}$ *leaves* the encirclement of $G$ as $A$ increases.
- **Unstable limit cycle** if the encirclement is *gained* as $A$ increases.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Non-Linear System (Describing Function Setup)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input R_s] --> SUM[Comparator]
    SUM --> B[Linear Plant G_s]
    B --> C[Output Y_s]
    C --> D[Process Variable feedback]
    D --> SUM
    E[Disturbance / Limit Cycle Source] -.-> B
    F[Non-Linear Element N] -.-> B
    G[Quasi-Linear Gain N of A] -.-> F
    H[Harmonic Approximation Filter] -.-> G
```

### 4.2 Phase-Plane Method — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: 2nd order ODE] --> S2[Convert to state form]
    S2 --> S3[Find singular points]
    S3 --> S4{Linearize at each SP}
    S4 --> S5[Compute Jacobian]
    S5 --> S6[Find eigenvalues]
    S6 --> S7{Classify SP}
    S7 --> S8[Node / Focus / Saddle / Center]
    S8 --> S9[Sketch trajectories using isoclines]
    S9 --> S10[Identify limit cycle if any]
```

### 4.3 Describing Function Method — Procedural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Identify non-linearity type] --> A2[Compute Fourier coefficient b1]
    A2 --> A3[Form N of A equals b1 over A]
    A3 --> A4[Plot minus one over N locus in G plane]
    A4 --> A5[Plot Nyquist of G j omega]
    A5 --> A6{Intersection exists?}
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Compute limit cycle amplitude A and frequency omega]
    A6 -- No --> A8[System is stable, no limit cycle]
    A7 --> A9[Check stability by Kochenburger criterion]
```

### 4.4 Classification of Singular Points — Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Singular Point] --> P1{Real eigenvalues?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2{Same sign?}
    P1 -- No --> P3{Complex conj pair?}
    P2 -- Same sign positive --> P4[Unstable Node]
    P2 -- Same sign negative --> P5[Stable Node]
    P2 -- Opposite signs --> P6[Saddle Point]
    P3 --> P7{Re part positive?}
    P3 --> P8{Re part negative?}
    P3 --> P9{Re part zero}
    P7 --> P10[Unstable Focus]
    P8 --> P11[Stable Focus]
    P9 --> P12[Center or Vortex]
```

### 4.5 Closed-Loop Limit-Cycle Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Input R] --> C1[Comparator e equals R minus Y]
    C1 --> N[Non-linearity N]
    N --> G1[Linear Part G1]
    G1 --> P[Plant G2]
    P --> Y[Output Y]
    Y --> FB[Feedback path]
    FB --> C1
    DF[Describing Function N of A] -.-> N
    NY[Nyquist of G1 G2] -.-> P
    INV[Minus one over N locus] -.-> DF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
*Define a non-linear control system. List any four common types of non-linearities encountered in physical systems.*

**Model Answer:**
A non-linear control system is one whose governing differential equation contains at least one non-linear operator (product, trigonometric, dead-zone, etc.), and therefore does **not** obey the principle of superposition.

Four common non-linearities:
1. **Saturation** (e.g., op-amp output clipping)
2. **Dead-zone** (e.g., stiction in DC motor)
3. **Relay / On-Off** (e.g., thermostat controller)
4. **Hysteresis** (e.g., backlash in gears)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
*State the conditions under which the Describing Function Method gives an accurate prediction of limit cycle behaviour.*

**Model Answer:**
The Describing Function Method is valid when:
1. The system contains only **one** non-linear element (or can be reduced to one).
2. The linear part $G(s)$ is a **good low-pass filter** — it attenuates all higher harmonics of the output by at least a factor of 10.
3. The non-linearity is **memoryless** (no dynamics inside it).
4. The non-linearity is **odd-symmetric** (so that the input/output relation generates only sine terms in Fourier expansion).
5. The system is **unforced** (or has constant DC input) so that any sustained oscillation is a **limit cycle**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For a relay with dead-zone $d$ and output level $M$, derive an expression for the **Describing Function** $N(A)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**
Given $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$ and the non-linearity
$$y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & A\sin\psi > d \\ 0, & \vert A\sin\psi \vert \le d \\ -M, & A\sin\psi < -d \end{cases}$$

The switching angles occur at $\sin\psi = d/A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_1 &= \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{A}\right) \\
\psi_2 &= \pi - \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{A}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Fundamental sine coefficient (using half-period symmetry):

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{\psi_1}^{\pi/2} M \sin\psi \, d\psi \\
&= \frac{4M}{\pi}\bigl[-\cos\psi\bigr]_{\psi_1}^{\pi/2} \\
&= \frac{4M}{\pi}\bigl[0 + \cos\psi_1\bigr] \\
&= \frac{4M}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence,
$$\boxed{\, N(A) = \frac{b_1}{A} = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2}, \qquad A \ge d \,}$$

For $A < d$: $N(A) = 0$.

---

**(b)** A non-linear system has $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$ and an ideal relay with $M = 1$. Using the Describing Function Method, **predict whether a limit cycle exists** and find its **amplitude and frequency**. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

For ideal relay: $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A} = \dfrac{4}{\pi A}$.

**Limit cycle condition:** $G(j\omega) = -\dfrac{1}{N(A)} = -\dfrac{\pi A}{4}$.

**Step 1: Compute $G(j\omega)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(j\omega) &= \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega + 1)(j\omega + 2)} \\
&= \frac{10}{-3\omega^2 + j\omega(2 - \omega^2)} \\
&= \frac{10\bigl[-3\omega^2 - j\omega(2 - \omega^2)\bigr]}{9\omega^4 + \omega^2(2 - \omega^2)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Equate real and imaginary parts.**

Imaginary part of $G(j\omega)$ must equal zero since $-\pi A/4$ is purely real:

$$
\omega(2 - \omega^2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt{2} \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $G(j\sqrt{2})$:**

$$
G(j\sqrt{2}) = \frac{10}{-3(2)} = \frac{10}{-6} = -\frac{5}{3}
$$

**Step 4: Equate to $-\pi A / 4$:**

$$
-\frac{\pi A}{4} = -\frac{5}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{20}{3\pi} \approx 2.122
$$

**Result:** A limit cycle exists with $\omega \approx 1.414$ rad/s and $A \approx 2.12$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Phase Plane Method** of analysis. Classify the singular points based on the nature of eigenvalues of the Jacobian matrix. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

The Phase Plane Method is a graphical technique used to study the **qualitative behaviour** of second-order systems. The system $\ddot{x} = f(x, \dot{x})$ is reduced to two first-order equations using $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = \dot{x}$:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = f(x_1, x_2)$$

**Singular points** satisfy $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$. Their nature is determined by the Jacobian $J = \left[\partial f_i / \partial x_j\right]$ evaluated at the singular point, with eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2}$.

**Classification Table:**

| Eigenvalue Nature | Singular Point Type | Stability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Real, distinct, both negative | Stable Node | Asymptotically stable |
| Real, distinct, both positive | Unstable Node | Unstable |
| Real, opposite signs | Saddle Point | Unstable |
| Complex, $\text{Re} < 0$ | Stable Focus (Spiral) | Asymptotically stable |
| Complex, $\text{Re} > 0$ | Unstable Focus (Spiral) | Unstable |
| Pure imaginary | Center (Vortex) | Stable (not asymptotic) |
| Real, equal, negative | Stable Degenerate Node | Asymptotically stable |

---

**(b)** Consider $\ddot{x} + \dot{x} + x^3 = 0$. Find the **singular points** and analyze their **stability** using Lyapunov's indirect method. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**State-space form:**
$$x_1 = x, \quad x_2 = \dot{x}$$
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = -x_2 - x_1^3$$

**Singular point:** $\dot{x}_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$; $\dot{x}_2 = 0 \Rightarrow -x_2 - x_1^3 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1^3 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0$.

**Only singular point:** $(0, 0)$.

**Jacobian:**
$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_1}{\partial x_2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -3x_1^2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

At $(0, 0)$:
$$
J\big|_{(0,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Characteristic equation:** $\det(\lambda I - J) = \lambda(\lambda + 1) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 0, \lambda_2 = -1$.

**Conclusion:** Since one eigenvalue is zero (on the $j\omega$-axis), **Lyapunov's indirect method gives no conclusion** about stability. The origin is *non-hyperbolic*, and the non-linear term $x^3$ must be examined directly.

In this case, energy-based Lyapunov analysis $V = \tfrac{1}{2}x_1^4 + \tfrac{1}{2}x_2^2$ shows the origin is **asymptotically stable** (a non-linear result not detectable by linearization).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Module 1:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant $\pi$ in describing function derivations** — students often write $N(A) = 4M/A$ instead of $4M/(\pi A)$. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $b_1$ and $N(A)$:** $b_1$ is the Fourier coefficient (in Volts or N·m); $N(A) = b_1/A$ is the *gain* (dimensionless). Don't divide twice.
> 3. **Missing the $A \ge d$ condition** in the relay-with-dead-zone DF — examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **In phase-plane problems, students often forget to classify singular points using Jacobian eigenvalues** — they only find the equilibrium and stop. Always complete the classification.
> 5. **Lyapunov's indirect method on a non-hyperbolic point (eigenvalue on $j\omega$ axis):** Many students wrongly conclude "stable" or "unstable". The correct answer is *no conclusion can be drawn*.
> 6. **In limit-cycle problems, always state the Kochenburger stability criterion** — examiners award 2 marks specifically for this.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Non-linear systems** violate superposition; no universal closed-form solution.
- [x] **Phase plane** is restricted to 2nd-order systems; uses $x_1, x_2$ state space.
- [x] **Singular points** are where $\dot{x}_1 = \dot{x}_2 = 0$; classification via Jacobian eigenvalues.
- [x] **Six types** of singular points: stable/unstable node, stable/unstable focus, saddle, center.
- [x] **Describing function** $N(A) = b_1/A$ for memoryless, odd-symmetric non-linearities.
- [x] **Ideal relay DF** = $4M/(\pi A)$ — purely real and amplitude-dependent.
- [x] **Relay + dead-zone DF** = $\frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - (d/A)^2}$, valid only for $A \ge d$.
- [x] **Limit cycle condition** = $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$ ↔ $G(j\omega) = -1/N(A)$.
- [x] **Stability of limit cycle** checked by **Kochenburger criterion** (encirclement changes with $A$).
- [x] **Linearization** via Jacobian: $\dot{\tilde{x}} = A\tilde{x} + B\tilde{u}$ around equilibrium.
- [x] **Lyapunov indirect method**: valid only for **hyperbolic** equilibrium points (no eigenvalues on $j\omega$ axis).
- [x] **Van der Pol oscillator** is the canonical example — unstable origin, stable limit cycle.
- [x] For **higher-order** non-linear systems, prefer **Describing Function** or **Lyapunov direct** method (Module 2).
- [x] **Memoryless** non-linearities: no time delays, no dynamics inside the block.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classifications of Nonlinearities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classifications of Nonlinearities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Nonlinear System?

A **nonlinear system** is a dynamical system whose mathematical model does not satisfy the principle of superposition. Formally, a system described by an operator $N[\cdot]$ acting on input $u(t)$ to produce output $y(t)$ is nonlinear if, for any two inputs $u_1, u_2$ and scalars $\alpha, \beta$:

$$N[\alpha u_1(t) + \beta u_2(t)] \neq \alpha N[u_1(t)] + \beta N[u_2(t)]$$

Equivalently, the system is governed by a nonlinear differential (or difference) equation of the form:

$$F\bigl(t,\, y(t),\, \dot y(t),\, \ddot y(t),\, \dots,\, u(t),\, \dot u(t),\, \dots\bigr) = 0$$

where $F[\cdot]$ is **not** a linear functional of $y$ and $u$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A nonlinear control system contains one or more nonlinear elements such that the principle of superposition does not hold. Such systems are described by nonlinear differential equations and cannot be analyzed using classical linear techniques such as Laplace transforms or transfer functions in the usual sense."

## 1.2 Why Do We Classify Nonlinearities?

Because there is **no single universal tool** (like the transfer function for linear systems) to handle all nonlinearities, classification allows the engineer to:

- Choose the **right analytical method** for each category (describing function, phase-plane, Lyapunov, etc.).
- Predict qualitative behaviour such as **limit cycles, jump resonance, subharmonic oscillations**, and **chaos**.
- Design compensators tailored to a specific nonlinearity type.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: A Sailing Boat & Wind Force**
> Think of a small sailing boat. When wind is gentle (small input $u$), doubling the wind force roughly doubles the boat's speed (linear behaviour). But when a storm hits (large input $u$), the boat cannot accelerate further — it has reached a maximum speed limited by hull drag and water resistance. The boat's response "**saturates**" at high inputs and exhibits "**dead-zone**" behaviour at low inputs (no motion until wind overcomes static friction). Each of these nonlinear effects is a distinct **class** of nonlinearity that engineers recognise and handle differently.

## 1.4 Primary Classification Axes (KTU 2024 Module 1)

Nonlinearities are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Class A | Class B |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Memory** | Static (memoryless) | Dynamic (with memory) |
| **Continuity** | Continuous | Discontinuous |
| **Linearity** | Single-valued | Multi-valued |
| **Intent** | Intentional (designed) | Incidental (unavoidable) |

The most academically important distinction taught at KTU is **Static vs. Dynamic**, which directly leads to whether the **Describing Function Method** (DFM) can be applied.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Nonlinearity Classification

```
NONLINEARITIES
├── STATIC (Memoryless)  → y(t) = f(u(t))
│     ├── Continuous, Single-valued
│     │     ├── Saturation (Limiter)
│     │     ├── Dead Zone (Threshold)
│     │     ├── Quadratic / Cubic (Multiplicative)
│     │     └── Piecewise-linear
│     ├── Discontinuous, Single-valued
│     │     ├── Ideal Relay (On-Off)
│     │     ├── Relay with Dead Zone
│     │     ├── Pre-loaded Relay
│     │     └── Quantizer
│     └── Multi-valued (Hysteresis type, but static)
│           ├── Relay with Hysteresis
│           └── Backlash
└── DYNAMIC (with Memory) → y(t) = f(u(t), u̇, ü, …)
      ├── Friction (Coulomb + Viscous + Stiction)
      ├── Transport Delay (pure time delay)
      ├── Rate-dependent hysteresis
      └── State-dependent (e.g., centripetal terms)
```

## 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Class

### 2.2.1 Saturation Nonlinearity

- **Definition:** Output grows linearly with input only up to a limit $\pm a$; beyond that, output is clamped at $\pm M$.
- **Why it occurs:** Magnetic saturation in motors, amplifier limits, valve opening limits.
- **Mathematical form:**

$$y = \begin{cases} k u, & \vert u \vert \le a \\ k\,a\,\text{sign}(u), & \vert u \vert > a \end{cases}$$

- **Effect on system:** Reduces effective loop gain at large signals → may slow response or cause limit cycle.

### 2.2.2 Dead-Zone Nonlinearity

- **Definition:** No output for inputs within a band $\vert u \vert \le a$; linear outside.
- **Why it occurs:** Static friction, transistor threshold voltage, valve overlap.
- **Mathematical form:**

$$y = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert u \vert \le a \\ k\bigl(u - a\,\text{sign}(u)\bigr), & \vert u \vert > a \end{cases}$$

- **Effect:** Introduces a positional error called **dead-band error**; can cause **limit cycles** in servos.

### 2.2.3 Ideal (On-Off) Relay

- **Definition:** Output switches between two fixed levels $\pm M$ with no dead zone and no hysteresis.
- **Mathematical form:** $y = M \cdot \text{sign}(u)$.
- **Effect:** Aggressive control action; almost always produces sustained **limit cycle** when placed in a feedback loop.

### 2.2.4 Relay with Dead Zone

$$y = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert u \vert \le a \\ M\,\text{sign}(u), & \vert u \vert > a \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.5 Relay with Hysteresis

$$y = \begin{cases} +M, & \text{if } u > +a \text{ or } (u \ge -a \text{ and } y_{prev} = +M) \\ -M, & \text{if } u < -a \text{ or } (u \le +a \text{ and } y_{prev} = -M) \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.6 Backlash

- **Definition:** Mechanical play between mating gears; the output does not change until the input has moved by a dead-zone width $2a$ to take up the gap.
- **Mathematical form (rate-based):** $\dot y = k\,\dot u$ if $\dot u > 0$ and $y \ne k(u - a)$, or $\dot u < 0$ and $y \ne k(u + a)$; otherwise $\dot y = 0$.

### 2.2.7 Multiplicative (Square / Cube) Nonlinearity

- **Form:** $y = k_1 u + k_2 u^2 + k_3 u^3$.
- **Occurrence:** Aerodynamic drag, fluid flow, non-linear spring stiffness.
- **Special case:** Cubic nonlinearity $y = k u^3$ is odd → only odd harmonics in the output.

### 2.2.8 Quantizer

- **Form:** $y = q\,\text{round}(u/q)$ where $q$ is the quantum step.
- **Occurrence:** Digital controllers, ADC, communication encoders.
- **Effect:** Introduces **quantization noise**; in feedback, can cause **limit cycles** known as "dead-band cycles."

### 2.2.9 Friction (Dynamic Nonlinearity)

$$F_f = F_c\,\text{sign}(\dot \theta) + B\,\dot \theta + F_s\,e^{-\dot \theta / \dot \theta_s}\,\text{sign}(\dot \theta)$$

- $F_c$ = Coulomb friction (constant)
- $B\dot\theta$ = Viscous friction (linear in velocity)
- $F_s$ term = **Stribeck effect** (negative slope near zero velocity)

### 2.2.10 Time Delay

$$y(t) = u(t - \tau), \quad \tau > 0$$

- Infinite-dimensional, dynamic nonlinearity; can destabilise an otherwise stable closed-loop system.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Describing Functions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Describing Function (DF)** $N(X)$ of a memoryless nonlinearity is the complex ratio of the fundamental component of the output to the sinusoidal input $u(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$:
>
> $$N(X) = \frac{Y_1}{X}\angle\phi_1 = \frac{1}{X}\Bigl(A_1 + jB_1\Bigr)$$
>
> where $A_1 = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} y(\theta)\cos\theta\,d\theta$, $B_1 = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} y(\theta)\sin\theta\,d\theta$.

| Nonlinearity | Parameter Definitions | Describing Function $N(X)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Saturation (slope $k$, sat. level $M$, $a=M/k$) | $X \le a$ | $N(X) = k$ |
| Saturation (continued) | $X > a$ | $N(X) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\Bigl[\arcsin\!\dfrac{a}{X} + \dfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}\Bigr]$ |
| Dead Zone (slope $k$, half-width $a$) | $X \le a$ | $N(X) = 0$ |
| Dead Zone (continued) | $X > a$ | $N(X) = k - \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\Bigl[\arcsin\!\dfrac{a}{X} + \dfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}\Bigr]$ |
| Ideal Relay (output $\pm M$) | $X>0$ | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}$ (real) |
| Relay with Dead Zone (output $\pm M$, threshold $a$) | $X \le a$ | $N(X) = 0$ |
| Relay with Dead Zone (continued) | $X > a$ | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}$ |
| Relay with Hysteresis (output $\pm M$, switch points $\pm a$) | $X > a$ | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2} - j\dfrac{4Ma}{\pi X^2}$ |
| Backlash (slope $k$, half-width $a$) | $X > a$ | $N(X) = \dfrac{k}{\pi}\Bigl[\dfrac{\pi}{2} + \arcsin\!\bigl(1-\tfrac{2a}{X}\bigr) + 2\bigl(1-\tfrac{2a}{X}\bigr)\sqrt{\tfrac{a}{X}\bigl(1-\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)}\Bigr] - j\,\dfrac{4ka}{\pi X}\bigl(1-\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)$ |
| Coulomb + Viscous Friction ($F_c$ + $B\dot\theta$) | $X>0$ | $N(X) = B + \dfrac{4F_c}{\pi X}$ (real, frequency independent) |
| Cubic $y = k u^3$ | All $X$ | $N(X) = \dfrac{3}{4}kX^2$ (real) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Saturation & Dead Zone** — every operational amplifier and servo valve exhibits both; understanding their DFs prevents under-designed controllers.
- **Backlash** — gear trains in robotics and CNC machines; describing function predicts the **chattering limit cycle** at low speeds.
- **Relay** — bang-bang controllers in temperature and attitude control (e.g., spacecraft thrusters); DF is used to size the switching boundary.
- **Friction** — precision machine tools and hard-disk drives; DFs of friction allow analysis of stick-slip instabilities.
- **Quantizer** — digital PID loops; DF predicts amplitude of the quantization-induced limit cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 General Derivation of the Describing Function

Let the input be a pure sinusoid: $u(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$. Let $\theta = \omega t$. The output $y(\theta)$ of a static nonlinearity is periodic with the same fundamental period $2\pi$ (assuming the nonlinearity is symmetric and the input is symmetric — otherwise DC and second harmonic terms appear).

The Fourier series of $y(\theta)$ is:

$$y(\theta) = \frac{A_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\bigl(A_n\cos n\theta + B_n\sin n\theta\bigr)$$

with

$$A_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\theta)\cos n\theta\, d\theta, \quad B_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\theta)\sin n\theta\, d\theta$$

**The Describing Function hypothesis** assumes the higher harmonics ($n\ge 2$) are filtered by the low-pass nature of the linear part $G(s)$ of the closed loop. Hence only the first harmonic $n=1$ matters:

$$N(X) = \frac{\sqrt{A_1^{\,2} + B_1^{\,2}}}{X}\,\angle\!\phi_1, \quad \phi_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\bigl(A_1/B_1\bigr)$$

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 1: Ideal Relay $y = M\,\text{sign}(u)$

**Setup:** $u(t) = X\sin\theta$, $X>0$. The output is a square wave: $y = +M$ for $0<\theta<\pi$ and $y = -M$ for $\pi<\theta<2\pi$.

**Step 1 — Compute $A_1$:** Because the square wave is odd, $A_1 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute $B_1$:**

$$B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\theta)\sin\theta\,d\theta = \frac{1}{\pi}\Bigl[\int_{0}^{\pi} M\sin\theta\,d\theta + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi}(-M)\sin\theta\,d\theta\Bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} M\sin\theta\,d\theta = M\bigl[-\cos\theta\bigr]_{0}^{\pi} = M(-\cos\pi + \cos 0) = M(1+1) = 2M$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second integral:**

$$\int_{\pi}^{2\pi}(-M)\sin\theta\,d\theta = -M\bigl[-\cos\theta\bigr]_{\pi}^{2\pi} = M\bigl[\cos 2\pi - \cos\pi\bigr] = M(1+1) = 2M$$

**Step 5 — Sum and divide:**

$$B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}(2M + 2M) = \frac{4M}{\pi}$$

**Step 6 — Form the DF:**

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X} \quad\blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 2: Saturation Nonlinearity

**Setup:** $u(t) = X\sin\theta$, $X>0$, saturation threshold $a$, output slope $k$, saturation level $M = ka$.

The output is linear in the interval where $\vert X\sin\theta\vert \le a$, i.e., $\vert\theta\vert \le \theta_1$ and $\vert\pi - \theta\vert \le \theta_1$, where

$$\theta_1 = \sin^{-1}\!\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)$$

In the linear region $y = kX\sin\theta$. Outside, $y = M\,\text{sign}(\sin\theta)$.

**Step 1 — Compute $A_1$:** $y$ is odd $\Rightarrow A_1 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute $B_1$:**

$$B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\theta_1} kX\sin\theta\cdot\sin\theta\,d\theta + \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{\theta_1}^{\pi/2} M\sin\theta\,d\theta$$

(The factor 4 accounts for symmetry over all four quadrants.)

**Step 3 — First integral:**

$$\frac{4kX}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\theta_1}\sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta = \frac{4kX}{\pi}\Bigl[\frac{\theta}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\theta}{4}\Bigr]_{0}^{\theta_1} = \frac{2kX\theta_1}{\pi} - \frac{2kX\sin\theta_1\cos\theta_1}{\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Second integral:**

$$\frac{4M}{\pi}\bigl[-\cos\theta\bigr]_{\theta_1}^{\pi/2} = \frac{4M}{\pi}\bigl(0 + \cos\theta_1\bigr) = \frac{4M\cos\theta_1}{\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $M = ka$, $\sin\theta_1 = a/X$, $\cos\theta_1 = \sqrt{1-(a/X)^2}$:**

$$B_1 = \frac{2kX\theta_1}{\pi} - \frac{2kX}{\pi}\cdot\frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2} + \frac{4ka}{\pi}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the last two terms:**

$$-\frac{2ka}{\pi}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2} + \frac{4ka}{\pi}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2} = \frac{2ka}{\pi}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}$$

**Step 7 — Use $\theta_1 = \sin^{-1}(a/X)$:**

$$B_1 = \frac{2kX}{\pi}\sin^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{a}{X}\Bigr) + \frac{2ka}{\pi}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}$$

**Step 8 — Divide by $X$ to obtain the DF:**

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{2k}{\pi}\Bigl[\sin^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{a}{X}\Bigr) + \frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}\Bigr] \quad\blacksquare$$

**Sanity check:** As $X \to \infty$, $a/X \to 0$, so $\sin^{-1}(a/X) \to a/X$ and $\sqrt{1-(a/X)^2} \to 1$:

$$N(X) \to \frac{2k}{\pi}\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X} + \tfrac{a}{X}\bigr) = \frac{4ka}{\pi X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}$$

This matches the **ideal-relay DF** for a very large signal — the saturated nonlinearity looks like a relay. ✓

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 3: Relay with Dead Zone

**Setup:** $u(t)=X\sin\theta$, $X>0$, threshold $a$, output $\pm M$ ($M>a$).

The output is zero in the dead zone $\vert\theta\vert\le\theta_1$ and $\vert\pi-\theta\vert\le\theta_1$, with $\theta_1=\sin^{-1}(a/X)$.

**Step 1 — $A_1 = 0$ (odd symmetry).**

**Step 2 — Compute $B_1$:**

$$B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{\theta_1}^{\pi/2} M\sin\theta\,d\theta = \frac{4M}{\pi}\bigl[-\cos\theta\bigr]_{\theta_1}^{\pi/2} = \frac{4M}{\pi}\cos\theta_1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\cos\theta_1 = \sqrt{1-(a/X)^2}$:**

$$B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}$$

**Step 4 — DF:**

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Sanity checks:**
- $X \to a^{+}$: $N \to 0$ (input just at threshold, no switching yet) ✓
- $a \to 0$: $N \to 4M/(\pi X)$ (recovers ideal relay) ✓

## 3.5 Worked Derivation 4: Cubic Nonlinearity $y = k u^3$

**Setup:** $u(t) = X\sin\theta$, $X>0$.

**Step 1 — Output:** $y = kX^{3}\sin^{3}\theta$.

**Step 2 — Trigonometric identity:** $\sin^{3}\theta = \dfrac{3\sin\theta - \sin 3\theta}{4}$.

**Step 3 — Compute $B_1$:**

$$B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} kX^{3}\sin^{3}\theta\,\sin\theta\,d\theta = \frac{kX^{3}}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sin^{4}\theta\,d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Use $\sin^{4}\theta = \dfrac{3-4\cos 2\theta + \cos 4\theta}{8}$.** The integral of $\cos 2\theta$ and $\cos 4\theta$ over $0$ to $2\pi$ is zero. Hence:

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sin^{4}\theta\,d\theta = \frac{3}{8}\cdot 2\pi = \frac{3\pi}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $B_1$:**

$$B_1 = \frac{kX^{3}}{\pi}\cdot\frac{3\pi}{4} = \frac{3kX^{3}}{4}$$

**Step 6 — DF:**

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{3kX^{2}}{4} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Note:** $N$ is **amplitude-dependent but real**, so the cubic nonlinearity adds a signal-dependent gain with no phase lag.

## 3.6 Limit-Cycle Prediction Using DFs (Conceptual Recipe)

For a unity feedback loop containing a nonlinearity $N(X)$ and a linear part $G(j\omega)$, the characteristic equation is:

$$1 + N(X)\,G(j\omega) = 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(X)}$$

The point $(X^*, \omega^*)$ of intersection of the **Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$** with the **negative reciprocal of $N(X)$** gives the predicted limit cycle (amplitude $X^*$, frequency $\omega^*$).

Stability rule (extended Kochenburger):
- If the intersection is **outside** the encirclement of $G(j\omega)$, the limit cycle is **stable**.
- If **inside**, it is **unstable**.

## 3.7 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Nonlin. Type | Output Waveform Symmetry | Odd Harmonics? | Real/Complex DF | Limit Cycle Tendency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Saturation | Odd | Yes (1,3,5…) | Real | Weak (damped) |
| Dead Zone | Odd | Yes | Real | Strong |
| Ideal Relay | Odd | Yes (1,3,5…) | Real | Very strong |
| Dead-Zone Relay | Odd | Yes | Real | Strong |
| Hysteresis Relay | Asymmetric | Yes | **Complex** (phase lag) | Strong, phase-shift |
| Backlash | Asymmetric | Yes | **Complex** | Strong at low speed |
| Cubic | Odd | 1, 3 only | Real | Subharmonic (1/3) possible |
| Quantizer | Stepped | All | Real | Dead-band cycles |
| Friction | Odd | All | Real | Stick-slip |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Hierarchy: Master Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Nonlinearities] --> B1[Static Memoryless]
    A0 --> B2[Dynamic With Memory]

    B1 --> C1[Continuous Single Valued]
    B1 --> C2[Discontinuous Single Valued]
    B1 --> C3[Multi Valued Hysteresis Type]

    C1 --> D1[Saturation Limiter]
    C1 --> D2[Dead Zone]
    C1 --> D3[Piecewise Linear]
    C1 --> D4[Multiplicative Squared Cubed]

    C2 --> D5[Ideal Relay On Off]
    C2 --> D6[Relay with Dead Zone]
    C2 --> D7[Preloaded Relay]
    C2 --> D8[Quantizer]

    C3 --> D9[Relay with Hysteresis]
    C3 --> D10[Backlash Gear Play]

    B2 --> E1[Friction Coulomb Viscous Stiction]
    B2 --> E2[Time Delay Transport Lag]
    B2 --> E3[Rate Dependent Hysteresis]
    B2 --> E4[State Dependent Dynamics]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Nonlinearity in a Closed-Loop System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Reference Input R] --> A2[Summing Junction]
    A2 --> A3[Linear Compensator Gc]
    A3 --> A4[Nonlinearity N of X]
    A4 --> A5[Plant Gp]
    A5 --> A6[Output Y]
    A6 --> A7[Feedback Sensor H]
    A7 --> A2

    A4 -. DF Model .-> A8[Describing Function N X]
    A8 -. For Limit Cycle Analysis .-> A9[Predict X Omega]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Flow: Choosing an Analytical Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[Given Nonlinearity] --> F2{Static or Dynamic}
    F2 -->|Static| F3{Discontinuous}
    F2 -->|Dynamic| F4[Use Phase Plane or Lyapunov]

    F3 -->|No| F5[Use Describing Function Method]
    F3 -->|Yes| F6{Single or Multi Valued}
    F6 -->|Single| F5
    F6 -->|Multi| F7[Use Extended DF with Phase]

    F5 --> F8[Find N X]
    F4 --> F9[Construct Trajectories]
    F7 --> F8
    F8 --> F10[Solve 1 plus N X G j omega equals 0]
    F10 --> F11[Predict Limit Cycle Amplitude and Frequency]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid: Mapping Nonlinearities to Engineering Hardware

```mermaid
graph TD
    H1[Saturation] --> H2[OpAmp Output Limit]
    H1 --> H3[Motor Magnetic Saturation]
    H1 --> H4[Valve Full Open Limit]

    H5[Dead Zone] --> H6[Transistor Threshold Vbe]
    H5 --> H7[Static Friction in Slides]
    H5 --> H8[Valve Overlap Zone]

    H9[Backlash] --> H10[Gear Train in Robotics]
    H9 --> H11[Spline Couplings]
    H9 --> H12[Steering Racks in Vehicles]

    H13[Relay] --> H14[Bang Bang Temperature Control]
    H13 --> H15[Spacecraft Thruster Firing]
    H13 --> H16[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]

    H17[Friction] --> H18[CNC Machine Slides]
    H17 --> H19[Hard Disk Drive Head Positioner]
```
