# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Classify the following nonlinearities as static/dynamic and single-valued/multi-valued: (i) Saturation, (ii) Backlash, (iii) Coulomb friction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Nonlinearity | Static / Dynamic | Single / Multi-valued |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Saturation | Static (memoryless) | Single-valued |
| (ii) | Backlash | Static (memoryless, but rate-dependent output) | Multi-valued |
| (iii) | Coulomb friction | **Dynamic** (depends on sign of velocity, hence on $\dot u$) | Single-valued |

**Marking pattern:** 1 mark per correct row, 0 if one of the two columns is wrong.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define describing function. State its significance in nonlinear control analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The describing function $N(X)$ of a static nonlinearity is the ratio of the fundamental component of the output to the amplitude of a sinusoidal input $u(t) = X\sin\omega t$, expressed as a complex number dependent on $X$ (and sometimes $\omega$):

$$N(X) = \frac{1}{X}\Bigl(A_1 + jB_1\Bigr)$$

- **Computation (1 mark):** $A_1 = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}y(\theta)\cos\theta\,d\theta$, $B_1 = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}y(\theta)\sin\theta\,d\theta$.

- **Significance (1 mark):** It is a **quasi-linear approximation** that allows application of Nyquist-type analysis to predict **limit cycles** in feedback systems. It extends the concept of "gain" to a non-linear block, with the added feature that the gain is **amplitude dependent**.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Set 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 choice pattern]`

**Q. A (a)** With neat input-output characteristics, derive the describing function of an **ideal relay** $y = M\,\text{sign}(u)$. (7 marks)

**Q. A (b)** Classify the various types of nonlinearities encountered in control systems. Give at least one real-world example for each class. (7 marks)

### Question B — Set 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 choice pattern]`

**Q. B (a)** Derive the describing function of a **saturation nonlinearity** with linear slope $k$ and saturation level $\pm M$. Discuss the limiting cases $X\to 0$ and $X\to\infty$. (7 marks)

**Q. B (b)** Explain **backlash** nonlinearity. Sketch its input-output characteristic and derive its describing function. State the condition for a limit cycle. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — Describing Function of Ideal Relay (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State the assumption and input form:** Apply $u(t) = X\sin\omega t$ with $X>0$. Output is a square wave: $y(\theta) = +M$ for $0<\theta<\pi$ and $y(\theta) = -M$ for $\pi<\theta<2\pi$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute the Fourier coefficients:** Since the wave is odd, $A_0 = A_1 = 0$.

$$B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}y(\theta)\sin\theta\,d\theta = \frac{1}{\pi}\Bigl[\int_0^{\pi}M\sin\theta\,d\theta + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi}(-M)\sin\theta\,d\theta\Bigr]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Evaluate integrals:**

$$= \frac{M}{\pi}\bigl[2 + 2\bigr] = \frac{4M}{\pi}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Form the describing function:**

$$\boxed{N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Comment:** $N(X)$ is **real and amplitude-dependent**, decreasing as $1/X$. There is **no phase lag** because the output square wave is in phase with the input sinusoid.

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — Classification with Examples (7 marks)

**Classification (4 marks):**

1. **Static (Memoryless) Nonlinearities** — output depends only on the instantaneous input.
   - *Example:* Saturation in an op-amp output stage.

2. **Dynamic (with Memory) Nonlinearities** — output depends on the input and its derivatives.
   - *Example:* Coulomb + viscous friction in a mechanical slide.

3. **Intentional Nonlinearities** — deliberately introduced to improve performance.
   - *Example:* Bang-bang (relay) controller in a thermostat.

4. **Incidental (Unavoidable) Nonlinearities** — exist due to physical limitations.
   - *Example:* Dead zone due to transistor threshold voltage $V_{BE}$.

5. **Continuous Nonlinearities** — output is a continuous function of input.
   - *Example:* Saturation, dead zone, square-law, cubic.

6. **Discontinuous Nonlinearities** — output has jump discontinuities.
   - *Example:* Ideal relay, quantizer.

7. **Single-Valued Nonlinearities** — one output value for each input value.
   - *Example:* Saturation, dead zone.

8. **Multi-Valued (Hysteresis) Nonlinearities** — output depends on the direction of input change.
   - *Example:* Backlash, magnetic hysteresis, relay with hysteresis.

**Examples (3 marks, distributed):** as above.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — Describing Function of Saturation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the nonlinearity:** With slope $k$, saturation level $M$, threshold $a = M/k$:

$$y = \begin{cases} kX\sin\theta, & \vert X\sin\theta\vert \le a \\ M\,\text{sign}(\sin\theta), & \vert X\sin\theta\vert > a \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Define the crossover angle:** $\theta_1 = \sin^{-1}(a/X)$.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Compute $B_1$ (already shown in Section 3.3):**

$$B_1 = \frac{2kX}{\pi}\sin^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{a}{X}\Bigr) + \frac{2ka}{\pi}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — DF:**

$$\boxed{N(X) = \frac{2k}{\pi}\Bigl[\sin^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{a}{X}\Bigr) + \frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1-\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)^2}\Bigr]}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Limiting cases:**
- $X \le a$: $N(X) = k$ (linear region — full gain).
- $X \to \infty$: $N(X) \to 4M/(\pi X) \to 0$ (saturated — gain collapses).

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — Backlash and its Describing Function (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Description and sketch:** Backlash is the mechanical play between mating gears. Output $y$ does not change until the input $u$ has moved by the half-width $a$ to take up the gap. The input-output curve is a **parallelogram** traced counter-clockwise.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Mathematical description (rate form):**

$$\dot y = \begin{cases} k\dot u, & \text{if } \dot u>0 \text{ and } y \ne k(u-a), \text{ or } \dot u<0 \text{ and } y\ne k(u+a) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks) — DF derivation (sketch):** Apply $u=X\sin\theta$. The output consists of:
- Flat portions (during reversal, $y$ stays constant while $u$ traverses the dead band).
- Linear portions with slope $k$.

By integrating over one period, the DF is:

$$N(X) = \frac{k}{\pi}\Bigl[\frac{\pi}{2} + \arcsin\!\bigl(1-\tfrac{2a}{X}\bigr) + 2\bigl(1-\tfrac{2a}{X}\bigr)\sqrt{\tfrac{a}{X}\bigl(1-\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)}\Bigr] - j\,\frac{4ka}{\pi X}\bigl(1-\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Limiting cases:**
- $X \to \infty$: $N(X) \to k$ (backlash becomes negligible relative to input swing).
- $X \to a^{+}$: $N(X) \to 0$ (input is just enough to engage the tooth).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Limit-cycle condition:** For a loop $G(s)$ with backlash, solve $1+N(X)G(j\omega)=0$, i.e.:

$$G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(X)}$$

The intersection of the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ with the negative reciprocal of $N(X)$ gives the limit cycle $(X^*, \omega^*)$. Backlash typically introduces **phase lag** in $N(X)$, which can destabilise a system that is stable for the linear plant alone.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting symmetry.** When computing $A_1$ and $B_1$, students often integrate over $[0,\pi]$ and forget the factor of 2 (or 4) for the symmetric quadrants. This gives an answer off by 2 or 4. *(Lose 1–2 marks.)*
> 2. **Mixing up the half-width $a$ and full width $2a$.** Convention varies by textbook — KTU consistently uses **half-width $a$**. Always state your convention explicitly. *(Lose 0.5–1 mark.)*
> 3. **Calling back-lash a "static" nonlinearity incorrectly.** Some students classify backlash as purely static; it is **static in input-output mapping** but the *trajectory* (rate of change) is path-dependent. Both interpretations are partially correct, but the safer KTU answer is: **"Static, multi-valued."**
> 4. **Skipping the limiting-case verification.** Examiners reward checking $X\to 0$ and $X\to\infty$ for sanity. Always include this. *(Lose 1 mark if missing.)*
> 5. **Forgetting to mark units or state assumptions** (e.g., $X>0$, odd-symmetric nonlinearity, sinusoidal input). Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing assumption.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition of nonlinear system:** violates superposition principle; modelled by nonlinear ODEs.
- **Four classification axes:** Memory (static/dynamic), Continuity (continuous/discontinuous), Linearity (single/multi-valued), Intent (intentional/incidental).
- **Static (memoryless)** nonlinearities: $y = f(u)$ only — Saturation, Dead Zone, Relay (all variants), Quantizer, Square, Cubic, Multiplicative.
- **Dynamic (with memory)** nonlinearities: depend on $\dot u, \ddot u, \dots$ or past values — Friction, Time delay, Rate-dependent hysteresis.
- **Single-valued** = one output per input (Saturation, Dead Zone, Cubic). **Multi-valued** = output depends on direction of input change (Backlash, Hysteresis).
- **Describing function** $N(X) = (A_1 + jB_1)/X$, computed from the first Fourier coefficient of the output for a sinusoidal input.
- **Key DFs to memorise (KTU-high-yield):**
  - Ideal relay: $N(X) = 4M/(\pi X)$
  - Relay with dead zone: $N(X) = (4M/\pi X)\sqrt{1-(a/X)^2}$
  - Saturation: $N(X) = (2k/\pi)\bigl[\sin^{-1}(a/X) + (a/X)\sqrt{1-(a/X)^2}\bigr]$
  - Dead zone: $N(X) = k - (2k/\pi)\bigl[\sin^{-1}(a/X) + (a/X)\sqrt{1-(a/X)^2}\bigr]$
  - Hysteresis relay: **complex** DF (real minus imaginary)
  - Cubic $y = ku^3$: $N(X) = (3/4)kX^2$ (real, amplitude dependent)
  - Friction: $N(X) = B + 4F_c/(\pi X)$
- **Limit cycle condition:** $1 + N(X)G(j\omega) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; G(j\omega) = -1/N(X)$. Intersection of $G(j\omega)$ with $-1/N(X)$ gives $(X^*, \omega^*)$.
- **Stability rule:** Limit cycle is stable if the intersection lies *outside* the Nyquist encirclement of $-1$ for the linear system; unstable if inside.
- **Convention at KTU:** half-width $a$, slope $k$ in the linear region, $X$ = input amplitude, $\omega$ = input frequency, $M$ = output magnitude for relays.
- **Common engineering examples:** Op-amp saturation, transistor dead zone, gear backlash, valve overlap, Coulomb friction, time delay in networks, quantizer in digital control.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Classifications of Nonlinearities** ✔

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Physical Nonlinearities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Common Physical Nonlinearities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In control theory, a **Physical Non-Linearity** is a system element whose input-output relationship cannot be described by a single, time-invariant linear differential equation satisfying the principle of superposition. In the context of KTU's Non-Linear Control syllabus, common physical nonlinearities refer to the static (memoryless) and dynamic (memory-based) input-output characteristics introduced by real-world hardware components such as actuators, sensors, valves, and motors. The two principal mathematical frameworks for analyzing them are the **Describing Function Method** (for quasi-linear approximation) and the **Phase Plane Method** (for second-order systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Physical nonlinearities are the *bridge* between idealized linear mathematics and the messy reality of engineering hardware. Every motor saturates, every gear has backlash, and every valve has a dead zone.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine you are driving a car and pressing the **accelerator pedal**:
- For small pedal travel, the car barely moves (**Dead Zone**).
- As you press harder, speed increases linearly.
- Press the pedal all the way to the floor — speed does *not* increase infinitely; it levels off at a maximum (~150 km/h). This flattening is **Saturation**.
- When you shift gears, the gear teeth must clear each other before engaging, causing a brief period where engine power does not reach the wheels. This is **Backlash**.
- A thermostat that clicks ON above 25°C and OFF below 24°C exhibits **Relay (On-Off)** behavior with **Hysteresis**.

In short: linear control assumes the pedal-to-speed graph is a perfectly straight line from origin to infinity. Physical nonlinearities tell us the *real graph bends, breaks, and loops*.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Non-Linear Control
Linear control design (root locus, Bode, Nyquist) fails or gives dangerously optimistic results when physical nonlinearities dominate. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically demands that students **identify**, **mathematically model**, and **predict the system-level impact** (e.g., limit cycles, steady-state error, instability) of these nonlinearities.

### 1.4 Classification of Physical Nonlinearities
Physical nonlinearities are broadly classified into two categories:

| Category | Property | Memory | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static (Memoryless)** | Output depends *only* on present input | No | Saturation, Dead Zone, Relay, Quantization |
| **Dynamic (Memory-based)** | Output depends on input *and its history* | Yes | Backlash, Hysteresis, Friction |

> [!NOTE]
> Static nonlinearities are easier to handle analytically (Describing Function works well). Dynamic nonlinearities often require phase-plane analysis or state-space modeling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Saturation Nonlinearity
**Physical Origin:** Every physical actuator (motor, valve, amplifier) has a maximum output capability limited by supply voltage, current, magnetic flux saturation, or mechanical stoppers.

**Operating Principle:**
- **Linear region:** For $\vert e \vert \le a$, output is a linear gain $K \cdot e$.
- **Saturated region:** For $\vert e \vert > a$, output is clamped at $\pm M$.

The transition is sharp (idealized) or smooth (real). Beyond the linear range, increasing input yields *zero additional output*, breaking the superposition principle.

### 2.2 Dead Zone (Deadband) Nonlinearity
**Physical Origin:** Hydraulic valves need minimum pressure to move the spool; small input signals produce no response.

**Operating Principle:**
- For $\vert e \vert \le \Delta$, output is exactly **zero**.
- For $\vert e \vert > \Delta$, output follows a linear slope $K \cdot (e - \Delta \cdot \text{sign}(e))$.

The system "ignores" small commands. It introduces a **threshold** below which the controller is effectively blind.

### 2.3 Backlash Nonlinearity
**Physical Origin:** Loose gear meshing, lead screw clearances, or any mechanical coupling where the output element must traverse a *gap* before engaging the input.

**Operating Principle:**
- The output follows input *with a delay*. The width of the gap is the **backlash width** $2j$.
- When input reverses direction, output remains *constant* until the input has traversed the backlash gap.
- This makes output a **multivalued function** of input during reversal — fundamentally non-functional (in the mathematical sense), requiring differential rather than algebraic description.

### 2.4 Relay (On-Off) Nonlinearity
**Physical Origin:** Thermostats, electrical contactors, bang-bang controllers in spacecraft thrusters.

**Operating Principle:**
- Ideal relay: $u = M \cdot \text{sign}(e)$ — instantaneous switch.
- Relay with dead zone: $u = 0$ for $\vert e \vert \le a$, else $\pm M$.
- Relay with hysteresis: switches ON at $e = +a$, OFF at $e = -a$.

### 2.5 Hysteresis Nonlinearity
**Physical Origin:** Magnetic hysteresis in iron-core inductors, mechanical stress-strain loops in materials, friction at rest (stiction).

**Operating Principle:** Output depends on the *direction* of input motion. The same input value can produce *two different* outputs depending on whether the input is increasing or decreasing. This creates a **closed loop** (B-H curve) in the input-output plane.

### 2.6 Friction Nonlinearity
**Physical Origin:** Contact between moving mechanical parts.
- **Static (Stiction):** Friction force at zero velocity, $F_s$.
- **Coulomb (Dry) Friction:** Constant friction opposing motion, $F_c \cdot \text{sign}(\dot{x})$.
- **Viscous Friction:** Linear in velocity, $B \cdot \dot{x}$.

Total friction: $F(\dot{x}) = F_c \cdot \text{sign}(\dot{x}) + B \cdot \dot{x}$ (with stiction when $\dot{x} = 0$).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Describing Functions of Common Nonlinearities

> [!NOTE]
> The **Describing Function (DF)** is the first-harmonic approximation $N(A)$ of a nonlinearity's response to a sinusoidal input $e(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$. For an odd-symmetric memoryless nonlinearity with input $A\sin(\omega t)$:
> $$ N(A) = \frac{2}{\pi A} \int_{0}^{\pi} y(A\sin\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta $$

| Non-Linearity | Describing Function $N(A)$ | Key Parameter | Limiting Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Relay** | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ | $M$ = output magnitude | $\to \infty$ as $A \to 0$ |
| **Relay with Dead Zone** | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right)^2}$, for $A \ge a$ | $a$ = dead-zone half-width | $0$ for $A < a$ |
| **Saturation** | $N(A) = \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right) + \dfrac{a}{A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right)^2}\right]$, for $A \ge a$ | $K$ = linear slope, $a$ = linear range | $\to K$ as $A \to 0$ |
| **Dead Zone** | $N(A) = K - \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right) + \dfrac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}\right]$, for $A \ge \Delta$ | $\Delta$ = dead-zone half-width | $0$ for $A < \Delta$ |
| **Coulomb Friction** | $N(A) = \dfrac{4F_c}{\pi A}$ (in phase with input velocity) | $F_c$ = friction force | $\to \infty$ as $A \to 0$ |

**Why these matter:** In the Nyquist plot, the linear part $G(j\omega)$ and $-1/N(A)$ are drawn on the same plane. If the two curves **intersect**, a **limit cycle** (sustained oscillation) exists. This is the KTU board favourite for 14-mark problems.

### 2.8 Engineering Utility
- **Saturation:** Used in anti-windup compensator design.
- **Dead Zone:** Compensated with dither signal in valve control.
- **Backlash:** Gear-train preloading, dual-arm robotic control.
- **Relay:** Foundation of sliding-mode and bang-bang optimal control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Describing Function of an Ideal Relay

**Given:** An ideal relay with output $y(t) = M \cdot \text{sign}(e(t))$, and sinusoidal input $e(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the output waveform.**
Since the relay is an **odd function** and memoryless, the output is a **square wave** of amplitude $\pm M$ in phase with the input:
$$ y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & 0 < \omega t < \pi \\ -M, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases} $$

**Step 2 — Fourier series expansion of the square wave.**
The output $y(t)$ is an odd function, so only sine terms appear. The fundamental component is:
$$ y_1(t) = B_1 \sin(\omega t) $$

The coefficient $B_1$ is computed as:
$$ B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta, \quad \theta = \omega t $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral by symmetry.**
Since the square wave is odd and quarter-wave symmetric:
$$ B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} M \sin\theta \, d\theta $$

$$ B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi} \left[-\cos\theta\right]_{0}^{\pi/2} $$

$$ B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi} \left[ -0 - (-1) \right] = \frac{4M}{\pi} $$

**Step 4 — Form the Describing Function.**
The describing function is the ratio of the fundamental output amplitude to the input amplitude:
$$ N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} = \frac{4M}{\pi A} $$

This is a **real, positive, amplitude-dependent** quantity. As $A \to 0$, $N(A) \to \infty$ (relay becomes an infinite gain for tiny signals), and as $A \to \infty$, $N(A) \to 0$ (relay becomes negligible for huge signals).

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Describing Function of Saturation

**Given:** Saturation nonlinearity with linear gain $K$ for $\vert e \vert \le a$, and output clamped at $\pm M$ for $\vert e \vert > a$, where $M = K a$.

**Step 1 — Express output for sinusoidal input.**
Let $e(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ with $A \ge a$. Define the saturation angle $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(a/A)$.
$$ y(t) = \begin{cases} K A \sin(\omega t), & \vert \omega t \vert \le \alpha \\ M, & \alpha < \omega t < \pi - \alpha \\ K A \sin(\omega t), & \pi - \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \end{cases} $$

**Step 2 — Set up the fundamental sine coefficient.**
By odd symmetry:
$$ B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\alpha} K A \sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta \, d\theta + \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} M \sin\theta \, d\theta \right] $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral (linear region).**
$$ I_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\alpha} K A \sin^{2}\theta \, d\theta = \frac{4KA}{\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[\theta - \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2}\right]_{0}^{\alpha} $$

$$ I_1 = \frac{2KA}{\pi} \left[\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right] $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second integral (saturated region).**
$$ I_2 = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} M \sin\theta \, d\theta = \frac{4M}{\pi} \left[-\cos\theta\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} $$

$$ I_2 = \frac{4M}{\pi} \left[ 0 - (-\cos\alpha) \right] = \frac{4M \cos\alpha}{\pi} $$

**Step 5 — Sum and simplify.**
$$ B_1 = I_1 + I_2 = \frac{2KA}{\pi}\left[\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right] + \frac{4M\cos\alpha}{\pi} $$

Using $\sin 2\alpha = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha$ and $M = Ka = KA\sin\alpha$:
$$ B_1 = \frac{2KA}{\pi}\left[\alpha - \sin\alpha\cos\alpha\right] + \frac{4KA\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}{\pi} $$

$$ B_1 = \frac{2KA}{\pi}\left[\alpha + \sin\alpha\cos\alpha\right] $$

**Step 6 — Form the Describing Function.**
$$ N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} = \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\alpha + \sin\alpha\cos\alpha\right] $$

With $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(a/A)$ and $\sin\alpha\cos\alpha = \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-(a/A)^2}$:
$$ N(A) = \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) + \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^2}\right], \quad A \ge a $$

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Describing Function of Dead Zone

**Given:** Dead-zone nonlinearity with zero output for $\vert e \vert \le \Delta$ and slope $K$ outside, for input $e(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, $A \ge \Delta$.

**Step 1 — Define the dead-zone angle.** $\beta = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/A)$.

**Step 2 — Output expression.**
$$ y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert \omega t \vert \le \beta \\ K(A\sin\theta - \Delta), & \beta < \omega t < \pi - \beta \\ 0, & \pi - \beta \le \omega t \le \pi \end{cases} $$

**Step 3 — Fundamental component (by odd symmetry):**
$$ B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{\beta}^{\pi/2} K(A\sin\theta - \Delta) \sin\theta \, d\theta $$

**Step 4 — Split the integral:**
$$ B_1 = \frac{4KA}{\pi} \int_{\beta}^{\pi/2} \sin^{2}\theta \, d\theta - \frac{4K\Delta}{\pi} \int_{\beta}^{\pi/2} \sin\theta \, d\theta $$

**Step 5 — Evaluate.**
$$ \int_{\beta}^{\pi/2} \sin^{2}\theta \, d\theta = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \beta + \frac{\sin 2\beta}{2}\right] $$

$$ \int_{\beta}^{\pi/2} \sin\theta \, d\theta = \cos\beta $$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify:**
$$ B_1 = \frac{4KA}{\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \beta + \frac{\sin 2\beta}{2}\right] - \frac{4K\Delta}{\pi}\cos\beta $$

Using $\Delta = A\sin\beta$ and $\sin 2\beta = 2\sin\beta\cos\beta$:
$$ B_1 = \frac{2KA}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \beta + \sin\beta\cos\beta\right] - \frac{4KA\sin\beta\cos\beta}{\pi} $$

$$ B_1 = KA - \frac{2KA}{\pi}\left[\beta + \sin\beta\cos\beta\right] $$

**Step 7 — Describing Function.**
$$ N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} = K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\beta + \sin\beta\cos\beta\right] $$

$$ N(A) = K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right) + \frac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}\right] $$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Impact of Nonlinearities on System Performance

| Non-Linearity | Steady-State Error | Transient Response | Stability | KTU Board Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Saturation** | Increases (output capped) | Slower due to reduced effective gain | Stable (output bounded) | Very High |
| **Dead Zone** | Increases (small inputs lost) | Sluggish near setpoint | Stable | High |
| **Backlash** | Oscillatory SS error | Causes limit cycles / hunting | Can become unstable | High |
| **Relay** | Zero (bang-bang) | Fast but oscillatory | Limit cycles guaranteed | Very High |
| **Hysteresis** | Multi-valued SS error | Hysteresis loop widens response | Marginal | Medium |
| **Coulomb Friction** | Constant offset error | Stick-slip oscillation | Marginal in low-velocity | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Non-Linear Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates where physical nonlinearities enter a closed-loop control system, and how the describing function method is applied for stability analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reference Input r t] --> S[Summing Junction]
    S --> E[Error e t = r - y]
    E --> NL[Physical Nonlinearity N e]
    NL --> U[Control Signal u t]
    U --> G1[Linear Plant G sub s]
    G1 --> Y[Output y t]
    Y --> F1[Feedback Path H sub s]
    F1 --> S

    NL -.-> DF[Describing Function N of A]
    DF -.-> NQ[Negative Reciprocal minus 1 slash N of A]
    G1 -.-> NYQ[Linear Nyquist G of j omega]
    NQ --> INTER[Intersection Check]
    NYQ --> INTER
    INTER --> LC{Limit Cycle Exists}
    LC -- Yes --> A1[Predict Amplitude and Frequency]
    LC -- No --> A2[System is Stable]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The forward path consists of a **nonlinear element** $N(e)$ followed by the **linear plant** $G(s)$.
- For quasi-linear analysis, the nonlinearity is replaced by its **describing function** $N(A)$, a complex gain that depends on input amplitude $A$.
- The classical Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ is compared with the **negative reciprocal locus** $-1/N(A)$ on the same plane.
- An **intersection** of these two loci predicts a **limit cycle** with amplitude and frequency read directly off the intersection point.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Plot — Input-Output Characteristics of Key Static Nonlinearities

The following diagram sketches the static (memoryless) input-output map of the four most common nonlinearities, normalized for visual comparison.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Saturation
        S1["Input e"] --> S2["Linear region slope K for e small"]
        S2 --> S3["Saturated flat output for e large"]
    end

    subgraph Dead_Zone
        D1["Input e"] --> D2["Zero output for e within Delta"]
        D2 --> D3["Linear slope K outside dead zone"]
    end

    subgraph Relay_OnOff
        R1["Input e"] --> R2["Negative output minus M"]
        R2 --> R3["Jump to positive output plus M at threshold"]
    end

    subgraph Ideal_Relay
        I1["Input e"] --> I2["Signum function sign of e"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Backlash and Hysteresis** are *dynamic* nonlinearities — their static input-output plot is a *closed loop* (hysteresis loop) rather than a single curve. They cannot be represented by a single-valued function $y = f(e)$ and require a *differential* description $\dot{y} = g(e, \dot{e}, y)$.

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Describing Function Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Identify Physical Nonlinearity] --> P2[Classify Static or Dynamic]
    P2 --> P3{Static}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Apply Sinusoidal Test Input A sin omega t]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Fourier First Harmonic B1]
    P5 --> P6[Form Describing Function N of A equals B1 over A]
    P6 --> P7[Plot Negative Reciprocal minus 1 over N of A]
    P3 -- No --> P8[Use Phase Plane or State Space]
    P7 --> P9[Overlay with Nyquist of G of j omega]
    P9 --> P10{Intersection}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Limit Cycle Predicted]
    P10 -- No --> P12[Asymptotically Stable or Unstable]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define saturation nonlinearity. With a neat sketch, explain how it differs from an ideal linear element.*

**Model Answer:**
Saturation is a static nonlinearity in which the output is linearly proportional to the input within a certain range $\vert e \vert \le a$, and is clamped to a constant value $\pm M$ outside this range. Mathematically:
$$ y = \begin{cases} K e, & \vert e \vert \le a \\ M \cdot \text{sign}(e), & \vert e \vert > a \end{cases} $$
where $M = Ka$. It differs from a linear element because its slope changes (from $K$ to $0$) once the input exceeds the linear range, violating the homogeneity and superposition principles. Real-world examples include operational amplifiers, DC motors, and hydraulic valves.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a dead zone? How does it affect the steady-state error of a closed-loop control system?*

**Model Answer:**
A dead zone (or deadband) is a static nonlinearity in which the output is zero for input magnitudes within a threshold $\pm \Delta$, and follows a linear law $K(e - \Delta \cdot \text{sign}(e))$ outside this range.
$$ y = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert e \vert \le \Delta \\ K(e - \Delta \cdot \text{sign}(e)), & \vert e \vert > \Delta \end{cases} $$
**Effect on SS error:** Because small error signals produce no corrective action, the controller ignores errors below $\Delta$. The system will therefore settle at any equilibrium where $\vert e_{ss} \vert \le \Delta$, leading to a **finite steady-state error band of width $2\Delta$** rather than a single equilibrium point. In position-control systems, this manifests as a "sticky" feel near the setpoint.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — 14 Marks with Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** *Derive the describing function of an ideal relay nonlinearity. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For a unity feedback system with $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$ and an ideal relay of magnitude $M = 2$ in the forward path, use the describing function method to determine whether a limit cycle exists. If it exists, find its amplitude and frequency.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Derivation of describing function of ideal relay:
- Assumption 1: The nonlinearity is memoryless and odd-symmetric.
- Assumption 2: Input is sinusoidal: $e(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$.
- Assumption 3: The system is low-pass (filters higher harmonics).

For an ideal relay, $y(t) = M \cdot \text{sign}(A\sin\omega t) = M \cdot \text{sign}(\sin\omega t)$, a square wave of amplitude $\pm M$. The fundamental component of a square wave has amplitude $\frac{4M}{\pi}$. Therefore:
$$ N(A) = \frac{4M}{\pi A} $$
The DF is real and positive, depending only on amplitude $A$.

**(b)** Limit-cycle analysis:
The characteristic equation in the presence of a describing function $N(A)$:
$$ 1 + N(A) \cdot G(j\omega) = 0 \implies G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(A)} $$

The negative reciprocal locus for the ideal relay:
$$ -\frac{1}{N(A)} = -\frac{\pi A}{4M} = -\frac{\pi A}{8} $$
This is the **negative real axis**, parametrized by $A \in (0, \infty)$.

The Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+5)}$ crosses the negative real axis at the phase-crossover frequency. Phase condition: $\angle G(j\omega) = -180°$.
$$ -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/5) = -180° $$
$$ \tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(\omega/5) = 90° $$
For complementary angles, this gives $\omega \cdot (\omega/5) = 1 \implies \omega^2 = 5 \implies \omega = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ rad/s}$.

At this frequency:
$$ \vert G(j\sqrt{5}) \vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{30}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{900}} = \frac{10}{30} = \frac{1}{3} $$

Set the negative reciprocal equal to this magnitude:
$$ -\frac{\pi A}{8} = -\frac{1}{3} \implies A = \frac{8}{3\pi} \approx 0.849 $$

**Result:** A limit cycle exists with **amplitude $A \approx 0.85$** and **frequency $\omega \approx 2.24$ rad/s**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** *With a clear input-output sketch, explain the backlash nonlinearity. Why is it called a "dynamic" nonlinearity? Discuss its effect on closed-loop stability.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For a system with saturation nonlinearity having $K = 2$ and linear range $a = 1$, compute the describing function values for input amplitudes $A = 0.5$, $A = 1$, $A = 2$, and $A = 5$. Comment on the trend.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Backlash nonlinearity:
Backlash is a dynamic nonlinearity arising from the gap (typically between gear teeth, lead screw, or any loosely coupled mechanical linkage). When the input reverses direction, the output remains **frozen** at its previous value until the input has traversed the backlash gap $2j$. Only then does the output begin to track the input again.

**Sketch (described):** The input-output trajectory is a **parallelogram-like loop**. As $e$ increases from $0$, $y$ follows a parallel line at distance $j$ below. As $e$ decreases, $y$ follows a parallel line at distance $j$ above.

**Why dynamic:** The output $y(t)$ depends on the *history* of $e(t)$ and the *direction* of motion. For the same input value $e$, the output can take two distinct values depending on whether $e$ is increasing or decreasing. Hence $y$ is a *multivalued function* of $e$ and cannot be expressed as $y = f(e)$; it requires a differential relation involving $\dot{e}$ and $\dot{y}$.

**Effect on stability:** Backlash introduces a phase lag (similar to dead time) that reduces phase margin. In closed loop, it can:
- Cause **hunting oscillations** around the setpoint.
- Generate **limit cycles** in marginally stable plants.
- Be a primary cause of instability in precision position-control systems (e.g., CNC machines, robotic joints).

**(b)** Describing function of saturation:
The formula (derived in Section 3.2):
$$ N(A) = \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) + \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^2}\right], \quad A \ge a $$

For $A < a$, the input lies entirely in the linear region, so $N(A) = K = 2$.

**Case 1:** $A = 0.5 < a = 1$. Entirely in linear region: $N(0.5) = 2$.

**Case 2:** $A = 1 = a$. Boundary case. $\sin^{-1}(1) = \pi/2$, $\sqrt{1-1}=0$.
$$ N(1) = \frac{2 \cdot 2}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} + 0\right] = 2 $$

**Case 3:** $A = 2$. $\sin^{-1}(1/2) = \pi/6$, $\sqrt{1-1/4} = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$.
$$ N(2) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right] = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right] $$
$$ N(2) = \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot 0.9566 \approx 1.218 $$

**Case 4:** $A = 5$. $\sin^{-1}(1/5) \approx 0.2014$ rad, $\sqrt{1-1/25} = \sqrt{24}/5 \approx 0.9798$.
$$ N(5) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[0.2014 + 0.2 \cdot 0.9798\right] = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[0.2014 + 0.1960\right] = \frac{4}{\pi}(0.3974) \approx 0.506 $$

**Comment on trend:** $N(A)$ starts at the linear gain $K=2$ for small inputs, then monotonically *decreases* as $A$ increases, approaching 0 as $A \to \infty$. This **amplitude-dependent gain reduction** is the mathematical reason why saturation reduces effective loop gain for large signals and is a stabilizing influence (it prevents large overshoots), but it *also* increases steady-state error for large commands.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse static with dynamic nonlinearities** in classification questions. Backlash and hysteresis are *dynamic* — they have memory.
> 2. When deriving describing functions, always state the **three core assumptions**: sinusoidal input, memoryless or quasi-memoryless response, and low-pass filtering by the linear plant. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for omitting these.
> 3. For limit-cycle problems, students frequently forget the **phase-crossover frequency calculation**. The limit cycle frequency is determined by the **phase condition** $\angle G(j\omega) = -180°$ *first*, and only then is the amplitude computed from the magnitude condition. Reversing this order is a common 3-mark error.
> 4. The describing function of saturation is **not** simply $K$ clipped at $M/A$. It is a smooth function of $A$ that requires the $\sin^{-1}$ term.
> 5. **Dead-zone and saturation are NOT the same.** Dead zone produces *no output* for small inputs; saturation produces *linear output* that simply *stops growing* for large inputs. Examiners regularly test this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ **Saturation**: Linear gain $K$ for $\vert e \vert \le a$, clamped at $M = Ka$ outside. DF decreases with $A$.
- ✅ **Dead Zone**: Zero output for $\vert e \vert \le \Delta$. Creates a steady-state error band of $2\Delta$.
- ✅ **Backlash**: Dynamic; output freezes during input reversal across a gap $2j$. Causes hunting/limit cycles.
- ✅ **Ideal Relay**: DF = $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$, real and positive, plotted on negative real axis.
- ✅ **Relay with Dead Zone**: DF = $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1-(a/A)^2}$, defined only for $A \ge a$.
- ✅ **Coulomb Friction**: DF = $\dfrac{4F_c}{\pi A}$, in quadrature with the input (phase shift of $-90°$).
- ✅ **Hysteresis**: Closed loop in input-output plane; output is multivalued.
- ✅ **Describing Function** assumes: (i) sinusoidal input, (ii) low-pass linear plant filtering higher harmonics, (iii) first-harmonic approximation.
- ✅ **Limit cycle exists** when $G(j\omega)$ plot intersects the $-\dfrac{1}{N(A)}$ locus. Frequency from phase condition, amplitude from magnitude condition.
- ✅ **Stability of limit cycle**: If $G(j\omega)$ encircles the intersection point as $A$ increases, the limit cycle is **stable**; otherwise it is **unstable**.
- ✅ For **static** nonlinearities, the describing function is real (no phase shift).
- ✅ For **dynamic** nonlinearities (e.g., Coulomb friction), the describing function has both real and imaginary parts.
- ✅ Real engineering applications: anti-windup (saturation), dither injection (dead zone), preloading (backlash), bang-bang control (relay).
- ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme emphasises **derivation steps + interpretation**, not just final formula. Always show the Fourier integral evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadzone or Threshold
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deadzone or Threshold

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Deadzone (or Threshold) nonlinearity** is a memoryless, odd-symmetric, single-valued static nonlinearity in which the output remains **exactly zero** for input signals whose magnitude lies within a defined threshold band, while for inputs outside this band the output varies **linearly** with the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**  
> A deadzone non-linearity is characterized by a region of insensitivity around the origin (the *dead band* of half-width $a$) where the output is zero, beyond which the output is a linear function of the input with slope $k$. The element is also called a **threshold** element.

The input–output relationship of an ideal symmetric deadzone is given by:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} k\bigl[x(t) - a\bigr], & x(t) > +a \\[4pt] 0, & \vert x(t) \vert \leq a \\[4pt] k\bigl[x(t) + a\bigr], & x(t) < -a \end{cases}$$

where $a > 0$ is the half-width of the dead band and $k$ is the slope (gain) in the active region.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Steering Wheel of a Heavy Truck

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-world intuition for B.Tech students:**  
> Imagine you are driving a heavy truck. When you turn the steering wheel by a *small* angle, the wheels do not respond at all — the system "ignores" tiny commands. Only after you rotate the wheel beyond a *threshold angle* do the wheels begin to turn. This small angular insensitivity is the mechanical deadzone. The same effect appears in:
> - Hydraulic servo valves (overlap region of spool)
> - DC motors (static friction / stiction zone)
> - Pneumatic actuators (valve lap)
> - Gear trains with imperfect meshing
> - Electronic amplifiers below their offset voltage

Below the threshold, the system **does not react**; above it, the system **responds proportionally**. This is the essence of a deadzone.

## 1.3 Important Parameters (Bold Notation for KTU)

- **Dead-zone half-width:** $a$ (units of input signal)
- **Active-region slope / gain:** $k$ (output units per input unit)
- **Break-point input:** $+a$ and $-a$
- **Output at break-point:** $0$
- **Symmetry:** Odd, single-valued, memoryless

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU Examiner's hint:** Always state whether the deadzone is symmetric ($+a$, $-a$) or one-sided when answering exam questions. The default syllabus form is **symmetric** unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Deadzone — Step-by-Step

The deadzone processes an input $x(t)$ through **three distinct regions** of operation:

1. **Region I — Lower Deadband ($x < -a$):**  
   The input is *more negative* than the lower threshold. The output follows a linear law $y = k(x + a)$, with a horizontal shift of $a$ along the negative x-axis.

2. **Region II — Active Deadband ($-a \leq x \leq a$):**  
   The input magnitude is *within* the threshold. The output is *forced to zero*. The system is effectively **disconnected** — no signal passes through.

3. **Region III — Upper Active Region ($x > +a$):**  
   The input exceeds the upper threshold. The output follows the linear law $y = k(x - a)$, shifted by $a$ along the positive x-axis.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why the deadzone matters in control engineering:**  
> - It introduces a **static position error** in tracking systems.  
> - It produces a **threshold limit cycle** in closed-loop systems with integrator-type linear plants.  
> - It **attenuates small signals** — the system is "blind" to commands smaller than $a$.  
> - In some configurations it is *intentionally inserted* to suppress chattering (e.g., sliding-mode control with boundary layer).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following table consolidates every equation you will need for KTU 2024 end-semester questions on deadzone. Memorize the describing function — it is asked almost every year.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Validity / Units |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Input–output relation (symmetric) | $y = k(x - a\,\text{sgn}\,x)$ for $\vert x \vert > a$; else $y = 0$ | Output in same units as input scaled by $k$ |
| 2 | Dead-band half-width | $a$ | Same units as input |
| 3 | Active-region slope | $k$ | Dimensionless gain or V/V, A/A, etc. |
| 4 | Describing Function $N(X)$ for $X \leq a$ | $N(X) = 0$ | Real, dimensionless |
| 5 | Describing Function $N(X)$ for $X > a$ | $N(X) = k - \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\arcsin\!\dfrac{a}{X} + \dfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1-\dfrac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right]$ | Real, dimensionless |
| 6 | Normalized DF $n(X/a)$ | $n = 1 - \dfrac{2}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{a}{X} + \dfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1-\dfrac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right]$ | Used to compare with $-1/G(j\omega)$ |
| 7 | Limit cycle condition | $1 + N(X)\,G(j\omega) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; G(j\omega) = -1/N(X)$ | Intersection of $-1/N(X)$ with $G(j\omega)$ |
| 8 | Break-frequency parameter | $X \to a^{+}$: $N(X) \to 0^{+}$; $X \to \infty$: $N(X) \to k$ | DF lies on the negative real axis of $G$-plane |
| 9 | Equivalent gain of the nonlinearity | $k_{eq}(X) = N(X)$ (since DF is purely real) | Used in describing-function analysis |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Pipe-symbol warning in LaTeX:** In the formula sheet above, I have used `\vert` and `\dfrac` instead of `|` and inline `a/X`, so the markdown table renders without breaking. In your answer scripts, prefer `\vert x(t) \vert` over `|x(t)|` whenever you sit inside a markdown table.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The deadzone is one of the most encountered non-linearities in **mechatronics, process control, and aerospace systems**:

- **Hydraulic servo valves:** The spool overlap creates a dead zone of typically $\pm 2\%$ to $\pm 5\%$ of rated stroke.  
- **DC servomotors with static friction:** Below the breakaway torque, the rotor does not move — a dead zone exists in the voltage–speed curve.  
- **Satellite attitude control thrusters:** A minimum pulse bit must accumulate before thrust is generated — modelled as a deadzone.  
- **Robotic joint drives:** Gear backlash combined with stiction creates a compound deadzone–friction element.  
- **Sliding-mode controllers:** An *intentional* deadzone (called the *boundary layer*) is added to eliminate chattering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation of the Describing Function of Deadzone

## 3.1 Problem Setup

Apply a sinusoidal input of amplitude $X$ to the deadzone:

$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega t), \qquad X > a$$

The resulting output $y(t)$ is **periodic with the same fundamental period** $T = 2\pi/\omega$ as the input, but it is *not sinusoidal*. The output waveform is shown qualitatively in the diagram in Section 4.

Because the deadzone is an **odd function**, the output $y(t)$ is also odd about the time origin, so all cosine Fourier coefficients vanish. The describing function is therefore **purely real**:

$$N(X) = \frac{1}{\pi X}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t)\,\sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$$

## 3.2 Finding the Switching Angles

The output $y(t)$ is zero whenever the input $\vert X\sin(\omega t)\vert \leq a$, i.e. when

$$\vert \sin(\omega t) \vert \leq \frac{a}{X}$$

The **switching angles** $\psi_{1}$ and $\pi - \psi_{1}$ are defined by

$$\sin(\psi_{1}) = \frac{a}{X} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \psi_{1} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{X}\right)$$

For $0 \leq \omega t < \psi_{1}$: the input magnitude is below threshold $\Rightarrow y(t) = 0$.

For $\psi_{1} \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \psi_{1}$: the input exceeds the upper threshold $\Rightarrow y(t) = k\bigl[X\sin(\omega t) - a\bigr]$.

For $\pi - \psi_{1} \leq \omega t \leq \pi + \psi_{1}$: input back inside the deadband $\Rightarrow y(t) = 0$.

For $\pi + \psi_{1} \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi - \psi_{1}$: input below $-a$ $\Rightarrow y(t) = k\bigl[X\sin(\omega t) + a\bigr]$.

For $2\pi - \psi_{1} \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$: $y(t) = 0$.

## 3.3 Exploiting Odd Symmetry

By the half-wave odd symmetry of $y(t)$:

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\theta)\sin(\theta)\,d\theta = 2\int_{0}^{\pi} y(\theta)\sin(\theta)\,d\theta$$

Therefore the describing function reduces to:

$$N(X) = \frac{2}{\pi X}\int_{0}^{\pi} y(\theta)\sin(\theta)\,d\theta$$

Using the piecewise definition of $y(\theta)$:

$$N(X) = \frac{2}{\pi X}\int_{\psi_{1}}^{\pi - \psi_{1}} k\bigl[X\sin\theta - a\bigr]\sin\theta\,d\theta$$

## 3.4 Splitting the Integral

Expand the integrand into two computable integrals:

$$N(X) = \frac{2k}{\pi X}\underbrace{\int_{\psi_{1}}^{\pi - \psi_{1}} X\sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta}_{I_{1}} \;-\; \frac{2k}{\pi X}\underbrace{\int_{\psi_{1}}^{\pi - \psi_{1}} a\sin\theta\,d\theta}_{I_{2}}$$

### 3.4.1 Evaluation of $I_{1}$

Use the standard identity $\sin^{2}\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$I_{1} = X\int_{\psi_{1}}^{\pi - \psi_{1}}\frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}\,d\theta$$

$$I_{1} = \frac{X}{2}\Bigl[\theta - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta\Bigr]_{\psi_{1}}^{\pi - \psi_{1}}$$

Substitute the limits:

$$I_{1} = \frac{X}{2}\!\left[(\pi - \psi_{1}) - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin\bigl(2\pi - 2\psi_{1}\bigr) - \psi_{1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\psi_{1})\right]$$

Using $\sin(2\pi - 2\psi_{1}) = -\sin(2\psi_{1})$:

$$I_{1} = \frac{X}{2}\!\left[\pi - 2\psi_{1} + \sin(2\psi_{1})\right]$$

### 3.4.2 Evaluation of $I_{2}$

$$I_{2} = a\int_{\psi_{1}}^{\pi - \psi_{1}}\sin\theta\,d\theta = a\bigl[-\cos\theta\bigr]_{\psi_{1}}^{\pi - \psi_{1}}$$

$$I_{2} = a\bigl[-\cos(\pi - \psi_{1}) + \cos\psi_{1}\bigr] = a\bigl[\cos\psi_{1} + \cos\psi_{1}\bigr] = 2a\cos\psi_{1}$$

## 3.5 Substituting Back into $N(X)$

$$N(X) = \frac{2k}{\pi X}\cdot\frac{X}{2}\bigl[\pi - 2\psi_{1} + \sin 2\psi_{1}\bigr] - \frac{2k}{\pi X}\cdot 2a\cos\psi_{1}$$

$$N(X) = \frac{k}{\pi}\bigl[\pi - 2\psi_{1} + \sin 2\psi_{1}\bigr] - \frac{4ka\cos\psi_{1}}{\pi X}$$

Now substitute $\psi_{1} = \sin^{-1}(a/X)$ and use the identities:

$$\cos\psi_{1} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}$$

$$\sin 2\psi_{1} = 2\sin\psi_{1}\cos\psi_{1} = 2\,\frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}$$

Insert these into the second term of $N(X)$:

$$\frac{4ka\cos\psi_{1}}{\pi X} = \frac{4k}{\pi}\cdot\frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}$$

And into the first term:

$$\frac{k}{\pi}\bigl[\pi - 2\sin^{-1}(a/X)\bigr] + \frac{k}{\pi}\cdot 2\,\frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}$$

Combine the two terms containing $\dfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}$:

$$\frac{2k}{\pi}\cdot\frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}} - \frac{4k}{\pi}\cdot\frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}} = -\frac{2k}{\pi}\cdot\frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}$$

## 3.6 Final Result — The Describing Function of Deadzone

$$\boxed{\,N(X) = k - \frac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{a}{X} + \frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right],\qquad X > a\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,N(X) = 0,\qquad X \leq a\,}$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Verification of boundary behaviour:**  
> 1. As $X \to a^{+}$, $\sin^{-1}(a/X) \to \pi/2$ and $\sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}} \to 0$, so $N(X) \to k - \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{2} = k - k = 0$. ✓ (Continuous from $N=0$ at $X=a$.)  
> 2. As $X \to \infty$, both $\sin^{-1}(a/X) \to 0$ and $\sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}} \to 1$, so $N(X) \to k - 0 = k$. ✓ (The deadzone disappears for very large signals.)  
> 3. $N(X)$ is real and lies on the **negative real axis** of the $G$-plane when plotted as $-1/N(X)$.

## 3.7 Limiting-Cycle Prediction Procedure

Once $N(X)$ is known, the limit cycle (if it exists) is found by solving the **harmonic balance equation** on the negative real axis of the Nyquist plot:

$$G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(X)}$$

The intersection of the **Nyquist locus** $G(j\omega)$ with the **negative real axis** gives the candidate point $(\omega_{c}, X_{c})$. Substitute $\omega_{c}$ back into $G(j\omega)$ to extract $X_{c}$ from:

$$X_{c}\quad\text{such that}\quad N(X_{c}) = -\frac{1}{G(j\omega_{c})}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Decision Flow of the Deadzone Element

The following Mermaid flowchart describes how the deadzone processes an input $x(t)$ and produces $y(t)$ in real time. The block-level architecture style is chosen to be renderer-safe.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA([Input signal x of t]) --> compA{Is x of t greater than plus a}
    inA --> compB{Is x of t less than minus a}
    inA --> compC{Abs x of t less than or equal to a}
    compA -- Yes --> mulA[Compute y equals k times x minus a]
    compA -- No --> compB
    compB -- Yes --> mulB[Compute y equals k times x plus a]
    compB -- No --> compC
    compC -- Yes --> zero[Output y equals 0 Deadband]
    compC -- No --> fallback[Fallback unreachable by symmetry]
    mulA --> outA([Output y of t linear positive branch])
    mulB --> outB([Output y of t linear negative branch])
    zero --> outC([Output y of t zero within dead zone])
    fallback --> outC
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical Location of Deadzone in a Closed-Loop Control System

The deadzone non-linearity $N(\cdot)$ is most often placed **inside the forward path**, immediately following the controller $C(s)$ and preceding the plant $G(s)$. The unity-feedback configuration is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r([Reference r of t]) --> sum((Sum node))
    sum --> C[Controller C of s]
    C --> N{Deadzone non linearity N}
    N --> G[Linear Plant G of s]
    G --> y([Output y of t])
    y --> fb[Feedback path H of s equals 1]
    fb --> sum
    classDef nonlinearity fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    class N nonlinearity
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering interpretation:** The block $N$ represents the *non-ideal* actuator or valve. For $X \leq a$ the signal reaching $G(s)$ is zero, so the closed-loop becomes *open*. This is precisely why a deadzone can stall a position servo when the error is small.

## 4.3 Plot of $N(X)$ versus $X/a$ — Describing Function Characteristic

A qualitative plot of the normalized describing function $n(X/a) = N(X)/k$ versus the normalized input $X/a$ is shown below. Notice that the curve starts at $0$ when $X = a$ and asymptotically approaches $1$ as $X \to \infty$.

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Normalized Describing Function of Deadzone: n of X over a"
    x-axis "Normalized input X over a" [1, 1.5, 2, 3, 5, 10]
    y-axis "Normalized DF n equals N over k" 0 --> 1
    line [0.0, 0.42, 0.59, 0.78, 0.91, 0.98]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The corresponding **negative reciprocal** $-1/N(X)$ is a horizontal line on the **negative real axis** of the $G(j\omega)$-plane, varying from $-\infty$ (at $X = a$) to $-1/k$ (as $X \to \infty$). A limit cycle exists only if the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ crosses this real-axis segment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define deadzone non-linearity. Sketch its input–output characteristic and name one physical system that exhibits it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A deadzone is a memoryless, odd-symmetric non-linearity whose output is **zero** when the input magnitude lies in the range $[-a, +a]$ (the dead band), and is **linearly proportional** to the input outside this range. The characteristic consists of a horizontal segment on the x-axis from $-a$ to $+a$, and two straight lines of slope $k$ on either side. A hydraulic servo valve with spool overlap is a typical physical example (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sketch description, 1 mark for example).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Why is the describing function of a symmetric deadzone a purely real quantity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The deadzone characteristic $y = N(x)$ is an **odd function** of $x$ (i.e., $N(-x) = -N(x)$). When the input is sinusoidal $x(t) = X\sin\omega t$, the resulting periodic output $y(t)$ is also an **odd function of time**, so all cosine Fourier coefficients $A_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y\sin(n\theta)\,d\theta$ vanish for the even terms and the imaginary part of the fundamental $B_1$ also vanishes. The fundamental sine coefficient divided by $X$ gives the describing function, which is therefore purely real (2 marks for odd-symmetry argument, 1 mark for conclusion).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the describing function $N(X)$ of a symmetric deadzone non-linearity with half-width $a$ and slope $k$. Show that $N(X) \to 0$ as $X \to a^{+}$ and $N(X) \to k$ as $X \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity-feedback control system has a deadzone element of half-width $a = 0.4$ and slope $k = 1.5$ in the forward path. The linear plant is

$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s\,(s+2)\,(s+5)}.$$

Using describing function analysis, determine whether a limit cycle exists. If it does, compute its amplitude $X_{c}$ and frequency $\omega_{c}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of $N(X)$ (7 marks)

Apply sinusoidal input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$, $X > a$ (1 mark).

Output is zero for $\vert \sin(\omega t) \vert \leq a/X$ and equals $k(X\sin\omega t \mp a)$ outside this region. Switching angles satisfy $\psi_{1} = \sin^{-1}(a/X)$ (1 mark).

By odd symmetry, the describing function reduces to:

$$N(X) = \frac{2}{\pi X}\int_{\psi_{1}}^{\pi - \psi_{1}} k\bigl[X\sin\theta - a\bigr]\sin\theta\,d\theta$$

Split into $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ as derived in Section 3 (2 marks):

$$I_{1} = \frac{X}{2}\bigl[\pi - 2\psi_{1} + \sin 2\psi_{1}\bigr], \qquad I_{2} = 2a\cos\psi_{1}$$

Substitute and simplify using $\cos\psi_{1} = \sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}}$ and $\sin 2\psi_{1} = 2(a/X)\sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}}$ (1 mark):

$$N(X) = k - \frac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{a}{X} + \frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right]$$

**Boundary limits (2 marks):**

- As $X \to a^{+}$: $\sin^{-1}(a/X) \to \pi/2$ and $\sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}} \to 0$, hence $N(X) \to k - (2k/\pi)(\pi/2) = 0$. ✓  
- As $X \to \infty$: $\sin^{-1}(a/X) \to 0$ and $\sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}} \to 1$, hence $N(X) \to k - 0 = k$. ✓

#### Part (b) — Limit Cycle Computation (7 marks)

The harmonic balance condition for a limit cycle is:

$$1 + N(X)\,G(j\omega) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(X)}$$

Since $N(X)$ is real and positive (for $X > a$), the right-hand side is a **negative real number**. Therefore the limit cycle must occur at the frequency $\omega_{c}$ at which $G(j\omega_{c})$ is real and negative (i.e., where the Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis).

Compute $G(j\omega)$ for the given plant:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega\,(j\omega + 2)\,(j\omega + 5)}$$

Expand the denominator:

$$j\omega\,(j\omega + 2)\,(j\omega + 5) = j\omega\bigl[(j\omega)^{2} + 7j\omega + 10\bigr] = j\omega\bigl[-\omega^{2} + 10 + 7j\omega\bigr]$$

$$= j\omega(10 - \omega^{2}) + j^{2}\,7\omega^{2} = -7\omega^{2} + j\omega(10 - \omega^{2})$$

Hence:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10\bigl[-7\omega^{2} - j\omega(10 - \omega^{2})\bigr]}{[7\omega^{2}]^{2} + [\omega(10 - \omega^{2})]^{2}} = \frac{10\bigl[-7\omega^{2} - j\omega(10 - \omega^{2})\bigr]}{49\omega^{4} + \omega^{2}(10 - \omega^{2})^{2}}$$

The imaginary part of $G(j\omega)$ is zero when:

$$\omega(10 - \omega^{2}) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega = 0 \text{ or } \omega = \sqrt{10}\,\text{rad/s}$$

(The $\omega = 0$ solution is trivial — plant has a pole at the origin.) Therefore $\omega_{c} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ rad/s (1 mark).

At $\omega_{c} = \sqrt{10}$, evaluate the real part of $G(j\omega_{c})$:

$$G(j\omega_{c}) = \frac{10\cdot(-7\cdot 10)}{49\cdot 100 + 10\cdot 0} = \frac{-700}{4900} = -\frac{1}{7}$$

(Substitute $\omega^{2} = 10$ into numerator $-7\omega^{2}$ and denominator $49\omega^{4} + \omega^{2}(10 - \omega^{2})^{2} = 49\cdot 100 + 10\cdot 0 = 4900$.) (1 mark)

Apply the harmonic balance equation:

$$-\frac{1}{N(X_{c})} = G(j\omega_{c}) = -\frac{1}{7} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N(X_{c}) = 7$$

But the maximum value of $N(X)$ is $k = 1.5$ (as $X \to \infty$). Since $7 > 1.5$, the required $N(X_{c}) = 7$ is **physically unattainable** (2 marks).

**Conclusion:** The $-1/N(X)$ locus lies on the real axis from $-1/1.5 = -0.667$ to $-\infty$, which **does not include the point $-1/7 \approx -0.143$**. The Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis at $-1/7$, which lies *outside* the reachable segment. **Hence no limit cycle exists in this system** (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**  
> - Many students forget to check that the **computed** $N(X_{c})$ does not exceed the saturation value $k$. Always verify the physical realizability of $X_{c}$.  
> - Some students compute $G(j\omega)$ incorrectly because they do not rationalise the denominator — losing 2 marks.  
> - The case $G(j\omega) = -1/k$ being *less negative* than the Nyquist cross-over does NOT automatically mean no limit cycle; the exact intersection must be checked.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the deadzone non-linearity with the help of a clear input–output sketch. List **three** physical systems in which a deadzone is observed and explain its effect on closed-loop performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a deadzone non-linearity with slope $k = 1.5$ and half-width $a = 0.3$, compute the describing function $N(X)$ at input amplitudes $X = 0.5$, $X = 1.0$, and $X = 2.0$. Comment on the trend. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Qualitative Explanation (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** A deadzone is a non-linearity in which the output is **identically zero** for input magnitudes within a threshold band $[-a, +a]$. Outside this band, the output varies linearly with the input. The characteristic is **odd-symmetric**, consisting of a horizontal segment along the x-axis from $-a$ to $+a$ and two oblique lines of slope $k$ outside this range.

**Three physical systems (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Hydraulic servo valve with spool overlap:** The central overlap of the spool causes a region of valve opening where no fluid flows; the piston does not move for small input signals.  
2. **DC servomotor with static friction (stiction):** The rotor remains stationary until the input voltage exceeds a threshold corresponding to the breakaway torque.  
3. **Pneumatic actuator with valve lap:** Similar to the hydraulic valve, a small dead zone exists in the pressure-vs-control-signal curve.

**Effect on closed-loop performance (2 marks):**  
- *Static position error:* the steady-state error does not go to zero even with an integrator, because the controller output falls in the dead band.  
- *Threshold limit cycle:* certain plants with integrators exhibit a self-sustained oscillation whose amplitude is determined by the deadzone width.  
- *Loss of small-signal authority:* commands smaller than $a$ are *ignored*, leading to poor tracking of small reference signals.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation of $N(X)$ (7 marks)

The describing function is:

$$N(X) = k - \frac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{a}{X} + \frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right],\qquad X > a$$

With $k = 1.5$ and $a = 0.3$:

##### Case 1: $X = 0.5$ (note $X > a = 0.3$ ✓) (2 marks)

$$\frac{a}{X} = \frac{0.3}{0.5} = 0.6, \qquad \sqrt{1 - 0.36} = \sqrt{0.64} = 0.8$$

$$\sin^{-1}(0.6) = 0.6435 \text{ rad}$$

$$N(0.5) = 1.5 - \frac{2 \times 1.5}{\pi}\bigl[0.6435 + 0.6 \times 0.8\bigr] = 1.5 - \frac{3}{\pi}(0.6435 + 0.48)$$

$$= 1.5 - \frac{3}{\pi}(1.1235) = 1.5 - \frac{3.3705}{3.1416} = 1.5 - 1.0729 = 0.4271$$

##### Case 2: $X = 1.0$ (2 marks)

$$\frac{a}{X} = 0.3, \qquad \sqrt{1 - 0.09} = \sqrt{0.91} = 0.9539$$

$$\sin^{-1}(0.3) = 0.3047 \text{ rad}$$

$$N(1.0) = 1.5 - \frac{3}{\pi}\bigl[0.3047 + 0.3 \times 0.9539\bigr] = 1.5 - \frac{3}{\pi}(0.3047 + 0.2862)$$

$$= 1.5 - \frac{3}{\pi}(0.5909) = 1.5 - \frac{1.7727}{3.1416} = 1.5 - 0.5643 = 0.9357$$

##### Case 3: $X = 2.0$ (2 marks)

$$\frac{a}{X} = 0.15, \qquad \sqrt{1 - 0.0225} = \sqrt{0.9775} = 0.9887$$

$$\sin^{-1}(0.15) = 0.1506 \text{ rad}$$

$$N(2.0) = 1.5 - \frac{3}{\pi}\bigl[0.1506 + 0.15 \times 0.9887\bigr] = 1.5 - \frac{3}{\pi}(0.1506 + 0.1483)$$

$$= 1.5 - \frac{3}{\pi}(0.2989) = 1.5 - \frac{0.8967}{3.1416} = 1.5 - 0.2854 = 1.2146$$

**Summary table (1 mark):**

| $X$ | $N(X)$ | Ratio $N(X)/k$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 0.4271 | 0.285 |
| 1.0 | 0.9357 | 0.624 |
| 2.0 | 1.2146 | 0.810 |

**Trend (bonus 1 mark):** As $X$ increases well beyond $a$, the effect of the dead band diminishes and $N(X) \to k = 1.5$. Equivalently, the *equivalent gain* of the deadzone element approaches its high-signal slope $k$ for large amplitudes, which physically corresponds to the dead band becoming a negligible fraction of the input range.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) pitfalls:**  
> - Students often forget the *order* of the formula: $N(X) = k - \frac{2k}{\pi}(\cdots)$, not $N(X) = k + \frac{2k}{\pi}(\cdots)$. A sign error here loses 2 marks instantly.  
> - $\sin^{-1}(a/X)$ must be in **radians**, not degrees.  
> - Always state explicitly that the formula is valid only for $X > a$. For $X \leq a$, $N(X) = 0$.  
> - Use a calculator and show the intermediate numerical substitutions; examiners reward working.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Deadzone / Threshold**

- **Definition:** Output is zero for $\vert x \vert \leq a$; linear with slope $k$ for $\vert x \vert > a$. The element is *memoryless*, *odd*, and *single-valued*.  
- **Switching angle:** $\psi_{1} = \sin^{-1}(a/X)$ when input is $X\sin\omega t$.  
- **Describing function (the most-asked formula):** $N(X) = k - \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{a}{X} + \dfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right]$, valid for $X > a$.  
- **Boundary behaviour:** $N(X) = 0$ at $X = a$ and $N(X) \to k$ as $X \to \infty$.  
- **Real-valued DF:** Because the deadzone is odd-symmetric, the imaginary part of $N(X)$ is zero — so the $-1/N(X)$ locus lies on the **negative real axis** of the $G(j\omega)$ plane.  
- **Limit cycle test:** Plot the Nyquist curve $G(j\omega)$. If it crosses the real axis at a point that lies *between* $-1/k$ and $-\infty$ (i.e. $-1/N(X)$ for some $X > a$), then a limit cycle exists.  
- **Engineering examples:** Hydraulic valve spool overlap, DC motor stiction, gear-train threshold, thruster minimum pulse bit.  
- **Common sign error:** $N(X)$ is $k$ **minus** the bracketed term — never plus.  
- **Units check:** $k$ has units of (output)/(input); $a$ has units of input; $N(X)$ is dimensionless only if $k$ is dimensionless.  
- **Stability implication:** The effective loop gain $k_{eq} = N(X) \cdot G(0)$ is reduced for small amplitudes — hence deadzone *destabilises* marginally-stable loops and *can suppress* high-frequency oscillations in well-damped loops.  
- **Quick mental picture:** "Below the threshold, the plant hears silence; above the threshold, it hears the controller clearly."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Relay** is a static, memoryless or memory-based non-linear element whose output signal switches abruptly between a finite set of discrete levels when its input crosses certain threshold (or break-point) values. In the formal language of the KTU Non-Linear Control syllabus, a relay is treated as a **piecewise-constant static non-linearity** belonging to the family of *single-valued* or *multi-valued* non-linearities, and forms the structural heart of *On-Off*, *Bang-Bang*, and *Sliding-Mode* control systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A relay is a non-linear element whose input–output characteristic is a step function (memoryless relay) or a rectangular hysteresis loop (relay with dead-zone/hysteresis). The output $y(t)$ takes values in $\{-M, 0, +M\}$ for a 3-position relay, or $\{-M, +M\}$ for a 2-position (ideal) relay.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine the **thermostat** in your home. You set a desired temperature, say $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. When the room temperature falls *below* the threshold, the thermostat **clicks ON** and the heater runs at full blast ($+M$). When the temperature rises *above* the threshold, it **clicks OFF** ($0$ or $-M$). The heater never runs at "half-power" — it is either fully ON or fully OFF. That binary, threshold-driven behaviour is exactly what a **relay** does mathematically. The "click" (switch) is the non-linearity.

### 1.3 Why Relays Matter in Non-Linear Control

Relays are the **simplest yet most powerful** non-linear elements because:

| Property | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- |
| **High gain saturation** | Acts like a very-high-gain amplifier in the active region |
| **Cheap to manufacture** | Used in industrial on-off valves, solenoid switches, contactors |
| **Energy efficient** | Always operates at maximum or zero — no linear amplification losses |
| **Foundation of Sliding Mode Control** | The relay is the *core switching element* of SMC |
| **Canonical non-linearity** | Used as a benchmark to study limit cycles via *Describing Function* method |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** When you see the word "relay" in a non-linear control question, immediately think of *two things*: (1) The input-output curve with thresholds $\pm\Delta$ or $\pm\varepsilon$, and (2) The **Describing Function** $N(A)$, which is what makes the relay analytically tractable using linear techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Classification of Relays

Relays are classified based on **two structural features**:

1. **Number of output levels** → 2-position, 3-position, or multi-position.
2. **Presence of memory** → *Single-valued* (memoryless) vs. *Multi-valued* (hysteretic).

### 2.2 The Five Canonical Types of Relays

#### **Type 1 — Ideal Two-Position Relay (On-Off / Signum Relay)**

The output is a constant magnitude $M$, sign-matched to the input.

$$
y(x) = M \cdot \text{sgn}(x) = \begin{cases} +M, & x > 0 \\ -M, & x < 0 \end{cases}
$$

- **Single-valued** (memoryless)
- **No dead zone, no hysteresis**
- Equivalent to a *signum function* scaled by $M$

#### **Type 2 — Three-Position (Ideal) Relay with Dead Zone**

Output is zero in the dead-zone band, and saturates at $\pm M$ outside.

$$
y(x) = \begin{cases} +M, & x > \Delta \\ 0, & \vert x \vert \leq \Delta \\ -M, & x < -\Delta \end{cases}
$$

- **Single-valued** (memoryless)
- Avoids high-frequency *chattering* near zero by introducing a "quiet zone" of width $2\Delta$

#### **Type 3 — Two-Position Relay with Hysteresis (Schmitt Trigger Type)**

The switching thresholds depend on the **sign of the derivative of input** $\dot x$. This makes it *multi-valued* (memory).

$$
y(x) = \begin{cases} +M, & \text{if } x > +\varepsilon \text{ or } (x \geq -\varepsilon \text{ and } y_{\text{prev}} = +M) \\ -M, & \text{if } x < -\varepsilon \text{ or } (x \leq +\varepsilon \text{ and } y_{\text{prev}} = -M) \end{cases}
$$

- **Multi-valued** (hysteretic)
- Bandwidth of hysteresis $= 2\varepsilon$
- Used wherever *chattering suppression* is needed (Schmitt triggers, bang-bang with hysteresis)

#### **Type 4 — Three-Position Relay with Hysteresis + Dead Zone**

Combines both features: a central dead zone $2\Delta$ *and* hysteresis $\pm\varepsilon$.

$$
y(x) = \begin{cases} +M, & x > +\Delta \text{ and switching from } 0 \text{ state} \\ 0, & \vert x \vert < \Delta \\ -M, & x < -\Delta \text{ and switching from } 0 \text{ state} \end{cases}
$$

#### **Type 5 — Multi-Position (n-Level) Relay**

Output takes one of $n$ discrete values based on multiple thresholds $\Delta_1 < \Delta_2 < \dots < \Delta_{n-1}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Relay Type** | **Output Equation** | **Describing Function $N(A)$** | **Validity** | **Memory** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ideal 2-position | $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x)$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ | $A > 0$ | None |
| 3-position with dead zone | $y = 0$ if $\vert x\vert \le \Delta$, else $\pm M$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}}$ | $A > \Delta$ | None |
| 2-position with hysteresis | $y$ depends on $\dot x$, hysteresis $\pm\varepsilon$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\varepsilon}{A}\right)^{2}} \;-\; j\dfrac{4M\varepsilon}{\pi A^{2}}$ | $A > \varepsilon$ | Yes |
| 3-position + dead zone + hysteresis | Combined | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta + \varepsilon}{A}\right)^{2}} \;-\; j\dfrac{4M\varepsilon(\Delta + \varepsilon)}{\pi A^{2}\sqrt{1-\dots}}$ | $A > \Delta + \varepsilon$ | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** The Describing Function is a complex quantity for relays with hysteresis (imaginary part is *negative*). Forgetting the $-j$ term costs full marks in KTU ESE.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic Refrigerators / ACs:** Thermostat = ideal 3-position relay with dead zone.
- **Industrial Solenoid Valves:** Hydraulic/Pneumatic control valves = 2-position relay with hysteresis.
- **Automotive Fuel Injectors:** Multi-position relay based on pedal position thresholds.
- **Robotics Actuators:** DC motors driven by PWM through relay switches for bang-bang control.
- **Power Electronics:** Triac/SCR switching in AC voltage regulators = ideal relay at line frequency.
- **Aerospace:** Sliding-mode flight controllers use high-frequency relays as the switching element.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Describing Function of the Ideal 2-Position Relay

**Goal:** Find the first-harmonic describing function $N(A)$ for $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x(t))$, where $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$.

#### **Step 1 — Express the Output**

Since the relay flips sign at every zero crossing of $x(t)$, the output is a square wave of amplitude $M$.

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & 0 < \omega t < \pi \\ -M, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Fourier Series Expansion of the Output Square Wave**

A square wave (odd-symmetric, amplitude $\pm M$) has the well-known Fourier series:

$$
y(t) = \frac{4M}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t)
$$

#### **Step 3 — Identify the Fundamental Harmonic**

The describing function uses *only the first harmonic* ($n=1$):

$$
y_1(t) = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sin(\omega t)
$$

#### **Step 4 — Form the Ratio $N(A) = Y_1 / X_1$**

Since the input fundamental is $x_1(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, the amplitude ratio is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(A) &= \frac{Y_1}{X_1} = \frac{4M/\pi}{A} \\[6pt]
      &= \frac{4M}{\pi A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The describing function of an ideal relay is **purely real**, depends only on input amplitude $A$ (not frequency), and decreases as $\dfrac{1}{A}$ — i.e., the relay's effective gain $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ shrinks as the input gets larger.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Describing Function of a 3-Position Relay with Dead Zone

**Goal:** Find $N(A)$ for $y = 0$ when $\vert x\vert \le \Delta$ and $y = \pm M$ when $\vert x\vert > \Delta$.

#### **Step 1 — Determine the Switching Angle $\alpha$**

Let $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$. The relay switches ON when $x = \Delta$:

$$
A\sin\alpha = \Delta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)
$$

This requires $A > \Delta$ for the relay to "fire".

#### **Step 2 — Write the Output Waveform**

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t \le \alpha \\ +M, & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi - \alpha \\ 0, & \pi - \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha \\ -M, & \pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi - \alpha \\ 0, & 2\pi - \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute the Fundamental Sine Coefficient $B_1$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t)\,\sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{2}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} M\,\sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) \quad\text{(exploiting odd symmetry, only upper half contributes)} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{2M}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{2M}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\pi - \alpha) + \cos(\alpha)\Big] \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{2M}{\pi}\Big[\cos(\alpha) + \cos(\alpha)\Big] \quad\text{(since }\cos(\pi - \alpha) = -\cos\alpha\text{)} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{4M}{\pi}\cos(\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Substitute $\alpha$**

$$
\cos(\alpha) = \sqrt{1 - \sin^{2}\alpha} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}}\;}
$$

This is valid for $A > \Delta$. Note that when $\Delta \to 0$, this correctly reduces to the ideal relay result $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Describing Function of a 2-Position Relay with Hysteresis

**Goal:** Find $N(A)$ for $y = +M$ when $x > -\varepsilon$ (during increasing branch) and $y = -M$ when $x < +\varepsilon$ (during decreasing branch).

#### **Step 1 — Switching Angles for Hysteretic Relay**

For $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$:

- **Switch ON** at angle $\alpha_1 = \pi - \sin^{-1}(\varepsilon/A)$ (when $x = +\varepsilon$ on the way down... wait, **carefully trace the loop**).

The standard result is:

$$
\alpha_1 = \pi - \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{A}\right), \quad \alpha_2 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{A}\right)
$$

Output switches from $-M$ to $+M$ at $\omega t = \alpha_1$ and back at $\omega t = \alpha_2$ (of the next half-cycle).

#### **Step 2 — Compute Fundamental Coefficients $B_1$ and $A_1$**

For a hysteretic relay, **both** $B_1$ (sine term) and $A_1$ (cosine term) are non-zero. The describing function is **complex**:

$$
N(A) = \frac{1}{A}\big(A_1 - jB_1\big)
$$

Carrying out the integrals (with $\phi = \sin^{-1}(\varepsilon/A)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{A^{2} - \varepsilon^{2}} \\[6pt]
A_1 &= \frac{-4M\varepsilon}{\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — the sign and structure for the complex describing function is:

$$
\boxed{\;N(A) = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\varepsilon}{A}\right)^{2}} \;-\; j\frac{4M\varepsilon}{\pi A^{2}}\;}
$$

> **KTU Valuation Tip:** The imaginary part carries the **negative** sign convention. Some textbooks write the $-j$ as $+j$ depending on phasor convention. **Always state your sign convention explicitly** in your answer script.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Relay

| **If your control requirement is…** | **Use this relay type** | **Reason** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fastest possible switching, no chattering concern | Ideal 2-position | Highest gain, simplest |
| Avoid chattering near zero (no actuator motion for small errors) | 3-position with dead zone | Silent zone $\vert x \vert < \Delta$ |
| Eliminate chattering + tolerate noise | 2-position with hysteresis | Noise-immunity band $\pm\varepsilon$ |
| Maximum smoothness + noise rejection | 3-position with both | Engineering best-of-both |
| Multi-stage actuation (e.g., gear-shift logic) | Multi-position | Multiple discrete outputs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relay Block within a Closed-Loop System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rref["Reference Input r t"]:::inputNode
    SumNode(("Sum Junction")):::sumNode

    SumNode --> ErrX

```

**Reading the diagram:** The relay sits in the **forward path**, directly between the error signal $e(t)$ and the plant input. Its non-linearity $N(A)$ replaces a linear controller entirely — this is the essence of a *relay control system*.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Input/Output Characteristics of All Five Relay Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    BranchA{"Is Memory Present"}:::decisionNode
    IdealRel["Ideal Two Position Relay y equals M times sgn of x"]:::idealNode
    ThreeRel["Three Position Relay with Dead Zone width 2 Delta"]:::threeNode
```
