    CombinedRel["Three Position Relay with Dead Zone and Hysteresis"]:::combinedNode
    MultiRel["Multi Position n Level Relay"]:::multiNode
    Out(["OUTPUT y t in set minus M, zero, plus M, plus additional levels"]):::endNode

    Start --> BranchA
    BranchA -- "No memory" --> IdealRel
    BranchA -- "No memory plus dead zone" --> ThreeRel
    BranchA -- "Has memory" --> BranchB
    BranchB -- "Yes" --> HystRel
    BranchB -- "Yes plus dead zone" --> CombinedRel
    BranchA -- "n levels" --> MultiRel

    ThreeRel --> Out
    HystRel --> Out

    ChooseRelay{"Select Relay Type"}:::decNode

    ThreeDF["3 Position with Dead Zone DF equals 4M over piA times sqrt 1 minus Delta over A squared"]:::mathNode
    HystDF["2 Position with Hysteresis DF equals 4M over piA sqrt 1 minus epsilon over A squared minus j 4M epsilon over piA squared"]:::mathNode

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Relay Behavioural State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StatePosM : x exceeds positive threshold
    StatePosM : Output equals plus M
    StatePosM --> StateNegM : x falls below negative threshold
    StateNegM : Output equals minus M
    StateNegM --> StatePosM : x rises above positive threshold
    StateNegM --> StateZero : x enters dead zone plus hysteresis band
    StatePosM --> StateZero : x enters dead zone plus hysteresis band
    StateZero : Output equals zero
    StateZero --> StatePosM : x exceeds positive threshold Delta
    StateZero --> StateNegM : x falls below negative threshold Delta
    [*] --> StateNegM : x below negative threshold
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1.** What is a relay in a non-linear control system? List any two types of relays.

**Model Answer:**
A relay is a non-linear element whose output switches abruptly between discrete levels when its input crosses preset threshold values. It is a piecewise-constant static non-linearity used in on-off, bang-bang, and sliding-mode control systems.
**Two types:**
1. Ideal two-position relay (signum-type, output $\pm M$)
2. Three-position relay with dead zone (output $-M, 0, +M$)

*(Valid alternatives: Relay with hysteresis, Multi-position relay, etc.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between a relay with dead zone and a relay with hysteresis.

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Relay with Dead Zone** | **Relay with Hysteresis** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Memory** | Memoryless (single-valued) | Hysteretic (multi-valued) |
| **Switching rule** | Depends only on current input $x$ | Depends on current input and past output |
| **Output in central region** | Always zero for $\vert x\vert \le \Delta$ | Depends on which threshold was last crossed |
| **Chattering behaviour** | Avoids chatter *only* at zero crossing | Avoids chatter robustly over full $\pm\varepsilon$ band |
| **Describing function** | Purely real | Complex (with $-j$ term) |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the input-output characteristics of the following relays:
    (i) Ideal two-position relay
    (ii) Three-position relay with dead zone
    (iii) Two-position relay with hysteresis **— (7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the describing function for an ideal two-position relay. **— (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]**

**(i) Ideal Two-Position Relay**

Output equation:

$$
y(x) = M\,\text{sgn}(x) = \begin{cases} +M, & x > 0 \\ -M, & x < 0 \end{cases}
$$

The characteristic is a vertical jump at $x = 0$, from $-M$ to $+M$. Single-valued, memoryless. *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Three-Position Relay with Dead Zone**

$$
y(x) = \begin{cases} +M, & x > \Delta \\ 0, & \vert x \vert \leq \Delta \\ -M, & x < -\Delta \end{cases}
$$

The output remains zero within the dead zone $-\Delta \leq x \leq \Delta$, then jumps to $\pm M$ outside. Single-valued, memoryless. The dead zone prevents unnecessary actuator activity near the setpoint. *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Two-Position Relay with Hysteresis**

The characteristic is a *rectangular hysteresis loop* with switching thresholds at $\pm\varepsilon$. The output is $+M$ when the input rises above $+\varepsilon$, and switches to $-M$ only when the input falls below $-\varepsilon$. Multi-valued — the output for a given input depends on the past trajectory. *(3 marks)*

```
  y
  |    +M  _____________
  |       |             |
  |       |             |
  +-------+-------------+----> x
  |      -ε      +ε
  |       |             |
  |       |_____________|
  |    -M
```

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]**

**Derivation of Describing Function for Ideal 2-Position Relay**

Let $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$ and $y(t) = M\,\text{sgn}(x(t))$.

**Step 1 — Output waveform:** Since the relay flips sign at every zero of $x$, $y(t)$ is a square wave of amplitude $\pm M$.

**Step 2 — Fourier Series:**

$$
y(t) = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Extract first harmonic ($n=1$):**

$$
y_1(t) = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4 — Describing function:**

$$
N(A) = \frac{Y_1}{X_1} = \frac{4M/\pi}{A} = \boxed{\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}}
$$

The describing function is purely real, amplitude-dependent, and decreases as $1/A$. For $A \to 0$, $N(A) \to \infty$ — the relay has *infinite effective gain* for tiny inputs. For $A \to \infty$, $N(A) \to 0$ — the relay becomes ineffective for huge inputs. *(Full marks distributed: 2 marks for setup, 2 for Fourier series, 2 for DF definition, 1 for physical interpretation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the describing function of a three-position relay with dead zone of width $2\Delta$ and amplitude $\pm M$. **— (7 Marks)**

**(b)** A relay control system has $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{10}{j\omega(1 + j\omega)(1 + 0.5j\omega)}$ in the forward path with an ideal relay of amplitude $M = 1$ in the feedback loop. Using the describing function method, determine whether the system exhibits a limit cycle, and if so, find its amplitude and frequency. **— (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]**

**Given:** Three-position relay — output $0$ for $\vert x \vert \le \Delta$, and $\pm M$ for $\vert x \vert > \Delta$.

**Step 1 — Let $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$.** The relay fires at angle $\alpha$ where $A\sin\alpha = \Delta$:

$$
\alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Output is non-zero only in the intervals** $[\alpha, \pi-\alpha]$ (where $y = +M$) and $[\pi+\alpha, 2\pi-\alpha]$ (where $y = -M$).

**Step 3 — Fundamental sine coefficient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \frac{4M}{\pi}\cos(\alpha) \\
    &= \frac{4M}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Describing function:**

$$
\boxed{\;N(A) = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}}\;}
$$

Valid for $A > \Delta$. When $\Delta = 0$, this reduces to the ideal relay result $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$, as a sanity check. *(7 marks: 1.5 for switching angle, 2 for integral setup, 2 for evaluation, 1.5 for final expression + check.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Set up the characteristic equation for limit cycle:**

For a relay system, a limit cycle occurs where the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ intersects $-\dfrac{1}{N(A)}$.

Since this is an **ideal relay**, $-\dfrac{1}{N(A)} = -\dfrac{\pi A}{4M} = -\dfrac{\pi A}{4}$ (since $M = 1$).

So we need to find $A$ and $\omega$ such that:

$$
G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(A)} = -\frac{\pi A}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Real and Imaginary parts of $G(j\omega)$:**

Let $u = \omega$. Then $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{10}{j\omega(1 + j\omega)(1 + 0.5j\omega)}$.

After rationalization:

$$
\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = \frac{-10(1 - 0.5\omega^{2})}{\omega^{2}(1+\omega^{2})(1+0.25\omega^{2})}
$$

$$
\text{Im}[G(j\omega)] = \frac{-10(1 - 0.75\omega^{2})}{\omega^{2}(1+\omega^{2})(1+0.25\omega^{2})}
$$

Wait — let me redo more carefully. Denominator magnitude-squared: $\omega^{2}(1+\omega^{2})(1+0.25\omega^{2})$.

Multiplying numerator and denominator's complex conjugate:

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{10 \cdot (-j\omega)(1-j\omega)(1-0.5j\omega)}{\omega^{2}(1+\omega^{2})(1+0.25\omega^{2})}
$$

The denominator is purely real and positive. The numerator's imaginary part, when expanded, gives us:

For the limit cycle, **Imag**$[G(j\omega)] = 0$, which requires:

$$
1 - 0.75\omega^{2} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Re$[G(j\omega)]$ at $\omega = 1.155$:**

$\omega^{2} = 4/3$, so $1 + \omega^{2} = 7/3$, $1 + 0.25\omega^{2} = 1 + 1/3 = 4/3$.

$$
\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = \frac{-10(1 - 0.5 \cdot 4/3)}{(4/3)(7/3)(4/3)} = \frac{-10(1 - 2/3)}{112/27} = \frac{-10/3}{112/27} = \frac{-10 \cdot 27}{3 \cdot 112} = \frac{-270}{336} = -0.804
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for amplitude:**

$$
-\frac{\pi A}{4} = -0.804 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A = \frac{0.804 \times 4}{\pi} = \frac{3.216}{3.14159} \approx 1.024
$$

**Conclusion:** The system exhibits a **stable limit cycle** with amplitude $A \approx 1.024$ and frequency $\omega \approx 1.155$ rad/s.

*(Mark distribution: 2 for setting up DF equation, 2 for finding $\omega$, 1.5 for finding $A$, 1.5 for stability & interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of the imaginary part** in hysteresis DF — KTU answer scripts lose **2 to 3 marks** per question for missing the $-j$ term.
> 2. **Conflating the switching angle $\alpha$** — students confuse "switch-on" vs "switch-off" angles for the dead-zone relay. Always derive $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/A)$ from scratch.
> 3. **Plotting the wrong direction of hysteresis loop** — the loop must be **counter-clockwise** for the standard convention. Drawing it clockwise loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not stating the validity range** $A > \Delta$ (or $A > \varepsilon$) — KTU answer key deducts 1 mark for missing the validity condition.
> 5. **Mixing up the roles of $\Delta$ (dead zone half-width)** and $\varepsilon$ (hysteresis half-width) — they are **distinct** parameters and must not be interchanged.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **relay** is a **piecewise-constant static non-linearity** with output switching between discrete levels at threshold inputs.
- ✅ **Five canonical types:** Ideal 2-position, 3-position with dead zone, 2-position with hysteresis, 3-position with dead-zone + hysteresis, multi-position.
- ✅ **Ideal 2-position relay DF:** $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ — purely real, no imaginary part.
- ✅ **3-position with dead-zone DF:** $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}}$ — purely real, valid for $A > \Delta$.
- ✅ **2-position with hysteresis DF:** $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\varepsilon}{A}\right)^{2}} - j\dfrac{4M\varepsilon}{\pi A^{2}}$ — **complex**, valid for $A > \varepsilon$.
- ✅ Dead-zone relays are **memoryless (single-valued)**; hysteresis relays are **multi-valued (memory)**.
- ✅ Dead-zone width $= 2\Delta$; Hysteresis bandwidth $= 2\varepsilon$.
- ✅ Describing function analysis predicts **limit cycles** by intersecting $G(j\omega)$ with $-\dfrac{1}{N(A)}$.
- ✅ Real-world examples: thermostats (dead zone), Schmitt triggers (hysteresis), solenoid valves (ideal), fuel injectors (multi-position).
- ✅ Always state **validity range, sign convention, and physical interpretation** in KTU exam answers.
- ✅ The relay is the **core switching element** of sliding-mode control — remember this for higher-module questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Springs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Springs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Non-Linear Control Systems** (Module 1 – Mechanical Non-Linearities), a **spring** is a mechanical energy-storage element that exhibits a restoring force $F$ as a function of its displacement $x$ from the equilibrium (unstressed) position. When this force–displacement relationship deviates from the classical linear Hooke's law ($F = kx$), the spring is classified as a **non-linear spring**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In PERUT742, springs are studied not as mechanical design components, but as **sources of intentional (or parasitic) non-linearities** that fundamentally alter system dynamics, stability, and equilibrium points.

### 1.2 Classification of Springs in Non-Linear Control

Springs are mathematically classified based on the **shape of their force–displacement characteristic curve**:

| Category | Spring Type | Nature |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| **Linear** | Hookean Spring | Linear (Baseline Reference) |
| **Non-Linear** | Hardening / Progressive | Cubic stiffening |
| **Non-Linear** | Softening / Degressive | Cubic softening |
| **Non-Linear** | Bilinear / Piecewise Linear | Two distinct slopes |
| **Non-Linear** | Spring with Dead Zone | No force in central gap |
| **Non-Linear** | Preloaded Spring | Constant offset force |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The Trampoline Story

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine standing on a **trampoline** (linear) versus a **bowflex resistance band** (hardening spring) versus a **soft foam cushion that bottoms out** (softening spring).  
> - A *trampoline* pushes back the same way for small and large jumps.  
> - A *resistance band* gets **noticeably harder** the more you stretch it.  
> - A *foam cushion* gives easily at first, then **suddenly becomes rigid** at the bottom.  
>   
> These three feel different because the **stiffness $k$ is not constant** — it depends on position $x$. This position-dependent stiffness is the **essence of spring non-linearity**.

## 1.4 Why Springs Matter in Non-Linear Control

The presence of a non-linear spring in a mass-spring-damper system transforms the governing differential equation from a **linear second-order ODE** (with constant coefficients) into a **non-linear ODE** (with state-dependent coefficients). This leads to:

- Multiple equilibrium points
- Jump phenomena (bifurcations)
- Limit cycle oscillations
- Sub-harmonic and super-harmonic resonances
- Chaos (in coupled systems)

> [!TIP]  
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU questions on springs almost always ask for the *mathematical model* and the *resulting non-linear equation of motion*. Memorize the force expressions, not just the names.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Modelling Approach

For every spring, the analyst must derive:
1. The **force equation** $F(x)$
2. The **stiffness (slope) equation** $k(x) = \frac{dF}{dx}$
3. The **potential energy equation** $U(x) = \int_{0}^{x} F(\sigma)\, d\sigma$

These three quantities are **interchangeable** in KTU problems — once you find one, the others follow by differentiation or integration.

## 2.2 Detailed Type-by-Type Analysis

### 2.2.1 Linear (Hookean) Spring — The Reference Model

- **Governing Law:** Hooke's Law
- **Stiffness:** Constant
- **Symmetry:** Symmetric about the origin ($F(-x) = -F(x)$)

The restoring force is strictly proportional to displacement. This is the **baseline** against which all non-linear springs are compared in KTU exam problems.

### 2.2.2 Hardening (Progressive) Spring

- **Governing Law:** $F(x) = kx + \alpha x^3$, where $\alpha > 0$
- **Stiffness:** $k(x) = k + 3\alpha x^2$ — **increases** with displacement
- **Physical meaning:** The spring becomes *stiffer* the more it is stretched or compressed.
- **Energy storage:** Higher than linear spring for the same displacement (cubic term adds energy).

> [!NOTE]  
> Hardening springs are also called **stiffening springs** or **cubic springs**. They are commonly modelled with a **positive cubic term**.

### 2.2.3 Softening (Degressive) Spring

- **Governing Law:** $F(x) = kx - \alpha x^3$, where $\alpha > 0$
- **Stiffness:** $k(x) = k - 3\alpha x^2$ — **decreases** with displacement
- **Physical meaning:** The spring becomes *softer* as it stretches further.
- **Stability caveat:** For large $|x|$, stiffness can become **negative**, leading to buckling and unbounded motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Classic KTU Question Hook:** "A softening spring with $F = kx - \alpha x^3$ produces a Duffing-type equation. Find equilibrium points and discuss stability." This is a **guaranteed 14-mark problem** pattern.

### 2.2.4 Bilinear (Piecewise Linear) Spring

- **Governing Law:** Two linear regions with different slopes, meeting at a **kink point** $\pm x_0$
- **Physical meaning:** A central "soft" zone flanked by two "stiff" zones (or vice versa).
- **KTU Exam Relevance:** Often appears in **bouncing ball, relay, and saturation** problems.

### 2.2.5 Spring with Dead Zone

- **Governing Law:** No force is exerted for $|x| < \delta$; beyond that, normal Hookean behaviour resumes.
- **Physical meaning:** The spring must be displaced by at least $\delta$ before it engages.
- **Engineering Example:** Slack in a cable, backlash in a gear train, air gap in a magnetic actuator.

### 2.2.6 Preloaded Spring

- **Governing Law:** $F(x) = F_0 \pm kx$ — there is an **initial force** $F_0$ even at zero displacement.
- **Physical meaning:** The spring is pre-compressed (or pre-tensioned) at equilibrium.
- **Use Case:** Clutch springs, valve springs, suspension systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Yield Formula Table** — Memorize these entries in **bold-face**. They account for $\geq 80\%$ of marks in spring-related questions.

| Spring Type | Force Equation $F(x)$ | Stiffness $k(x) = dF/dx$ | Potential Energy $U(x)$ |
|-------------|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Linear (Hookean)** | $kx$ | $k$ (constant) | $\tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ |
| **Hardening (Cubic)** | $kx + \alpha x^3$ | $k + 3\alpha x^2$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} k x^2 + \tfrac{1}{4} \alpha x^4$ |
| **Softening (Cubic)** | $kx - \alpha x^3$ | $k - 3\alpha x^2$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} k x^2 - \tfrac{1}{4} \alpha x^4$ |
| **Bilinear (Soft-Centre)** | $\begin{cases} k_1 x & \vert x \vert \leq x_0 \\ k_2 x + (k_1 - k_2) x_0 \text{sgn}(x) & \vert x \vert > x_0 \end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases} k_1 & \vert x \vert \leq x_0 \\ k_2 & \vert x \vert > x_0 \end{cases}$ | Piecewise quadratic |
| **Dead Zone** | $\begin{cases} 0 & \vert x \vert \leq \delta \\ k(x - \delta\text{sgn}(x)) & \vert x \vert > \delta \end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases} 0 & \vert x \vert \leq \delta \\ k & \vert x \vert > \delta \end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases} 0 & \vert x \vert \leq \delta \\ \tfrac{1}{2} k (x \mp \delta)^2 & \vert x \vert > \delta \end{cases}$ |
| **Preloaded** | $F_0 + kx$ (for $x \geq 0$) | $k$ (constant) | $F_0 x + \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ |

> **Notation:** $\text{sgn}(x)$ is the signum function. All $\alpha, k, k_1, k_2, F_0, \delta, x_0 > 0$ are **positive real constants**.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Non-linear springs appear in:
- **Aerospace:** Aeroelastic flutter analysis (hardening/softening in lifting surfaces).
- **Automotive:** Suspension systems, CV joint boots, clutch plates.
- **Robotics:** Variable-stiffness actuators (VSAs) for safe human–robot interaction.
- **Civil Engineering:** Cable-stayed bridges with geometric stiffening.
- **MEMS:** Micro-beams with cubic stiffening from mid-plane stretching.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Potential Energy of a Hardening Spring

Starting from the force expression for a hardening spring:

$$F(x) = kx + \alpha x^3$$

The **potential (strain) energy** stored in the spring is defined as the work done in displacing it from the unstressed position $x = 0$ to a final displacement $x$:

$$U(x) = \int_{0}^{x} F(\sigma)\, d\sigma$$

Substituting the force expression:

$$U(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \left( k\sigma + \alpha \sigma^{3} \right) d\sigma$$

Split the integral into two terms (linearity of integration):

$$U(x) = \int_{0}^{x} k\sigma\, d\sigma \; + \; \int_{0}^{x} \alpha \sigma^{3}\, d\sigma$$

Evaluate each integral separately using the standard power rule $\int \sigma^{n} d\sigma = \frac{\sigma^{n+1}}{n+1}$:

$$U(x) = k \left[ \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{x} \; + \; \alpha \left[ \frac{\sigma^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{x}$$

Apply the upper and lower limits:

$$U(x) = k \left( \frac{x^{2}}{2} - 0 \right) \; + \; \alpha \left( \frac{x^{4}}{4} - 0 \right)$$

Final result:

$$\boxed{U(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^{2} + \tfrac{1}{4} \alpha x^{4}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The first term is the conventional linear elastic energy. The second (quartic) term is the **extra energy** stored due to the hardening behaviour — it grows faster than $x^2$, which is why the system resists large displacements more aggressively.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equation of Motion with a Non-Linear Spring

Consider a mass $m$ attached to a hardening spring and a viscous damper $b$, with no external forcing. Newton's second law gives:

$$m\ddot{x} = -F(x) - b\dot{x}$$

Substitute the hardening spring force $F(x) = kx + \alpha x^{3}$:

$$m\ddot{x} = -(kx + \alpha x^{3}) - b\dot{x}$$

Bring all terms to the left-hand side:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx + \alpha x^{3} = 0$$

**Final Equation (Duffing Oscillator — Unforced, Undamped, Hardening):**

$$\boxed{m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx + \alpha x^{3} = 0}$$

**Step-by-step conversion logic:**

- **Step 1:** Newton's 2nd law — net force equals mass × acceleration.
- **Step 2:** Restoring force opposes displacement (negative sign).
- **Step 3:** Viscous damping force opposes velocity (negative sign).
- **Step 4:** Substitute the non-linear force law.
- **Step 5:** Rearrange to the standard **state-zero** form (homogeneous ODE).

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Significance:** This is the **canonical Duffing equation**, a cornerstone of non-linear dynamics. Any spring-type problem in Module 1 of PERUT742 will reduce to (or extend) this form.

## 3.3 Derivation: Stiffness Loss Condition for a Softening Spring

For the softening spring $F(x) = kx - \alpha x^{3}$:

$$k(x) = \frac{dF}{dx} = k - 3\alpha x^{2}$$

Set $k(x) = 0$ to find the **buckling threshold** (the point beyond which the spring offers no resistance):

$$k - 3\alpha x^{2} = 0$$

Solve for $x$:

$$3\alpha x^{2} = k \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x^{2} = \frac{k}{3\alpha}$$

Take the positive square root (the buckling displacement is symmetric):

$$\boxed{\vert x \vert_{crit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{3\alpha}}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** For $|x| < |x|_{crit}$, the spring provides a restoring force and the system oscillates. For $|x| > |x|_{crit}$, the cubic term **overwhelms** the linear term, the "stiffness" becomes negative, and the system is **unstable** — leading to unbounded growth or a snap-through jump.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Equilibrium Points & Their Linearized Stability

For the unforced, undamped Duffing oscillator with a softening spring:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx - \alpha x^{3} = 0$$

Equilibrium points are found by setting $\ddot{x} = 0$ and $\dot{x} = 0$:

$$kx - \alpha x^{3} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x(k - \alpha x^{2}) = 0$$

Three equilibrium points exist:

$$x_{eq} = 0, \quad x_{eq} = +\sqrt{k/\alpha}, \quad x_{eq} = -\sqrt{k/\alpha}$$

Linearize using $k_{lin}(x_{eq}) = k - 3\alpha x_{eq}^{2}$ and apply the rule: **stable if $k_{lin} > 0$, unstable if $k_{lin} < 0$**.

| Equilibrium $x_{eq}$ | Linearized Stiffness $k_{lin}$ | Stability |
|-----------------------|-------------------------------|-----------|
| $0$ | $k$ (positive) | **Stable** (centre) |
| $+\sqrt{k/\alpha}$ | $k - 3\alpha(k/\alpha) = -2k$ (negative) | **Unstable** |
| $-\sqrt{k/\alpha}$ | $k - 3\alpha(k/\alpha) = -2k$ (negative) | **Unstable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Insight:** The softening spring Duffing oscillator has **one stable equilibrium (at the origin) flanked by two unstable equilibria** — this is the classic **bistable potential** that drives snap-through behaviour in non-linear control.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spring Elements in Non Linear Control] --> B[Linear Spring]
    A --> C[Non Linear Spring]
    B --> B1[Hookean F equals kx]
    B --> B2[Stiffness k constant]
    B --> B3[Single equilibrium at origin]
    C --> D[Cubic Type]
    C --> E[Piecewise Type]
    C --> F[Discontinuous Type]
    D --> D1[Hardening F equals kx plus alpha x cubed]
    D --> D2[Softening F equals kx minus alpha x cubed]
    E --> E1[Bilinear Two slopes with kink]
    E --> E2[Dead Zone No force for small x]
    F --> F1[Preloaded Initial force F0]
    F --> F2[On Off Relay Type]
    D1 --> G1[Stiffness grows with x]
    D2 --> G2[Stiffness drops with x]
    E1 --> H1[Saturation in mechanical systems]
    E2 --> H2[Backlash in gear trains]
    F1 --> I1[Valve and clutch systems]
    F2 --> I2[Bang Bang actuators]
```

## 4.2 Force-Displacement Characteristic (Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[Input Displacement x] --> K{Spring Type Selector}
    K -->|Linear| L1[Output F equals k times x]
    K -->|Hardening| L2[Output F equals kx plus alpha x cubed]
    K -->|Softening| L3[Output F equals kx minus alpha x cubed]
    K -->|Dead Zone| L4[Output zero for small x, then k times x]
    K -->|Preloaded| L5[Output F0 plus k times x]
    L1 --> M1[Stiffness Constant]
    L2 --> M2[Stiffness Increases with x]
    L3 --> M3[Stiffness Decreases with x]
    L4 --> M4[Stiffness Zero or Constant]
    L5 --> M5[Stiffness Constant with offset]
    M1 --> N1[Linear ODE]
    M2 --> N2[Duffing Equation Hardening]
    M3 --> N3[Duffing Equation Softening]
    M4 --> N4[Piecewise ODE]
    M5 --> N5[Shifted Equilibrium]
```

## 4.3 Equation of Motion Synthesis Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Mass m and Damper b] --> P[Select Spring Type]
    P --> Q1[Linear]
    P --> Q2[Hardening]
    P --> Q3[Softening]
    P --> Q4[Dead Zone]
    Q1 --> R1[m x double dot plus b x dot plus k x equals F applied]
    Q2 --> R2[m x double dot plus b x dot plus k x plus alpha x cubed equals F applied]
    Q3 --> R3[m x double dot plus b x dot plus k x minus alpha x cubed equals F applied]
    Q4 --> R4[m x double dot plus b x dot plus k times x minus delta equals F applied when x greater than delta]
    R1 --> S1[Linear Second Order System]
    R2 --> S2[Hardening Duffing Oscillator]
    R3 --> S3[Softening Duffing Oscillator]
    R4 --> S4[Non Smooth Dynamical System]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Every leaf node (e.g., `R2`, `R3`, `R4`) represents the **final differential equation** you must write in the KTU exam. The intermediate nodes are intermediate reasoning steps the examiner awards partial credit for.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Short-Answer Conceptual
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

> Define a **hardening spring** and a **softening spring**. Give one mathematical model for each and state how the stiffness varies with displacement.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hardening spring** is a non-linear spring whose effective stiffness *increases* with displacement magnitude. Its force–displacement law is:

$$F(x) = kx + \alpha x^{3}, \quad \alpha > 0$$

with stiffness:

$$k(x) = k + 3\alpha x^{2}$$

A **softening spring** is a non-linear spring whose effective stiffness *decreases* with displacement magnitude. Its law is:

$$F(x) = kx - \alpha x^{3}, \quad \alpha > 0$$

with stiffness:

$$k(x) = k - 3\alpha x^{2}$$

**(1 mark – definition of hardening, 1 mark – definition of softening, 1 mark – stiffness expressions.)**

### Question A2: Short-Answer Derivative
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

> A non-linear spring has the force characteristic $F(x) = 5x + 0.2x^{3}$ (N, with $x$ in metres). Find the potential energy stored when $x = 0.5$ m.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The potential energy is:

$$U(x) = \int_{0}^{x} F(\sigma)\, d\sigma = \int_{0}^{x} (5\sigma + 0.2\sigma^{3})\, d\sigma$$

Evaluate:

$$U(x) = 5 \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} + 0.2 \cdot \frac{x^{4}}{4} = 2.5 x^{2} + 0.05 x^{4}$$

Substitute $x = 0.5$ m:

$$U(0.5) = 2.5 (0.25) + 0.05 (0.0625) = 0.625 + 0.003125 = 0.6281 \text{ J}$$

**Answer: $U(0.5) = 0.6281$ J. (1 mark – setup, 1 mark – integration, 1 mark – substitution and final value.)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified from Past Pattern]**

> Consider a mass $m = 2$ kg attached to a **softening spring** with force characteristic $F(x) = 20x - 5x^{3}$ N, and a viscous damper with coefficient $b = 4$ Ns/m. The system is initially at rest at $x = 0$.
>
> **(a)** Derive the **equation of motion** and identify the resulting non-linear differential equation. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Find all **equilibrium points** and determine their **stability** using linearization. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Choice A — Part (a) [7 marks]

Apply Newton's second law to the mass:

$$m\ddot{x} = \sum F = -F_{spring}(x) - F_{damper}(\dot{x})$$

Substitute the spring and damper forces:

$$2\ddot{x} = -(20x - 5x^{3}) - 4\dot{x}$$

Rearrange all terms to the left:

$$2\ddot{x} + 4\dot{x} + 20x - 5x^{3} = 0$$

Divide through by $m = 2$ to obtain the standard normalized form:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\dot{x} + 10x - 2.5 x^{3} = 0$$

**Identification:** This is a **softening-type Duffing oscillator** (negative cubic stiffness term). **(2 marks — equation of motion setup, 3 marks — substitution and simplification, 2 marks — identification and final standard form.)**

#### Model Solution for Choice A — Part (b) [7 marks]

Equilibrium requires $\dot{x} = 0$ and $\ddot{x} = 0$:

$$10x - 2.5x^{3} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x(10 - 2.5x^{2}) = 0$$

Solve:

$$x_{1} = 0, \quad x_{2} = +\sqrt{10/2.5} = +2, \quad x_{3} = -\sqrt{10/2.5} = -2$$

Compute the **linearized stiffness** $k_{lin} = \frac{d}{dx}(10x - 2.5x^{3})\Big\vert_{x_{eq}} = 10 - 7.5x_{eq}^{2}$:

- At $x_{1} = 0$: $k_{lin} = 10 > 0$ → **Stable equilibrium (centre).**
- At $x_{2} = +2$: $k_{lin} = 10 - 7.5(4) = 10 - 30 = -20 < 0$ → **Unstable (saddle).**
- At $x_{3} = -2$: $k_{lin} = 10 - 7.5(4) = -20 < 0$ → **Unstable (saddle).**

**Stability Summary Table (for full marks, present in a table):**

| Equilibrium | $k_{lin}$ | Stability |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| $x = 0$ | $+10$ | **Stable** |
| $x = +2$ | $-20$ | **Unstable** |
| $x = -2$ | $-20$ | **Unstable** |

**(2 marks — finding equilibria, 3 marks — linearization, 2 marks — stability classification and table.)**

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, Module 1 Focus]**

> A mechanical oscillator consists of a mass $m = 1$ kg, a linear damper $b = 2$ Ns/m, and a **hardening spring** with the force law $F(x) = 8x + x^{3}$ (N, $x$ in m).
>
> **(a)** Write the **equation of motion** and confirm that it represents a **hardening Duffing oscillator**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Derive the **expression for potential energy** $U(x)$ and explain, using physical reasoning, why the system resists large displacements more strongly than a linear spring. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Choice B — Part (a) [7 marks]

Newton's second law:

$$m\ddot{x} = -F_{spring} - F_{damper}$$

$$1 \cdot \ddot{x} = -(8x + x^{3}) - 2\dot{x}$$

Rearrange:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\dot{x} + 8x + x^{3} = 0$$

This is a **second-order non-linear ODE**. Identification of the non-linear terms:

- Linear stiffness: $k = 8$ N/m.
- Cubic coefficient: $\alpha = 1$ (positive).
- Sign of cubic term: **positive** → hardening behaviour.

Compare with the canonical **Duffing equation**:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx + \alpha x^{3} = 0$$

The two forms match exactly, confirming this is a **hardening Duffing oscillator**. **(2 marks — equation setup, 2 marks — substitution, 3 marks — identification of canonical form and discussion.)**

#### Model Solution for Choice B — Part (b) [7 marks]

Potential energy:

$$U(x) = \int_{0}^{x} F(\sigma)\, d\sigma = \int_{0}^{x} (8\sigma + \sigma^{3})\, d\sigma$$

Evaluate each integral:

$$U(x) = 8 \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} + \frac{x^{4}}{4} = 4x^{2} + 0.25 x^{4}$$

**Physical reasoning (for full marks, write 3–4 sentences):**

The total stored energy has two parts. The first part, $4x^{2}$, is the conventional elastic potential energy of a Hookean spring — it grows **quadratically** with displacement. The second part, $0.25 x^{4}$, is the **additional quartic energy contribution** from the non-linear hardening behaviour. For small displacements, the quadratic term dominates and the spring behaves almost linearly. For large displacements, the quartic term dominates, and the energy grows much faster. Equivalently, the force required to stretch the spring by an extra unit length increases with $x$ — the spring *stiffens*. This is why hardening springs resist large displacements more aggressively than linear springs of the same small-displacement stiffness $k$. **(2 marks — integration, 2 marks — final expression, 3 marks — physical reasoning.)**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Spring Problems in PERUT742:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of the restoring force.** Students often write $m\ddot{x} = +(kx + \alpha x^{3}) - b\dot{x}$. The spring force is *always* negative (opposes displacement). Lose **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Mixing up hardening and softening.** The cubic term sign determines the type. Positive $x^3$ = hardening, negative $x^3$ = softening. A common error is to swap them under exam pressure. Lose **2 marks** if the type is misidentified in a 14-mark question.
> 3. **Omitting the divide-by-$m$ step.** Many students leave the equation as $m\ddot{x} + \ldots = 0$ without normalizing. KTU partial-credit schemes expect a unit-coefficient form: $\ddot{x} + \ldots = 0$. Lose **1 mark** for non-normalized form.
> 4. **Wrong stability criterion.** "Stable if $k_{lin} > 0$" is correct. Writing "stable if $k_{lin} < 0$" is **a fatal error** that flips every conclusion. Lose up to **3 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting the second equilibrium for softening springs.** A softening Duffing system has *three* equilibria, not one. Students who list only $x = 0$ lose **2 marks**.
> 6. **Not drawing or stating the stability table.** Examiners award explicit marks for a **well-labelled stability table** in 7-mark sub-parts. Missing it costs **1–2 marks**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Types of Springs**

- **Linear spring:** $F = kx$. Stiffness constant. Single equilibrium at origin.
- **Hardening spring:** $F = kx + \alpha x^{3}$, $\alpha > 0$. Stiffness **increases** with $|x|$. **Duffing equation** with positive cubic term.
- **Softening spring:** $F = kx - \alpha x^{3}$, $\alpha > 0$. Stiffness **decreases** with $|x|$. Bistable; three equilibria (one stable, two unstable).
- **Bilinear spring:** Two linear regions with a kink at $\pm x_0$. Different slopes $k_1$ and $k_2$.
- **Dead-zone spring:** Zero force for $|x| \leq \delta$; linear $k(x - \delta\text{sgn}(x))$ beyond. Models backlash, slack, air gaps.
- **Preloaded spring:** $F = F_0 + kx$ — constant initial force. Equilibrium is **shifted** from the origin.
- **Buckling threshold (softening):** $|x|_{crit} = \sqrt{k/(3\alpha)}$.
- **Energy stored in a hardening spring:** $U(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} kx^{2} + \tfrac{1}{4}\alpha x^{4}$.
- **Energy stored in a softening spring:** $U(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} kx^{2} - \tfrac{1}{4}\alpha x^{4}$ — non-convex for large $|x|$.
- **Canonical equation of motion (hardening):** $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^{2} x + \alpha x^{3} = u(t)$.
- **Stability rule for linearization:** $k_{lin}(x_{eq}) > 0$ → stable; $k_{lin}(x_{eq}) < 0$ → unstable.
- **Bistable potential** $\Rightarrow$ **snap-through** and **jump phenomena** in softening systems.
- **Always** write the equation of motion in the normalized form $\ddot{x} + \ldots = 0$ for full marks.
- **Always** include a stability table in 7-mark sub-parts.
- **Watch the sign** of the cubic term — it is the single most important KTU exam discriminator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backlash in Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Backlash in Gears # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Backlash in Gears** is defined as the **small clearance or lost motion** intentionally provided between mating tooth surfaces of a pair of gears to prevent jamming and to accommodate manufacturing errors, thermal expansion, and lubrication. In the domain of **Non-Linear Control Systems**, backlash is modeled as a **non-linear dead-zone (multi-valued) element** where the output displacement is a function of both the magnitude and the *direction* of the input motion. Mathematically, backlash is classified as a **static, memory-type, non-linear element** because the output at any instant depends not only on the present input but also on the **past history** of the input (specifically, whether the input is increasing or decreasing). The standard physical backlash value is denoted as **'j'** (in radians or meters), representing the **total angular slack** measured at the output shaft when the input reverses direction. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy: The Window with a Loose Latch** > Imagine pushing a sliding window. When you first start pushing, the latch has some free play — you push with your hand, but the window doesn't move immediately. After a small amount of "wasted" motion, the latch engages and the window finally slides. Similarly, when you *reverse* the push direction, again there is a small "dead zone" where the window doesn't respond until the latch catches. That tiny play between the latch and the catch is exactly **backlash** — it is the "wasted motion" of any mechanical transmission. In gears, this play exists because if teeth were perfectly tight, they would bind, overheat, and quickly fail. Engineers deliberately leave a small gap. The cost: the system becomes **non-linear** because, for a small range of input motion, **no output motion occurs at all**. ## 1.3 Physical Significance in Control Systems | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Backlash zone (j) | **0.03 to 0.10 rad** (typical for control servos) | Manufacturing tolerance + lubrication | | Kinematic viscosity | **Not directly applicable** | — | | Dead-band ratio | j / 2 (half-zone) | Symmetric backlash model input | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *backlash* with *hysteresis*. While backlash is essentially a geometric dead-zone, the describing-function analysis treats it as a **dead-zone with memory** — the output has two possible states for the same input value, depending on motion history. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Backlash Non-Linearity The backlash element is a **two-port mechanical element** with one rotational input (driver gear angle θᵢ) and one rotational output (driven gear angle θₒ). The relationship is governed by the following structured logic: - **Step 1 — Engagement Condition (Forward Motion):** When the input rotates *forward*, the driven gear does **not move** until the input has swept across the entire backlash gap. Mathematically, no torque is transmitted during this interval. - **Step 2 — Meshing Phase:** Once the input tooth contacts the output tooth, the output follows the input with a 1:1 (or N:1) ratio, minus the offset caused by the lost motion. - **Step 3 — Reverse Motion Decoupling:** When the input reverses, the output remains *frozen* at its last value until the input has travelled back across the backlash gap. Then re-engagement occurs. - **Step 4 — Memory Storage:** The element "remembers" the point of last contact, which is why backlash is a **memory non-linearity**. ## 2.2 Mathematical Model (Symmetric Backlash) For a **symmetric backlash non-linearity** of total width **j**, the output x(t) is governed by: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } u(t) &= \text{input motion},\quad x(t) = \text{output motion} \\ \text{Define } \alpha &= \frac{j}{2}\;\; \text{(half-width of the dead zone)} \end{aligned} $$ The **piecewise input-output relation** is: $$ \begin{aligned} x(t) = \begin{cases} u(t) - \alpha, & \text{if } \dot{u} > 0 \text{ and } u > x + \alpha \quad \text{(forward engagement)} \\[4pt] x(t^-), & \text{if } \vert u - x \vert \leq \alpha \quad \text{(floating / disengaged)} \\[4pt] u(t) + \alpha, & \text{if } \dot{u} < 0 \text{ and } u < x - \alpha \quad \text{(reverse engagement)} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ where $x(t^-)$ is the value of x just before the disengagement began. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **must-memorize** for KTU ESE — these formulas appear every year in the describing-function module. | S.No | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Engineering Utility | |------|---------------------|------------|--------------------| | 1 | Half dead-zone | $\alpha = \dfrac{j}{2}$ | Geometric setup | | 2 | Describing function magnitude | $N(A) = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{j}{A}\right) - \dfrac{j}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{j}{A}\right)^2}\right]$ | Predict limit cycles | | 3 | Describing function phase | $\angle N(A) = 0\deg$ (real-valued) | Backlash introduces no phase shift — only gain reduction | | 4 | Limit-cycle condition | $1 + N(A)\,G(j\omega) = 0$ | Closed-loop oscillation test | | 5 | Critical amplitude ratio | $A > j$ (sinusoidal input must exceed j) | Defines "linear" range | | 6 | Equivalent gain (small signal) | $k_{eq} = 0$ for $\vert A \vert \leq j$ | No torque transmission inside dead zone | | 7 | Equivalent gain (large signal) | $k_{eq} \to 1$ as $A \to \infty$ | At high amplitudes, behaves linearly | | 8 | Torque transmitted | $T = k_m(u - x) - c\dot{x}$ | Spring-damper tooth model | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Backlash analysis is critical in: - **Robotic manipulator joints** — affects positional accuracy and tracking. - **CNC machine tools** — directly impacts machining tolerance. - **Servo steering systems of automobiles** — causes "dead spot" in steering feel. - **Antenna pointing systems in radar** — reduces tracking precision. - **Printers and plotters** — causes banding and registration errors. > [!TIP] > **Industry Tip:** Anti-backlash gears (split gears with springs) are used in telescope mounts and aerospace servos. KTU examiners often test this *compensation* concept as a 14-mark question. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Input-Output Characteristic of Symmetric Backlash ### Given: - Input motion: $u(t)$ - Output motion: $x(t)$ - Total backlash width: $j$ - Half-width: $\alpha = \dfrac{j}{2}$ ### To Prove: The three-state output relation stated in Section 2.2. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Define the geometric contact condition.** Two meshing gear teeth make contact only when the relative displacement of the input shaft exceeds the half-width $\alpha$. The contact condition is: $$ \begin{aligned} \vert u - x \vert \geq \alpha \end{aligned} $$ This inequality is the **engagement criterion**. If satisfied, the teeth are in physical contact and torque can be transmitted. **Step 2 — Case (i): Forward motion engagement.** When the input is increasing ($\dot{u} > 0$) and the input has moved sufficiently forward, the driving tooth has just made contact with the *trailing* face of the driven tooth. The output must then equal the input minus the lost motion $\alpha$: $$ \begin{aligned} x(t) = u(t) - \alpha \quad \text{for } \dot{u} > 0 \end{aligned} $$ The minus sign reflects that the output is "behind" the input by the amount $\alpha$ that was lost during the dead zone. **Step 3 — Case (ii): Floating (disengaged) state.** Once the input reverses, the driving tooth leaves the driven tooth. No torque is transmitted, so the output remains at its last engaged value due to inertia and friction: $$ \begin{aligned} x(t) = x(t^-) \quad \text{when } \vert u - x \vert \leq \alpha \end{aligned} $$ The notation $x(t^-)$ denotes the limit from the left — i.e., the last value the output held just before disengagement. **Step 4 — Case (iii): Reverse motion engagement.** When the input has reversed through the full backlash gap and contact is re-established on the opposite tooth face, the output is now "ahead" of the input by $\alpha$: $$ \begin{aligned} x(t) = u(t) + \alpha \quad \text{for } \dot{u} < 0 \end{aligned} $$ The plus sign arises because the *leading* face of the driving tooth now pushes the driven tooth, advancing it by $\alpha$ relative to the input. **Step 5 — Final compact form.** $$ \begin{aligned} x(t) = \begin{cases} u(t) - \alpha, & \text{forward contact} \\ x(t^-), & \text{floating} \\ u(t) + \alpha, & \text{reverse contact} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ This is the canonical **KTU-board** backlash characteristic. It must be drawn as a "bow-tie" or "parallelogram with a gap" — examiners will mark full credit only if all three branches are shown correctly. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Describing Function of Backlash ### Given: A sinusoidal input $u(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ applied to a symmetric backlash element of total width $j$. ### To Prove: The describing function $N(A)$ reduces to a real-valued gain given in the formula sheet. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Output waveform construction.** For $A > j$ (the only case where there is any output), the input crosses the dead-zone at angles $\omega t_1$ and $\omega t_2$ defined by: $$ \begin{aligned} A \sin(\omega t_1) = \alpha, \quad A \sin(\omega t_2) = -\alpha \end{aligned} $$ Solving for the crossing instants: $$ \begin{aligned} \omega t_1 = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{A}\right), \quad \omega t_2 = \pi - \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{A}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Express the output in the three phases.** For a symmetric input cycle, the output trajectory is: $$ \begin{aligned} x(\omega t) = \begin{cases} A \sin(\omega t) - \alpha, & \omega t_1 \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \omega t_1 \quad \text{(forward)} \\ A \sin(\omega t) + \alpha, & \pi + \omega t_1 \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi - \omega t_1 \quad \text{(reverse)} \\ \text{constant} = A - \alpha, & \text{elsewhere} \quad \text{(floating)} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the fundamental component of the output.** The describing function is the complex ratio of the fundamental Fourier coefficient of the output to the input phasor: $$ \begin{aligned} N(A) = \dfrac{1}{\pi A}\int_{0}^{2\pi} x(\omega t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, d(\omega t) \end{aligned} $$ Because the backlash characteristic is *odd-symmetric* over a full cycle, the imaginary part vanishes identically, so $N(A)$ is real: $$ \begin{aligned} N(A) = \dfrac{2}{\pi A}\int_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1}\left[A\sin(\omega t) - \alpha\right]\cos(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral term by term.** First integral: $$ \begin{aligned} I_1 &= \dfrac{2}{\pi A}\int_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1} A \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\ &= \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1} \sin(2\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\ &= \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[-\dfrac{\cos(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left[\cos(2\omega t_1) - \cos(2\pi - 2\omega t_1)\right] \\ &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left[2\cos(2\omega t_1)\right] = \dfrac{\cos(2\omega t_1)}{\pi} \end{aligned} $$ Second integral: $$ \begin{aligned} I_2 &= \dfrac{2}{\pi A}\int_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1}(-\alpha)\cos(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\ &= -\dfrac{2\alpha}{\pi A}\left[\sin(\omega t)\right]_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1} \\ &= -\dfrac{2\alpha}{\pi A}\left[0 - 2\sin(\omega t_1)\right] \\ &= \dfrac{4\alpha \sin(\omega t_1)}{\pi A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\sin(\omega t_1) = \alpha/A$ and $\cos(2\omega t_1) = 1 - 2\sin^2(\omega t_1) = 1 - 2(\alpha/A)^2$.** $$ \begin{aligned} I_1 &= \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[1 - 2\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{A}\right)^2\right] \\ I_2 &= \dfrac{4\alpha^2}{\pi A^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Combine.** $$ \begin{aligned} N(A) &= I_1 + I_2 \\ &= \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[1 - \dfrac{2\alpha^2}{A^2} + \dfrac{4\alpha^2}{A^2}\right] \\ &= \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[1 + \dfrac{2\alpha^2}{A^2}\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to total backlash j using $\alpha = j/2$, hence $\alpha^2 = j^2/4$.** $$ \begin{aligned} N(A) &= \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[1 + \dfrac{2 \cdot j^2/4}{A^2}\right] \\ &= \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[1 + \dfrac{j^2}{2A^2}\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Final canonical KTU form (using $\arcsin$ expression, equivalently):** $$ \begin{aligned} N(A) &= \dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{j}{A}\right) - \dfrac{j}{A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{j}{A}\right)^2}\right] \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification sanity check:** As $A \to \infty$, the ratio $j/A \to 0$, so $N(A) \to \dfrac{1}{\pi}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}$. This matches the well-known result that the **describing function of backlash is bounded between 0 and 0.5**, never reaching unity. This is the key reason backlash *cannot* be ignored in high-precision control loops. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Limit-Cycle Prediction Using Describing Function ### Given: - Linear part: $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ - Non-linearity: symmetric backlash of width $j$ ### To Prove: The amplitude and frequency of the sustained oscillation (limit cycle). ### Solution: **Step 1 — Set up the characteristic equation for oscillation.** Substituting $s = j\omega$ in the linear part: $$ \begin{aligned} G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{j\omega(j\tau\omega + 1)} = \dfrac{K}{j\omega - \tau\omega^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Impose the oscillation condition $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$:** $$ \begin{aligned} G(j\omega) = -\dfrac{1}{N(A)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Equate real and imaginary parts.** Real part: $\dfrac{-K\tau\omega^2}{(\tau\omega^2)^2 + \omega^2} = -\dfrac{1}{N(A)}$ Imaginary part: $\dfrac{-K\omega}{(\tau\omega^2)^2 + \omega^2} = 0$ From the imaginary part, we require $\omega \neq 0$, so the denominator must vanish in a specific way — actually the condition is that the linear Nyquist locus crosses the negative real axis. Setting the imaginary part of $G(j\omega)$ to zero gives $\omega = 0$ or… for $G(s) = K/[s(\tau s+1)]$ the Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis at $\omega = 1/\tau$ with magnitude $K\tau$. **Step 4 — Find the limit-cycle amplitude.** At $\omega = 1/\tau$: $$ \begin{aligned} -\dfrac{1}{N(A)} &= -K\tau \\ \Rightarrow N(A) &= \dfrac{1}{K\tau} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting the backlash describing function: $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{j}{A}\right) - \dfrac{j}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{j}{A}\right)^2}\right] &= \dfrac{1}{K\tau} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Solve numerically** for $A$ given the values of $j$, $K$, $\tau$. The result is the **limit-cycle amplitude**. > [!TIP] > **Examination tip:** For a 14-mark problem, you are NOT required to solve numerically. You must: (a) write the characteristic equation, (b) substitute the describing function, (c) separate real/imaginary parts, and (d) state that $A$ is found by graphical/iteration method. This fetches 10/14 marks. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Backlash in a Closed-Loop Position Control System The following Mermaid block diagram shows the role of backlash in a typical **servo position-control** loop (e.g., a robotic joint driven by a motor through a gear train). ```mermaid graph LR nodeR["Reference
Input r t"] nodeSUMM["Summing
Junction"] nodeAMP["Power
Amplifier"] nodeMTR["DC Servo
Motor"] nodeBKL["BACKLASH
NONLINEARITY
j = 0.05 rad"] nodeGR["Gear Train
N1 to N2"] nodeLD["Load
Position y t"] nodeFBD["Feedback
Potentiometer"] nodeR --> nodeSUMM nodeSUMM --> nodeAMP nodeAMP --> nodeMTR nodeMTR --> nodeBKL nodeBKL --> nodeGR nodeGR --> nodeLD nodeLD --> nodeFBD nodeFBD --> nodeSUMM style nodeBKL fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px style nodeSUMM fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366 style nodeLD fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The backlash element sits **between the motor shaft and the gear train output**. Its non-linear effect is felt by the load, and through the feedback path, by the controller. The red-highlighted block is the source of the non-linearity under analysis. ## 4.2 Input-Output Characteristic Curve (Schematic) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph FORWARD["FORWARD MOTION u dot greater than 0"] nodeF1["Origin 0,0"] nodeF2["Engagement Point
u equal to alpha x equal to 0"] nodeF3["Slope 1 line
x equal to u minus alpha"] nodeF1 --> nodeF2 --> nodeF3 end subgraph FLOATING["FLOATING ZONE
vertical jump on reversal"] nodeFL1["Last value x hold"] nodeFL2["Decoupled no torque"] nodeFL1 --> nodeFL2 end subgraph REVERSE["REVERSE MOTION u dot less than 0"] nodeR1["Reversal point
x held constant"] nodeR2["Re-engagement
u equal to minus alpha"] nodeR3["Slope 1 line
x equal to u plus alpha"] nodeR1 --> nodeR2 --> nodeR3 end FORWARD --> FLOATING FLOATING --> REVERSE style FORWARD fill:#fff4e1 style FLOATING fill:#f0f0f0 style REVERSE fill:#e1f0ff ``` ## 4.3 State-Flow Diagram of the Backlash Element ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Disengaged Disengaged --> ForwardContact: input increases past plus alpha ForwardContact --> Disengaged: input reverses Disengaged --> ReverseContact: input decreases past minus alpha ReverseContact --> Disengaged: input reverses ForwardContact --> ForwardContact: x equals u minus alpha slope 1 ReverseContact --> ReverseContact: x equals u plus alpha slope 1 Disengaged --> Disengaged: x constant memory hold ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Compensation View) ```mermaid graph TB nodeIN["Input Motion
Command"] nodeCTRL["Controller
PID or Lead-Lag"] nodeDRV["Motor Driver"] nodeBKL["Backlash Zone
j"] nodeSP["Split Gear
Preload Spring"] nodeOUT["Output Shaft"] nodeIN --> nodeCTRL nodeCTRL --> nodeDRV nodeDRV --> nodeBKL nodeBKL --> nodeSP nodeSP --> nodeOUT nodeSP -. "Compensation Path" .-> nodeBKL style nodeBKL fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style nodeSP fill:#d1ffd1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading this diagram:** The dashed "Compensation Path" represents the **preload spring** that takes up the slack. In a physical anti-backlash gear, two identical gears are mounted on the output shaft with a torsional spring between them, eliminating the dead-zone. This is the **most common KTU 14-mark question** on backlash. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1.a]** > **Q1.** Define the term *backlash* in gear systems. How is it modeled mathematically in non-linear control? > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Backlash is the small clearance (j) intentionally provided between mating gear teeth to prevent jamming and to allow for thermal expansion and lubrication. **(1 mark)** > In non-linear control, it is modeled as a **dead-zone element with memory**, in which the output is a multi-valued function of the input depending on the direction of motion. **(1 mark)** > The three-state relation is: $x = u - \alpha$ (forward), $x = x(t^-)$ (floating), $x = u + \alpha$ (reverse), where $\alpha = j/2$. **(1 mark)** > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q1.b]** > **Q2.** What are the two main effects of backlash on a closed-loop control system? > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **(1) Limit-cycle oscillations** — sustained oscillations occur in servo systems due to the dead-zone, which can be predicted using describing function analysis. **(1.5 marks)** > **(2) Inaccuracy and poor tracking** — during slow reversals, the output remains constant (floating state), causing a position error equal to the backlash width j. **(1.5 marks)** ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2]** > > ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **(a)** Derive the input-output relationship for a symmetric backlash non-linearity. Clearly show the three operating states with the help of a characteristic plot. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For a system with $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$ in cascade with a backlash non-linearity of $j = 0.4$ rad, determine whether a limit cycle can exist. Use the describing function method. **(7 marks)** > > ### **Model Solution** > > **Part (a) — 7 marks:** > The complete derivation is given in **Section 3.1, Steps 1–5** of these notes. The student should: > - Define $u$, $x$, and $\alpha = j/2$ **(1 mark)** > - State the engagement condition $\vert u - x \vert \geq \alpha$ **(1 mark)** > - Derive the three cases: $x = u - \alpha$, $x = x(t^-)$, $x = u + \alpha$ **(3 marks)** > - Sketch the characteristic with floating zone **(1 mark)** > - State the memory property **(1 mark)** > > **Part (b) — 7 marks:** > The complete solution flow is given in **Section 3.3, Steps 1–5**. The student should: > > **Step 1 — Find the frequency of limit cycle.** Set Im[G(jω)] = 0. **(1 mark)** > For $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$, the Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis at $\omega = \sqrt{2}$ rad/s. **(1 mark)** > > **Step 2 — Find the magnitude at this frequency.** $|G(j\sqrt{2})| = \dfrac{10}{2\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{10}{4} = 2.5$. So $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = -2.5$. **(1 mark)** > > **Step 3 — Apply oscillation condition** $-\dfrac{1}{N(A)} = -2.5 \Rightarrow N(A) = 0.4$. **(1 mark)** > > **Step 4 — Compare with backlash describing function bounds.** $N(A)$ lies in $[0, 0.5]$. Since $0.4 \in [0, 0.5]$, a limit cycle is possible. **(1 mark)** > > **Step 5 — Solve for amplitude A numerically** using the transcendental equation > $\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{0.4}{A}\right) - \dfrac{0.4}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{0.4}{A}\right)^2}\right] = 0.4$. **(1 mark)** > > Iterating gives $A \approx 0.85$ rad. **(1 mark)** > > **Conclusion:** A limit cycle of amplitude $\approx 0.85$ rad and frequency $\approx \sqrt{2}$ rad/s will exist in this system. --- > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q2]** > > ### **Question B (14 Marks)** > **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how backlash arises in a gear train. Discuss its effect on the closed-loop response of a position-control servo. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A position control system uses a motor-gear-load arrangement with backlash $j = 0.2$ rad. Propose and justify **two engineering methods** to mitigate the undesirable effects of backlash. **(7 marks)** > > ### **Model Solution** > > **Part (a) — 7 marks:** > - **Gear train diagram:** Show input gear, output gear, tooth flanks, clearance gap. **(2 marks)** > - **Operating principle:** During forward motion, contact on trailing face; on reverse, contact on leading face; in between, floating. **(2 marks)** > - **Effect on closed-loop response:** (i) Dead-zone in step response causing steady-state error, (ii) Limit cycle during low-speed reversals, (iii) Instability if controller gain is high. **(3 marks)** > > **Part (b) — 7 marks:** > > **Method 1: Use of preloaded split gears (anti-backlash gears).** Two identical gears on the output shaft, separated by a torsional spring, eliminate the dead-zone. Justification: effectively sets $j \to 0$, so $N(A) \to 0.5$ (pure gain), removing the limit cycle. **(3.5 marks)** > > **Method 2: Reduction of loop gain or use of lead-compensator.** Lowering gain ensures the linear locus does not intersect the $-1/N(A)$ curve. Justification: from the limit-cycle analysis, smaller gain shifts the Nyquist plot, preventing sustained oscillation. **(3.5 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Most common 2-mark loss:** Forgetting to specify the *direction of motion* in the piecewise definition. The relation $x = u - \alpha$ holds **only when** $\dot{u} > 0$ and contact condition is satisfied. > - **Second most common loss:** Drawing the characteristic as a *single-valued* curve. Backlash is **multi-valued** — you must show the vertical jump on reversal. > - **Third pitfall:** Confusing the describing function with the *linear* gain. The describing function of backlash is **always ≤ 0.5**, never 1. Examiners love testing this. > - **Numerical trap:** Students often write $N(A) = 1 - \dfrac{j}{\pi A}$ — this is **wrong**. The correct form involves the $\arcsin$ and $\sqrt{1-(j/A)^2}$ terms. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Backlash in Gears** - ☐ **Backlash (j)** = total angular slack at the output shaft when input reverses. - ☐ **Half-width** $\alpha = j/2$ — used in all three-state equations. - ☐ **Three operating states:** forward contact, floating, reverse contact. - ☐ **Memory non-linearity** — output depends on history, not just present input. - ☐ **Dead-zone range:** output frozen when $\vert u - x \vert \leq \alpha$. - ☐ **Describing function** $N(A)$ is **real-valued** (no phase shift), bounded in $[0, 0.5]$. - ☐ **Limit-cycle condition:** $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$. - ☐ **No output for** $A \leq j$ (small inputs produce no response). - ☐ **Compensation methods:** preloaded split gears, lowered loop gain, lead compensator, dither signal. - ☐ **Real-world impact:** positional error, limit cycles, poor low-speed tracking in servos. - ☐ **Master the derivation** of the describing function — it is asked almost every KTU ESE cycle. - ☐ **Sketch the "bow-tie" characteristic** accurately with all three branches — this is a guaranteed sub-question.

topics.title: Inherent Nonlinearity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inherent Nonlinearity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inherent Nonlinearity** refers to the non-linear behaviour that arises **naturally** in physical dynamic systems due to the fundamental physical laws, material properties, geometric constraints, and energy conversion mechanisms governing the system — *as opposed to non-linearities intentionally introduced by the designer for a specific control purpose (e.g., an on-off relay used as a controller).*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PERUT742), inherent non-linearities are categorised under the broader umbrella of **static (memoryless) non-linearities** and **dynamic (memory) non-linearities**, and they form the first conceptual building block before introducing the **Describing Function (DF) method** in subsequent modules.

Mathematically, an inherent non-linearity is a function $N(\cdot)$ such that for an input $x(t)$ and output $y(t)$:

$$y(t) = N\bigl(x(t)\bigr) \quad \text{or} \quad y(t) = N\bigl(x(t),\,\dot{x}(t)\bigr)$$

where $N(\cdot)$ **violates the principle of superposition** (i.e., the system is neither additive nor homogeneous).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wobbly Steering Wheel" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are driving a car with a mechanical steering linkage. When you turn the wheel by a *tiny* amount, **nothing happens to the wheels** (this is the **dead-zone** caused by gear backlash and slack in the linkage). Once you turn past that dead-zone, the wheels begin to turn, but as you keep turning the wheel more and more, the front wheels *stop responding* beyond a maximum angle — they have **saturated**. If you now reverse the steering, the wheels do not immediately follow back — there is a **hysteresis gap** caused by mechanical play. None of these behaviours were *designed* into your car; they are **inherent** to the physical construction.

This is precisely what inherent non-linearity means in control engineering: **the physical hardware itself, by the very nature of its construction, refuses to behave as a perfectly linear element.**

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Dead-zone width | $\Delta$ | Same unit as input signal |
| Saturation level | $\pm M$ | Same unit as output signal |
| Backlash (play) width | $B$ | Same unit as input shaft rotation |
| Coulomb friction torque | $T_c$ | N·m |
| Viscous friction coefficient | $b$ | N·m·s/rad |
| Break-away (stiction) torque | $T_b$ | N·m |
| Hysteresis loop area | $A_h$ | Unit² (energy-like) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PERUT742, Module 1):** The following inherent non-linear elements are *explicitly* listed in the syllabus: **Dead-Zone, Saturation, Backlash, Hysteresis, Relay (with/without dead-zone), Friction (static, coulomb, viscous), and Multi-loop non-linearities.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Inherent Non-Linearities

Inherent non-linearities are classified into two major families based on whether they possess **memory**.

### 2.1.1 Static (Memoryless) Inherent Non-Linearities

These produce an output that depends **only on the instantaneous value of the input**. The output–input relationship can be drawn as a single-valued curve on the $y$–$x$ plane.

| Element | Cause in Physical Systems |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Saturation** | Magnetic saturation in iron-core inductors/transformers; amplifier output limits; valve stroke limits |
| **Dead-Zone** | Threshold voltages in semiconductor devices; stiction threshold in bearings; threshold in magnetic amplifiers |
| **On-Off (Ideal Relay)** | Thermostats; limit switches; Schmitt trigger comparators |
| **Coulomb Friction** | Dry friction between sliding surfaces in bearings, gear trains |
| **Quantization** | Encoder resolution; ADC/DAC discrete levels |

### 2.1.2 Dynamic (Memory) Inherent Non-Linearities

These produce an output that depends on the **history** of the input — they are multi-valued when plotted against instantaneous input.

| Element | Cause in Physical Systems |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Backlash** | Gear-tooth play in gear trains; lost motion in lead screws; slack in chain drives |
| **Hysteresis** | Magnetic hysteresis (B-H loop) in ferromagnets; elastic hysteresis in rubber mounts; pneumatic/hydraulic hysteresis |
| **Static Friction (Stiction)** | Break-away friction higher than running friction in mechanical slides |

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Why Inherent Non-Linearities Cannot Be Ignored

> [!NOTE]
> **Why they matter in KTU exams (and in real engineering):**
> 1. **Superposition fails:** You cannot apply Bode/Nyquist/root-locus directly to a non-linear system.
> 2. **Linearisation is local:** A Taylor-series linearisation around an operating point is only valid for *small* deviations; large signals reveal the true non-linear shape.
> 3. **Unique phenomena appear:** Limit cycles, jump resonance, sub-harmonics, chaos — none of these exist in linear systems.
> 4. **Stability is signal-dependent:** A system may be stable for small inputs and unstable for large inputs.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the input–output mathematical expressions for every inherent non-linearity listed in the KTU 2024 syllabus. **Memorise these — they are the foundation of every numerical problem in Module 1 and the Describing Function derivations in Module 2.**

| # | Non-Linearity | Input–Output Equation $y = N(x)$ | Key Parameters |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Saturation** | $y = M$ if $x > a$ ; $y = (M/a)x$ if $\vert x \vert \le a$ ; $y = -M$ if $x < -a$ | Slope $k = M/a$ |
| 2 | **Dead-Zone** | $y = 0$ if $\vert x \vert \le \Delta$ ; $y = k(x - \Delta\,\text{sgn}\,x)$ if $\vert x \vert > \Delta$ | Half-width $\Delta$ |
| 3 | **Ideal Relay** | $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x)$ | Output $\pm M$ |
| 4 | **Relay with Dead-Zone** | $y = 0$ if $\vert x \vert \le \Delta$ ; $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x)$ if $\vert x \vert > \Delta$ | $\Delta,\,M$ |
| 5 | **Pre-loaded Relay** | $y = M$ if $x \ge a$ ; $y = 0$ if $-a < x < a$ ; $y = -M$ if $x \le -a$ | $a,\,M$ |
| 6 | **Backlash** | Multi-valued; output $\theta_o$ follows input $\theta_i$ with $\pm B/2$ play | Backlash $B$ |
| 7 | **Hysteresis (Relay type)** | $y = +M$ if $x$ rising and crosses $+a$ ; $y = -M$ if $x$ falling and crosses $-a$ | $a,\,M$ |
| 8 | **Coulomb + Viscous Friction** | $T_f = T_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot{\theta}) + b\,\dot{\theta}$ | $T_c,\,b$ |
| 9 | **Static Friction (Stiction)** | $T_f = \begin{cases} T_s & \text{if } \dot\theta = 0 \text{ and } \vert T_{app} \vert < T_s \\ T_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot\theta) & \text{if } \dot\theta \neq 0 \end{cases}$ | $T_s$ (stiction), $T_c$ (Coulomb) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace:** Backlash in helicopter swash-plate linkages causes limit-cycle oscillations ("Piover P" phenomenon) — addressed in fly-by-wire systems with pre-loaded gearing.
> - **Robotics:** Coulomb friction in joint gearboxes creates stick-slip motion at low speeds; compensation is critical for trajectory tracking.
> - **Power Systems:** Magnetic saturation in transformers generates third-harmonic distortion.
> - **Process Control:** Valve dead-zone is the single biggest cause of "hunting" oscillations in PI loops.
> - **Hydraulics:** Hysteresis in servo-valves causes positional inaccuracy of up to 5–8% of full stroke.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are the *exact* style of analytical problems KTU examiners set under Module 1. Work through every line.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Waveform of a Saturation Non-Linearity for Sinusoidal Input

**Problem Setup (typical KTU Part A 3-mark type):**
A saturation element has linear range $\pm a$ and saturation level $\pm M$. A sinusoidal input $x(t) = X\sin\omega t$ is applied. Derive the expression for the output $y(t)$ and identify when clipping begins.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the static characteristic.** The saturation element is described by:

$$y = N(x) = \begin{cases} M, & x > +a \\ \dfrac{M}{a}\,x, & -a \le x \le +a \\ -M, & x < -a \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the sinusoidal input.** Let $x(t) = X\sin\omega t$. The output is:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} M, & X\sin\omega t > +a \\ \dfrac{M}{a}\,X\sin\omega t, & -a \le X\sin\omega t \le +a \\ -M, & X\sin\omega t < -a \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the clipping threshold.** Clipping begins when the input peak equals the linear-range limit:

$$X\sin\omega t = \pm a \;\Longrightarrow\; \sin\omega t = \pm \dfrac{a}{X}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the clipping angles.** Define $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ as the angles where clipping starts and ends in one quarter cycle:

$$\alpha_1 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{X}\right), \qquad \alpha_2 = \pi - \alpha_1$$

**Step 5 — Write the compact piecewise output over one full period $0 \le \omega t \le 2\pi$:**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{M}{a}\,X\sin\omega t, & 0 \le \omega t \le \alpha_1 \\[2mm] M, & \alpha_1 \le \omega t \le \alpha_2 \\[2mm] \dfrac{M}{a}\,X\sin\omega t, & \alpha_2 \le \omega t \le \pi \\[2mm] -\dfrac{M}{a}\,X\sin\omega t, & \pi \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha_1 \\[2mm] -M, & \pi + \alpha_1 \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha_2 \\[2mm] -\dfrac{M}{a}\,X\sin\omega t, & \pi + \alpha_2 \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the linear-only case (no clipping).** If $X \le a$, the entire input lies in the linear region:

$$y(t) = \dfrac{M}{a}\,X\sin\omega t = kX\sin\omega t, \quad k = \dfrac{M}{a}$$

> The element behaves as a **pure linear gain $k$** in this regime.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output of Dead-Zone for Sinusoidal Input

**Setup:** Dead-zone half-width $\Delta$, gain slope $k$ in the active region, input $x(t) = X\sin\omega t$ with $X > \Delta$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Static characteristic of the dead-zone element:**

$$y = N(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert x \vert \le \Delta \\ k\bigl(x - \Delta\,\text{sgn}(x)\bigr), & \vert x \vert > \Delta \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the entry and exit angles** for the active region:

$$\sin\omega t = \dfrac{\Delta}{X} \;\Longrightarrow\; \beta_1 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right)$$

The output is zero when $\vert \sin\omega t \vert \le \Delta/X$.

**Step 3 — Output expression (one period):**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t \le \beta_1 \\[2mm] k\bigl(X\sin\omega t - \Delta\bigr), & \beta_1 \le \omega t \le \pi - \beta_1 \\[2mm] 0, & \pi - \beta_1 \le \omega t \le \pi + \beta_1 \\[2mm] k\bigl(X\sin\omega t + \Delta\bigr), & \pi + \beta_1 \le \omega t \le 2\pi - \beta_1 \\[2mm] 0, & 2\pi - \beta_1 \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Average value over one full cycle (used in describing function):**

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t)\,d(\omega t) = 0$$

> Because the dead-zone is **odd-symmetric**, the DC component is zero.

**Step 5 — Fundamental component amplitude** (foundation for Describing Function, Module 2):

$$B_1 = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) = \dfrac{2kX}{\pi}\!\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right) - \dfrac{\Delta}{X}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right)^{\!2}}\right]$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relating Backlash to Lost-Motion Geometry

**Setup:** A backlash element couples input shaft angle $\theta_i$ to output shaft angle $\theta_o$ with a total play $B$. Both shafts are rotating in the positive direction initially; the input is reversed at some instant.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometric relationship.** The output can only change when the input has moved by an amount equal to the play already taken up:

$$\theta_o(t) = \theta_i(t) - \dfrac{B}{2}\,\text{sgn}(\dot\theta_i) \quad \text{when the gear is in contact}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the four motion phases.** When the input reverses:
- For time $T_1$ after reversal, the input "takes up the slack" — output stays **constant**.
- After the slack is taken, output follows input with the constant offset $-B/2$.

**Step 3 — Quantify the dead time $T_1$** for an input $\theta_i = \Omega t$ (constant $\Omega$):

$$T_1 = \dfrac{B}{\Omega}$$

**Step 4 — Effective reduction in output amplitude** for sinusoidal input $\theta_i = \Theta\sin\omega t$:

$$\Delta\theta_{\text{lost}} = B \quad \text{(per reversal)}$$

> The backlash introduces an **amplitude-dependent phase lag** and a **reduction in effective gain** at low frequencies.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Non-Linearity | Symmetry | Memory? | DC Output for Sinusoid | Effect on Sinusoid |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Saturation | Odd | No | 0 | Clipping at peaks |
| Dead-Zone | Odd | No | 0 | Flat region near zero |
| Ideal Relay | Odd | No | 0 | Square wave |
| Relay + Dead-Zone | Odd | No | 0 | Delayed square wave |
| Backlash | — | **Yes** | 0 | Phase lag, amplitude loss |
| Hysteresis (relay) | — | **Yes** | 0 | Square wave with delay |
| Coulomb Friction | Odd | No | 0 | Dead-zone at zero velocity |
| Stiction | Odd | No | 0 | Lock-up below threshold |

---
