# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** 
*Define inherent non-linearity. How does it differ from intentional non-linearity?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **inherent non-linearity** is a non-linear behaviour that arises *naturally* in a physical system due to inherent physical properties such as material characteristics (magnetic saturation), geometric imperfections (backlash in gears), or friction (Coulomb/stiction). It is **not introduced by the designer**; rather, it is an unavoidable consequence of the system's physical construction.

In contrast, an **intentional non-linearity** is deliberately introduced by the designer to achieve a specific control objective, e.g., using an on-off relay controller to reduce cost or using a non-linear compensator to improve transient response.

*Marking scheme (1+2):* Definition 1 mark; difference 2 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** 
*List any four types of inherent non-linearities with one physical example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Saturation** — magnetic saturation in an iron-core inductor.
2. **Dead-Zone** — threshold voltage below which a diode does not conduct.
3. **Backlash** — play between meshing gear teeth in a gear train.
4. **Hysteresis** — B–H loop in a ferromagnetic core of a transformer.

*(1 mark per correct item, 0.75 for partial list of 4.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, every Part B Module-1 question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Two complete alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the following inherent non-linear elements: (i) Saturation, (ii) Dead-Zone, (iii) Relay with dead-zone and hysteresis. State the input–output relationship for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A saturation non-linearity has a linear range of $\pm 2$ V and an output saturation level of $\pm 10$ V. A sinusoidal input $x(t) = 3 \sin 5t$ is applied. Determine the output $y(t)$ and identify the clipping angles. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks)

| Element | Description | Input–Output Relation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Saturation** | Output grows linearly with input up to a level $\pm a$, beyond which it remains constant at $\pm M$ | $y = (M/a)x$ for $\vert x \vert \le a$ ; $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x)$ for $\vert x \vert > a$ |
| **Dead-Zone** | Output is zero for $\vert x \vert \le \Delta$ and linear with slope $k$ outside | $y = 0$ for $\vert x \vert \le \Delta$ ; $y = k(x - \Delta\,\text{sgn}\,x)$ for $\vert x \vert > \Delta$ |
| **Relay with Dead-Zone & Hysteresis** | Output is $\pm M$ only when input exceeds $\pm a$ with hysteresis gap | $y = +M$ if $x$ rising and $x \ge +a$ ; $y = -M$ if $x$ falling and $x \le -a$ ; $y$ retains previous value otherwise |

*(Neat sketch — 3 marks, equations — 2 marks, physical cause — 2 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $a = 2$ V, $M = 10$ V, $x(t) = 3\sin 5t$.

**Step 1 — Compute the linear gain:**
$$k = \dfrac{M}{a} = \dfrac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ V/V}$$

**Step 2 — Check for clipping:** Since $X = 3 > a = 2$, clipping occurs.

**Step 3 — Compute the clipping angles:**
$$\alpha_1 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{X}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right) = 41.81^\circ = 0.7297 \text{ rad}$$

$$\alpha_2 = \pi - \alpha_1 = 180^\circ - 41.81^\circ = 138.19^\circ = 2.412 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Write the output waveform** (one cycle $0 \le 5t \le 2\pi$):

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 5 \cdot 3\sin 5t = 15\sin 5t, & 0 \le 5t \le 0.7297 \\[2mm] 10, & 0.7297 \le 5t \le 2.412 \\[2mm] 15\sin 5t, & 2.412 \le 5t \le \pi \\[2mm] -15\sin 5t, & \pi \le 5t \le \pi + 0.7297 \\[2mm] -10, & \pi + 0.7297 \le 5t \le \pi + 2.412 \\[2mm] -15\sin 5t, & \pi + 2.412 \le 5t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Sketch:** Plot of $y(t)$ shows the sine wave clipped to $+10$ and $-10$ between angles $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ (and their symmetric counterparts).

*(3 marks for angle calculation, 3 marks for waveform, 1 mark for units.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]*

**(a)** Explain the following inherent non-linearities with neat sketches and mathematical models: (i) Backlash, (ii) Hysteresis, (iii) Coulomb + Viscous friction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dead-zone non-linearity has half-width $\Delta = 0.5$ and slope $k = 4$ in the active region. For an input $x(t) = 2\sin\omega t$, derive the expression for the output $y(t)$ and compute the fundamental component amplitude $B_1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks)

| Element | Description | Input–Output Relation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Backlash** | Multi-valued non-linearity due to lost motion in gears/screws. Output follows input with play $\pm B/2$ on reversal | $\theta_o$ constant during slack uptake; $\theta_o = \theta_i \pm B/2$ when contact made |
| **Hysteresis** | Output depends on past values of input; characteristic forms a closed loop on the $y$–$x$ plane | $y$ jumps between $\pm M$ at different input thresholds on rising vs. falling |
| **Coulomb + Viscous Friction** | Combination of constant opposing torque $T_c$ (Coulomb) and velocity-proportional torque $b\dot\theta$ (viscous) | $T_f = T_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot\theta) + b\dot\theta$ |

*(Sketch 3 + equation 2 + cause 2 = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $\Delta = 0.5$, $k = 4$, $x(t) = 2\sin\omega t$, so $X = 2$.

**Step 1 — Verify the dead-zone is active:** $X = 2 > \Delta = 0.5$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute the entry/exit angle:**

$$\beta_1 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.5}{2}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.25) = 14.48^\circ = 0.2527 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Output waveform (one period):**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t \le 0.2527 \\[2mm] 4\bigl(2\sin\omega t - 0.5\bigr), & 0.2527 \le \omega t \le \pi - 0.2527 \\[2mm] 0, & \pi - 0.2527 \le \omega t \le \pi + 0.2527 \\[2mm] 4\bigl(2\sin\omega t + 0.5\bigr), & \pi + 0.2527 \le \omega t \le 2\pi - 0.2527 \\[2mm] 0, & 2\pi - 0.2527 \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Fundamental amplitude $B_1$:**

$$B_1 = \dfrac{2kX}{\pi}\!\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right) - \dfrac{\Delta}{X}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right)^{\!2}}\right]$$

Substituting $k = 4$, $X = 2$, $\Delta/X = 0.25$:

$$B_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 4 \cdot 2}{\pi}\!\left[1.5708 - 0.2527 - 0.25\sqrt{1-0.0625}\right]$$

$$B_1 = \dfrac{16}{\pi}\!\left[1.5708 - 0.2527 - 0.25(0.9682)\right]$$

$$B_1 = \dfrac{16}{\pi}\!\left[1.5708 - 0.2527 - 0.2421\right] = \dfrac{16}{\pi}(1.0760) = 5.479$$

$$\boxed{B_1 \approx 5.48 \text{ units}}$$

*(Equation 2 marks, substitution 2 marks, numerical result 2 marks, units 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and where marks are lost):**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of the offset in dead-zone:** $y = k(x - \Delta\,\text{sgn}(x))$ — using $y = k(x - \Delta)$ for *all* $x$ is wrong and loses **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing saturation and dead-zone:** Saturation output *saturates*; dead-zone output is *zero in the middle*. Many students draw the curves correctly but write the wrong equation.
> 3. **Backlash is multi-valued:** Plotting backlash as a single curve loses 1 mark. Always show both branches.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\pi/2$ in $B_1$ formula** of the dead-zone — this is the most common error in Part B derivations.
> 5. **Units:** Always quote the unit of $\Delta$, $B$, $X$ — examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for units.
> 6. **Symmetry argument:** Use the odd-symmetry of the characteristic to argue that $A_0 = A_2 = A_4 = \cdots = 0$ — this saves calculation time in the exam.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Inherent Non-Linearity:**

- **Definition:** Non-linearity arising *naturally* from physical construction (not designer-introduced).
- **Two families:** Static (memoryless) and Dynamic (with memory).
- **Static elements to master:** Saturation, Dead-Zone, Ideal Relay, Relay with Dead-Zone, Coulomb Friction, Stiction.
- **Dynamic elements to master:** Backlash, Hysteresis.
- **Symmetry trick:** All odd-symmetric characteristics yield $A_0 = 0$ and only odd harmonics in Fourier series — this simplifies the Describing Function analysis in Module 2.
- **Key angular formulas to remember:**
  - Saturation: $\alpha_1 = \sin^{-1}(a/X)$, $\alpha_2 = \pi - \alpha_1$.
  - Dead-Zone: $\beta_1 = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/X)$.
- **Slope of linear region:** $k = M/a$ for saturation, $k = M/(\text{slope range})$ for dead-zone.
- **Physical cause ↔ Element mapping (high-yield for short answers):**
  - Magnetic core → Saturation & Hysteresis.
  - Gear train → Backlash.
  - Diode/transistor → Dead-Zone.
  - Mechanical slide → Coulomb/Stiction friction.
- **Effect on closed-loop:** Limit cycles, jump resonance, signal-dependent stability, performance degradation.
- **Bridge to Module 2:** Every static non-linearity here will be analysed using the **Describing Function** method; the $B_1$ and $A_1$ formulas derived here are the **direct inputs** to that method.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearisation of Nonlinear System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linearisation of Nonlinear System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linearisation** is the process of approximating a *nonlinear dynamical system* by a *linear time-invariant (LTI) model* that is valid within a small neighbourhood of a chosen **operating point** (also called the **equilibrium point** or **nominal trajectory**). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, linearisation converts a nonlinear state-space description of the form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$ and $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$ into a linearised state-space model $\delta \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\,\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\,\delta\mathbf{u}$, where the matrices $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ are the **Jacobians** of $\mathbf{f}$ evaluated at the operating point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PERUT742 - Module 1):** Linearisation is the gateway concept of nonlinear control. Every classical tool (Bode plots, Nyquist, root locus, pole-placement) is built on a *linear* model — therefore, mastering linearisation is the prerequisite for using all linear design techniques on inherently nonlinear plants.

### 1.2 Why Linearisation is Necessary

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Tool availability** | A vast arsenal of linear design tools (PID tuning, LQR, $H_\infty$, Kalman filter) only applies to LTI systems. |
| **Local insight** | Most physical systems are *weakly nonlinear*; a small-signal model captures dominant behaviour near the operating point. |
| **Initial design** | Servo loops, flight controllers, and robotic arms are first synthesised on the linearised plant, then validated on the full nonlinear simulation. |
| **Stability analysis** | Lyapunov's indirect method reduces the *local stability* of a nonlinear equilibrium to an eigen-value test of the Jacobian $\mathbf{A}$. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Curved Road Approximation

Imagine driving on a **curved mountain road** and wanting to know your *local heading* at the current instant. You do not need the global curvature of the entire route — you only need the **tangent line** at the exact spot where the car sits. The tangent is the *best linear approximation* of the curve *at that point*, and the further you move away, the worse the approximation becomes.

This is precisely what linearisation does to a nonlinear system:
- **Operating point** $\equiv$ the car's current position on the curve.
- **Tangent line** $\equiv$ the linear model $\delta\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\delta\mathbf{u}$.
- **Validity radius** $\equiv$ how far you can drive before the straight-line approximation is no longer trustworthy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Take-away:** Linearisation is *always local*. Never apply a linearised model for large excursions, transients crossing multiple equilibria, or for systems exhibiting hard nonlinearities (relay, saturation, dead-zone, hysteresis).

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** — standard gravitational acceleration (used in pendulum, levitation, and aerospace examples).
- **Equilibrium (operating) point** — denoted $(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$, where $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Small-signal (perturbation) variables** — $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*$ and $\delta \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}^*$.
- **Region of validity** — typically $\vert \delta \mathbf{x} \vert \ll 1$ (in normalised units) and $\vert \delta \mathbf{u} \vert \ll 1$.
- **Jacobian matrix** — the matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Nonlinear State-Space Model (Starting Point)

A general *autonomous* or *control-affine* nonlinear system in state-space form is written as

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}\bigl(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t)\bigr), \qquad \mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{h}\bigl(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t)\bigr)
$$

where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the state vector, $\mathbf{u} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the input vector, $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the output vector, and $\mathbf{f}(\cdot)$ and $\mathbf{h}(\cdot)$ are *at least* $C^1$ (continuously differentiable) functions.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Linearisation

1. **Identify the operating (equilibrium) point** $(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$ such that $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{h}^* = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$ is the corresponding output.
2. **Introduce perturbation variables** $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*$ and $\delta \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}^*$, so that the new origin corresponds to the equilibrium.
3. **Apply the multivariable Taylor series expansion** of $\mathbf{f}$ about $(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$, retaining only the first-order (linear) terms.
4. **Recognise the Jacobian matrices** $\mathbf{A} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\Big|_{*}$ and $\mathbf{B} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\Big|_{*}$ (and similarly $\mathbf{C}$, $\mathbf{D}$ from $\mathbf{h}$).
5. **Discard higher-order terms** $\mathcal{O}(\Vert \delta\mathbf{x}\Vert^2)$ as negligible for small signals.
6. **Obtain the linearised model** $\delta \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\,\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\,\delta\mathbf{u}$, $\delta \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\,\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\,\delta\mathbf{u}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - *Step 1* is non-negotiable: linearisation is *about* a specific point. Different equilibria give different linear models.
> - *Step 2* shifts the coordinate origin so the equilibrium sits at the origin, simplifying analysis.
> - *Step 3* is the mathematical engine — Taylor expansion is the formal link between a smooth nonlinear map and its best local linear approximation.
> - *Step 5* is justified only when the perturbations are *small* in magnitude; outside this zone the higher-order terms dominate and the linear model is meaningless.

### 2.3 The Multivariable Taylor Expansion (Key Identity)

For a scalar function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n+m} \to \mathbb{R}$ expanded about $(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg|_{*}\delta \mathbf{x} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\bigg|_{*}\delta \mathbf{u} + \mathcal{H.O.T.}
$$

For a vector function $\mathbf{f}$, this applies **component-wise**, producing the Jacobian matrices.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Dimension |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Equilibrium condition | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 2 | $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*$ | State perturbation | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 3 | $\delta \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}^*$ | Input perturbation | $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| 4 | $\mathbf{A} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\Big|_{*} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_n} \\[4pt] \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f_n}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_n}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix}_{*}$ | State Jacobian | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{B} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\Big|_{*}$ | Input Jacobian | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| 6 | $\mathbf{C} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\Big|_{*}$ | Output Jacobian | $\mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ |
| 7 | $\mathbf{D} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\Big|_{*}$ | Feed-through Jacobian | $\mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ |
| 8 | $\delta \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\,\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\,\delta\mathbf{u}$ | **Linearised state equation** | — |
| 9 | $\delta \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\,\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\,\delta\mathbf{u}$ | **Linearised output equation** | — |
| 10 | $e^{\mathbf{A}t}$ | State-transition matrix of linearised system | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| 11 | $\sigma(\mathbf{A}) = \{ \lambda \in \mathbb{C} \mid \det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0\}$ | Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ — govern local stability | — |
| 12 | $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$, $\cos(\theta) \approx 1$ | Small-angle linearisations (very common in pendulums & robotics) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise the *four Jacobians* $\mathbf{A, B, C, D}$ and the equilibrium condition verbatim. They are worth easy marks in Part A and form the foundation of every Part B linearisation problem.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Linearisation |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Linearisation of aircraft dynamics about a trim condition (e.g., level cruise) to design autopilot gains. |
| **Robotics** | Linearising robot manipulator dynamics about a desired joint configuration for computed-torque / feedback linearisation precursors. |
| **Power Electronics** | Small-signal model of boost / buck converters about a duty-cycle operating point for controller design. |
| **Process Control** | Linearising chemical reactor models about a steady state to apply PID / MPC. |
| **Biomedical** | Linearising pupil-reflex, insulin-glucose, and cardiac models for clinical control design. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 General Derivation — From Nonlinear to Linear State-Space

**Starting point:** A smooth nonlinear system

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}), \qquad \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})
$$

**Step 1 — Define the equilibrium.** An equilibrium $(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$ satisfies

$$
\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*) = \mathbf{0}
$$

When the system rests at this point, $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}$ and the state does not change.

**Step 2 — Define perturbation variables.** Let

$$
\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*, \qquad \delta \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}^*
$$

so that the *new* origin in perturbation coordinates is precisely the equilibrium.

**Step 3 — Express $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ in terms of perturbations.** Since the equilibrium is time-invariant,

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \dot{\delta \mathbf{x}} \quad \text{(because } \mathbf{x}^* = \text{const.)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the multivariable Taylor series expansion** of $\mathbf{f}$ about $(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$, retaining only first-order terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) &= \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*) + \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg|_{*} \delta \mathbf{x} + \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\bigg|_{*} \delta \mathbf{u} + \underbrace{\mathcal{H.O.T.}}_{\text{higher-order terms in } \delta \mathbf{x}, \delta \mathbf{u}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the equilibrium condition** $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\delta \mathbf{x}} &= \mathbf{0} + \mathbf{A}\,\delta \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\,\delta \mathbf{u} + \mathcal{H.O.T.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Drop the higher-order terms** (valid for $\Vert \delta \mathbf{x} \Vert$ and $\Vert \delta \mathbf{u} \Vert$ sufficiently small):

$$
\boxed{\delta \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\,\delta \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\,\delta \mathbf{u}}
$$

Applying the identical procedure to the output map $\mathbf{h}$ yields

$$
\boxed{\delta \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\,\delta \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\,\delta \mathbf{u}}
$$

This is the **linearised state-space model** about the equilibrium $(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Simple Pendulum (Classical KTU Problem)

**System description:** A rigid pendulum of length $L$ and bob mass $m$, pivoted at the origin, under gravity $g$ and a horizontal control force $u(t)$ applied at the bob. Viscous friction at the pivot is modelled as torque $-\,b\,\dot{\theta}$.

**Step 1 — Derive the nonlinear equations of motion** using Newton's second law for rotation:

$$
m L^2 \ddot{\theta} + b\,\dot{\theta} + m g L \sin\theta = L\,u \cos\theta
$$

Dividing throughout by $mL^2$ and choosing state variables $x_1 = \theta$, $x_2 = \dot{\theta}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= x_2 \\[4pt]
\dot{x}_2 &= -\frac{g}{L}\sin(x_1) - \frac{b}{mL^{2}}\,x_2 + \frac{1}{mL}\,u\,\cos(x_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the equilibrium point.** Set $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$ with constant $u = u^*$. The choice $x_1^* = 0$ (pendulum hanging straight down) gives $\sin(0) = 0$ and $\cos(0) = 1$, reducing the equilibrium condition to

$$
0 + 0 + \frac{1}{mL}\,u^* = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u^* = 0
$$

Hence the chosen operating point is

$$
\boxed{x_1^* = 0, \quad x_2^* = 0, \quad u^* = 0}
$$

**Step 3 — Define perturbation variables:**

$$
\delta x_1 = x_1, \quad \delta x_2 = x_2, \quad \delta u = u
$$

(They coincide with the original variables because the equilibrium is at the origin.)

**Step 4 — Compute the Jacobian matrices.** Let $f_1(x_1, x_2, u) = x_2$ and $f_2(x_1, x_2, u) = -\dfrac{g}{L}\sin x_1 - \dfrac{b}{mL^{2}}\,x_2 + \dfrac{1}{mL}\,u\cos x_1$.

Computing partial derivatives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} &= 0, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial u} = 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} &= -\frac{g}{L}\cos(x_1), \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} = -\frac{b}{mL^{2}}, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial u} = \frac{1}{mL}\cos(x_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluating at $(x_1^*, x_2^*, u^*) = (0, 0, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\dfrac{g}{L} & -\dfrac{b}{mL^{2}} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{mL} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the linearised state equation:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{bmatrix} \delta \dot{x}_1 \\ \delta \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\dfrac{g}{L} & -\dfrac{b}{mL^{2}} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \delta x_1 \\ \delta x_2 \end{bmatrix}
+ \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{mL} \end{bmatrix} \delta u
}
$$

This is the famous **inverted-pendulum-flavoured linear model** that students encounter in classical control courses. The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ are

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = -\frac{b}{2mL^{2}} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{b}{2mL^{2}}\right)^{2} - \frac{g}{L}}
$$

confirming the oscillatory (under-damped) response typical of a small-angle pendulum.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — A Two-State Nonlinear System (Comprehensive Template)

Consider a system described by

$$
\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_2^{2} + u, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = \sin(x_1) - 2 x_2
$$

**Step 1 — Find the equilibrium with $u^* = 0$.** Setting derivatives to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= -x_1^* + (x_2^*)^{2} \\
0 &= \sin(x_1^*) - 2 x_2^*
\end{aligned}
$$

From the first equation, $x_1^* = (x_2^*)^{2}$. Substituting in the second:

$$
\sin\!\big((x_2^*)^{2}\big) = 2 x_2^*
$$

This transcendental equation has the trivial solution $x_2^* = 0$ (since $\sin(0) = 0$). Then $x_1^* = 0$. Hence the equilibrium is

$$
\boxed{x_1^* = 0, \quad x_2^* = 0, \quad u^* = 0}
$$

**Step 2 — Define perturbations** $\delta x_1$, $\delta x_2$, $\delta u$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Jacobians.** Let $f_1 = -x_1 + x_2^{2} + u$ and $f_2 = \sin(x_1) - 2 x_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} &= -1, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} = 2 x_2, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial u} = 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} &= \cos(x_1), \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} = -2, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial u} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluating at the origin:

$$
\boxed{
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
}
$$

**Step 4 — Linearised state equation:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{bmatrix} \delta \dot{x}_1 \\ \delta \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \delta x_1 \\ \delta x_2 \end{bmatrix}
+ \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \delta u
}
$$

**Step 5 — Local stability check.** Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$:

$$
\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = (\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = -1, \; \lambda_2 = -2
$$

Both eigenvalues are negative real, so the equilibrium is **locally asymptotically stable** (Lyapunov's indirect method).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Nonlinearities and Their Linearised Forms

| Nonlinearity | Exact Expression | Linearised Form (small $\delta$) | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sine | $\sin(x)$ | $x$ | Pendulums, AC circuits |
| Cosine | $\cos(x)$ | $1$ | Pendulum coupling, modulators |
| Exponential | $e^{x}$ | $1 + x$ | RC circuits, statistical mechanics |
| Logarithm | $\ln(1 + x)$ | $x$ | Compressors, sensor response |
| Square / product | $x^{2}$ or $x_1 x_2$ | $x^*$ approximation (e.g. $2x^* \delta x$) | Aerodynamic drag, mixing |
| Square root | $\sqrt{x}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x^*}} \delta x$ | Fluid dynamics, optics |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process-Flow of Linearisation (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Nonlinear Plant"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Equilibrium"]
    B --> C{"fx Star u Star equals 0?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Re-solve or choose another OP"]
    D --> B
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Step 2: Define Perturbation Variables delta x and delta u"]
    E --> F["Step 3: Taylor Series Expansion of f and h"]
    F --> G["Step 4: Compute Jacobian Matrices A B C D"]
    G --> H["Step 5: Drop Higher Order Terms"]
    H --> I["Step 6: Assemble Linearised State Space Model"]
    I --> J["Step 7: Verify Validity Region via Small Signal Check"]
    J --> K["END: LTI Model delta x dot equals A delta x plus B delta u"]
    K --> L["Output: Eigenvalue Analysis and Linear Controller Design"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop with Linearised Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Reference r of t"] --> S["Summing Junction"]
    S --> C["Linear Controller K of s"]
    C --> P["Linearised Plant G linear of s = C inv sI minus A inv B"]
    P --> FB["Feedback Path H of s = C"]
    FB --> S
    P --> Y["Output y of t equals delta y plus y Star"]
    Y --> NM["Add Back Nominal Operating Point y Star"]
    NM --> OUT["True Plant Output Y of t"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping — Nonlinear Surface vs Tangent Plane

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Nonlinear Surface y equals f of x"] --> B["Choose Operating Point x Star and y Star"]
    B --> C["Construct Tangent Plane at x Star"]
    C --> D["Local Linear Model y approx f of x Star plus df dx at x Star times delta x"]
    D --> E{"delta x sufficiently small?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Linearisation is accurate"]
    E -- "No" --> G["Higher order terms dominate, model invalid"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Perturbation Variables in State-Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["True State x of t"] --> ADD1["Subtraction"]
    XS["Equilibrium x Star"] --> ADD1
    ADD1 --> DX["Perturbation delta x of t"]
    DX --> LIN["Linearised Plant delta x dot equals A delta x plus B delta u"]
    U["True Input u of t"] --> ADD2["Subtraction"]
    US["Equilibrium u Star"] --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> DU["Perturbation delta u of t"]
    DU --> LIN
    LIN --> DY["Perturbation Output delta y of t"]
    DY --> ADD3["Addition"]
    YS["Equilibrium Output y Star"] --> ADD3
    ADD3 --> YT["True Output y of t"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams:** The `flowchart TD` diagrams show the *algorithmic procedure* of linearisation. The `flowchart LR` diagrams show the *physical signal flow* — how the true nonlinear signals relate to the perturbation variables that drive the linearised model.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define linearisation of a nonlinear system. Why is it carried out only about an operating point?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Linearisation is the process of approximating a nonlinear dynamical system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$ by a linear time-invariant model $\delta\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\,\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\,\delta\mathbf{u}$ valid in a small neighbourhood of an **operating point** $(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$. (1.5 marks)

It is carried out only about an operating point because the Taylor-series approximation that produces the linear model is *local*: the higher-order terms that are discarded are negligible **only** when the perturbations $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*$ and $\delta \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}^*$ are sufficiently small. Far from the operating point, the higher-order terms dominate and the linear model loses validity. (1.5 marks)

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the Jacobian matrices $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ used in linearisation. What do they physically represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{A} = \left.\frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{*}, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \left.\frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{u}}\right|_{*}
$$

$\mathbf{A}$ is the **state Jacobian** — an $n \times n$ matrix whose $(i, j)$-th entry is the sensitivity of the $i$-th state's rate of change to the $j$-th state, evaluated at the operating point. (1.5 marks)

$\mathbf{B}$ is the **input Jacobian** — an $n \times m$ matrix whose $(i, j)$-th entry is the sensitivity of the $i$-th state's rate of change to the $j$-th input, evaluated at the operating point. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Failing to *verify* the chosen operating point actually satisfies $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*) = \mathbf{0}$. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting to substitute numerical values into the Jacobians *after* computing the partial derivatives. Many students leave $\cos(x_1)$ unevaluated. Loss: 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Confusing $\mathbf{A} = \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x}$ (rows are state derivatives) with the *transpose*. Loss: 2 marks.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Not mentioning that higher-order terms are *discarded under the small-signal assumption*. Loss: 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake #5:** Skipping the final linearised state equation in matrix form. Loss: 2 marks.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Pendulum Linearisation**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the nonlinear state-space equations for a simple pendulum of length $L$ and bob mass $m$ under gravity $g$ and a horizontal control force $u$ at the bob. Clearly identify the state variables. (7 marks)

**(b)** Find the equilibrium point and linearise the system about it. Hence obtain the state-space matrices $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ in numerical form for $L = 1$ m, $m = 1$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$, $b = 0.5$ N·m·s. Comment on the local stability of the equilibrium. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Free body diagram and torque equation.** The pendulum bob is acted upon by gravity (vertical), the horizontal control force $u$, and viscous damping torque $-b\dot{\theta}$ at the pivot. Taking moments about the pivot:

$$
m L^2 \ddot{\theta} = -\,b\,\dot{\theta} - m g L \sin\theta + L\,u\,\cos\theta
$$

The term $L\,u\,\cos\theta$ is the moment arm of the horizontal force about the pivot.

**Step 2 — Rearrange.** Dividing by $mL^2$:

$$
\ddot{\theta} = -\frac{g}{L}\sin\theta - \frac{b}{mL^{2}}\,\dot{\theta} + \frac{1}{mL}\,u\,\cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Choose state variables.** Let $x_1 = \theta$ and $x_2 = \dot{\theta}$. (1 mark for clear identification)

**Step 4 — Write the first-order state equations.** (2 marks for the correct nonlinear form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= x_2 \\[4pt]
\dot{x}_2 &= -\frac{g}{L}\sin(x_1) - \frac{b}{mL^{2}}\,x_2 + \frac{1}{mL}\,u\,\cos(x_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Vector form.** (1 mark)

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, u) = \begin{bmatrix} f_1 \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\[4pt] -\dfrac{g}{L}\sin(x_1) - \dfrac{b}{mL^{2}}\,x_2 + \dfrac{1}{mL}\,u\,\cos(x_1) \end{bmatrix}
$$

(Remaining 1 mark for the clean derivation flow and mentioning that the system is non-autonomous in $u$.)

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Find the equilibrium.** Set $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
x_2^* = 0, \qquad -\frac{g}{L}\sin(x_1^*) + \frac{1}{mL}\,u^*\cos(x_1^*) = 0
$$

Choose $x_1^* = 0$ (hanging pendulum) and $u^* = 0$. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Define perturbations** $\delta x_1$, $\delta x_2$, $\delta u$. (0.5 mark)

**Step 3 — Compute partial derivatives of $f_1$ and $f_2$.** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} &= 0, & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} &= 1, & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial u} &= 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} &= -\frac{g}{L}\cos(x_1), & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} &= -\frac{b}{mL^{2}}, & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial u} &= \frac{1}{mL}\cos(x_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at the origin.** (1 mark)

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\dfrac{g}{L} & -\dfrac{b}{mL^{2}} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{mL} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values** $L = 1$ m, $m = 1$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$, $b = 0.5$ N·m·s: (1 mark)

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -9.81 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Local stability.** Eigenvalues: (1 mark)

$$
\lambda = \frac{-0.5 \pm \sqrt{0.25 - 4(-9.81)}}{2} = -0.25 \pm j\,3.128
$$

The eigenvalues have negative real parts, so the equilibrium is **locally asymptotically stable** with damped oscillations of natural frequency $\omega_n \approx 3.13$ rad/s and damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.08$. (0.5 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Generic Two-State Nonlinear System**

**(a)** Consider the nonlinear system

$$
\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_1 x_2 + u, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = x_1 - x_2^{2}
$$

Find all its equilibrium points when $u = 0$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Linearise the system about the equilibrium at the origin and obtain $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$. Determine whether the origin is locally stable, unstable, or a saddle. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Set the state derivatives to zero with $u = 0$:**

$$
-x_1 + x_1 x_2 = 0, \qquad x_1 - x_2^{2} = 0
$$

(1 mark for setting up)

**Step 2 — Factor the first equation:**

$$
x_1(x_2 - 1) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 0 \;\text{or}\; x_2 = 1
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Case 1: $x_1 = 0$.** Substituting in the second equation: $0 - x_2^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$. Equilibrium $E_1 = (0, 0)$. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Case 2: $x_2 = 1$.** Substituting in the second equation: $x_1 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 1$. Equilibrium $E_2 = (1, 1)$. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Verify both equilibria.** Plug back into the original equations:

- For $E_1$: $\dot{x}_1 = -0 + 0\cdot 0 + 0 = 0$ ✓ ; $\dot{x}_2 = 0 - 0 = 0$ ✓.
- For $E_2$: $\dot{x}_1 = -1 + 1\cdot 1 + 0 = 0$ ✓ ; $\dot{x}_2 = 1 - 1 = 0$ ✓.

(1 mark for verification)

**Step 6 — Concluding statement** listing the two equilibria. (1 mark for clarity and completeness)

$$
\boxed{E_1 = (0, 0), \qquad E_2 = (1, 1)}
$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute Jacobians.** Let $f_1 = -x_1 + x_1 x_2 + u$, $f_2 = x_1 - x_2^{2}$. (1 mark)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} &= -1 + x_2, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} = x_1, \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial u} = 1 \\[6pt]
\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} &= 1, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} = -2 x_2, \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial u} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at $(0, 0, 0)$.** (1 mark)

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Linearised state equation.** (1 mark)

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \delta \dot{x}_1 \\ \delta \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \delta x_1 \\ \delta x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \delta u
$$

**Step 4 — Characteristic polynomial.** (1 mark)

$$
\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda + 1 & 0 \\ -1 & \lambda \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda + 1) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Eigenvalues.** $\lambda_1 = 0$, $\lambda_2 = -1$. (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Stability classification.** The eigenvalue at $\lambda_1 = 0$ lies on the imaginary axis (not strictly in the left-half plane). By Lyapunov's indirect method, **the origin is not asymptotically stable**; it is a **non-hyperbolic equilibrium** (centre / marginal case) and higher-order terms decide its true stability. The system exhibits a *saddle-like* behaviour in the $\delta x_1$ direction (one stable, one neutrally stable mode). (2 marks — full classification with reasoning)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Linearisation of Nonlinear Systems**

- **Definition (verbatim for KTU):** Linearisation is the local approximation of a nonlinear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$ by an LTI model $\delta\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\,\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\,\delta\mathbf{u}$ using the first-order Taylor expansion about an equilibrium point.
- **Equilibrium condition:** $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ — *must verify*, never assume.
- **Perturbation variables:** $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*$ and $\delta \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}^*$ — small by construction.
- **Four Jacobians:** $\mathbf{A} = \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x}$, $\mathbf{B} = \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{C} = \partial \mathbf{h}/\partial \mathbf{x}$, $\mathbf{D} = \partial \mathbf{h}/\partial \mathbf{u}$ — all evaluated at $(\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{u}^*)$.
- **Taylor series retention:** Only **first-order** terms are kept; second and higher derivatives are dropped under the *small-signal assumption*.
- **Common small-angle substitutions:** $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$ (very frequent in pendulum, satellite, and robotic-arm problems).
- **Stability link:** Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ determine *local* stability — all in LHP ⇒ asymptotically stable; any in RHP ⇒ unstable; on imaginary axis ⇒ inconclusive (non-hyperbolic).
- **Validity is local:** Never use a linearised model for large transients, multi-equilibrium jumps, or hard nonlinearities.
- **State-space output form:** $\delta \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\,\delta\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\,\delta\mathbf{u}$ — do not forget the $\mathbf{D}$ term in exam answers if the system has direct feed-through.
- **Procedure order (memorise):** (1) Find equilibrium → (2) Define perturbations → (3) Taylor expand → (4) Compute Jacobians → (5) Evaluate at OP → (6) Drop HOT → (7) Write linear model.
- **Engineering use:** Autopilot design (aerospace), boost-converter small-signal analysis (power electronics), computed-torque precursor (robotics), and reactor control (process industry).
- **Standard test values:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $g = 32.2$ ft/s² (imperial), $L$, $m$, $b$ are problem-specific.
- **Past-year KTU tag pattern:** Always present derivations with the equilibrium *and* the final $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B})$ matrices — these are the key deliverables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous and Non-Autonomous Systems - Modelling of Simple Pendulum - Mass Spring System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Autonomous and Non-Autonomous Systems - Modelling of Simple Pendulum - Mass Spring System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Autonomous and Non-Autonomous Systems — Modelling of the Simple Pendulum & Mass–Spring System

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Autonomous Systems (Formal KTU 2024 Definition)

An **autonomous system** is a dynamical system whose governing differential equation does **not explicitly contain the independent variable (time, t)** as an input. In other words, the time-dependence of the right-hand side is purely through the state variables themselves.

A general *n*-dimensional autonomous system is represented as:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t))$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The vector field $\mathbf{f}(\cdot)$ has no explicit $t$ term. If we shift the time origin ($t \rightarrow t + t_0$), the trajectory in the state-space remains **identical** — this is the signature property of an autonomous system. **Equilibrium points of autonomous systems are time-invariant.**

### 1.2 Non-Autonomous Systems (Formal KTU 2024 Definition)

A **non-autonomous system** explicitly contains the independent variable (time) on the right-hand side, either directly or through an external forcing function $u(t)$:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t), t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Pearl:** In the KTU board exam, if a question shows $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, t)$ or contains $A\cos(\omega t)$ terms on the right side, it is **strictly non-autonomous**, even if the time-dependence appears only in the forcing input.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

| System Type | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous** | A ball rolling on a fixed, stationary hill. The shape of the hill never changes — only the ball's position on it evolves. |
| **Non-Autonomous** | A ball rolling on a hill whose shape is being **reshaped by wind** every second. The "rules of the game" themselves change with time. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Translation:** Think of autonomous as a *self-driving car on a fixed map* and non-autonomous as a *self-driving car on a map that is being redrawn every minute*. The car's behavior depends on the changing map (time) in the second case.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- Mass of pendulum bob: $m$ in **kg**
- Length of pendulum rod: $L$ in **m**
- Viscous damping coefficient: $b$ in **N·s/m**
- Spring stiffness: $k$ in **N/m**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Structure — The Core Distinction

### 2.1.1 Autonomous Form

For an *n*th-order autonomous ODE, the standard canonical form is:

$$x^{(n)}(t) = F(x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}, \ldots, x^{(n-1)})$$

Notice that the right-hand side $F(\cdot)$ has **no $t$** argument. Every term depends only on the state $x$ and its derivatives.

### 2.1.2 Non-Autonomous Form

$$x^{(n)}(t) = F(x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}, \ldots, x^{(n-1)}, t)$$

The explicit appearance of $t$ (or a forcing function of $t$) is the mathematical signature.

### 2.2 Why This Distinction Matters in Non-Linear Control

- **Equilibrium analysis** is meaningful **only** for autonomous systems. A non-autonomous system technically has *no fixed equilibrium points* — only quasi-steady-state solutions.
- **Phase portraits** are time-invariant **only** for autonomous systems.
- **Lyapunov stability theory** is most cleanly applied to autonomous systems first.
- Most **real-world control systems are non-autonomous** (reference tracking, disturbance rejection). However, by *augmenting the state* with $\dot{x}_{n+1} = 1$, we can convert a non-autonomous system into an equivalent autonomous one. This is a **favourite KTU board exam question**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| System | Equation of Motion (Non-Linear) | State-Space Form (Autonomous) | Equilibrium Points |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Pendulum (Undamped) | $\ddot{\theta} + \dfrac{g}{L}\sin\theta = 0$ | $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -\dfrac{g}{L}\sin x_1$ | $x_1 = n\pi$, $x_2 = 0$ |
| Simple Pendulum (Damped) | $\ddot{\theta} + \dfrac{b}{m}\dot{\theta} + \dfrac{g}{L}\sin\theta = 0$ | $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -\dfrac{b}{m}x_2 - \dfrac{g}{L}\sin x_1$ | $x_1 = n\pi$, $x_2 = 0$ |
| Forced Pendulum (Non-Auto.) | $\ddot{\theta} + \dfrac{b}{m}\dot{\theta} + \dfrac{g}{L}\sin\theta = A\cos(\omega t)$ | Requires time-augmented state | Time-varying quasi-equilibria |
| Mass-Spring (Linear) | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = 0$ | $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -\dfrac{k}{m}x_1 - \dfrac{b}{m}x_2$ | $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 0$ |
| Mass-Spring (Non-Linear) | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + k_1 x + k_2 x^2 + k_3 x^3 = 0$ | $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -\dfrac{k_1}{m}x_1 - \dfrac{k_2}{m}x_1^2 - \dfrac{k_3}{m}x_1^3 - \dfrac{b}{m}x_2$ | $x_1 = 0$ (only one) |
| Forced Mass-Spring (Non-Auto.) | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + k_1 x + k_2 x^2 = F(t)$ | Non-autonomous form | None (quasi-equilibria only) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The simple pendulum models **robot arm joints, offshore crane sway, and gantry crane oscillations**. The non-linear mass-spring models **vehicle suspension systems, vibration isolators, and seismic dampers** under large deflections where linear Hooke's law fails.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Modelling of the Simple Pendulum (Undamped, Autonomous)

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Setup

Consider a rigid massless rod of length $L$ with a point mass $m$ at the end, free to swing in a vertical plane. Let $\theta$ be the angular displacement from the vertical (downward) equilibrium.

The two forces acting on the bob are:
- Gravity $mg$ acting vertically downward.
- Tension $T$ acting along the rod.

The component of gravity **tangential** to the arc of motion (restoring force) is $mg\sin\theta$, acting opposite to the direction of increasing $\theta$.

### 3.1.2 Applying Newton's Second Law (Rotational Form)

The arc-length displacement is $s = L\theta$, so the tangential acceleration is $L\ddot{\theta}$.

Summing forces along the tangential direction (taking the direction of increasing $\theta$ as positive):

$$mL\ddot{\theta} = -mg\sin\theta$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation Chain

$$\begin{aligned}
mL\ddot{\theta} &= -mg\sin\theta \\
\ddot{\theta} &= -\frac{mg}{mL}\sin\theta \\
\ddot{\theta} &= -\frac{g}{L}\sin\theta
\end{aligned}$$

The final non-linear autonomous ODE is:

$$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g}{L}\sin\theta = 0$$

### 3.1.4 Linearisation (Small-Angle Approximation)

Using the Taylor expansion $\sin\theta = \theta - \dfrac{\theta^3}{3!} + \dfrac{\theta^5}{5!} - \ldots$, for small angles ($\vert\theta\vert \ll 1$ rad):

$$\sin\theta \approx \theta$$

The linearised autonomous equation becomes:

$$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g}{L}\theta = 0$$

This is the classical simple harmonic motion with natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/L}$.

### 3.1.5 State-Space Representation (Autonomous)

Define state variables: $x_1 = \theta$, $x_2 = \dot{\theta}$.

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= x_2 \\
\dot{x}_2 &= -\frac{g}{L}\sin(x_1)
\end{aligned}$$

In vector form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ -\dfrac{g}{L}\sin(x_1) \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.6 Equilibrium Point Analysis

Setting $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$x_2 = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sin(x_1) = 0 \implies x_1 = n\pi, \; n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

- $n$ **even** ($\theta = 0, \pm 2\pi, \ldots$): **Stable** equilibrium (pendulum hanging down).
- $n$ **odd** ($\theta = \pm\pi, \pm 3\pi, \ldots$): **Unstable** equilibrium (pendulum balanced upright).

## 3.2 Modelling of the Damped, Forced Pendulum (Non-Autonomous)

Adding viscous damping torque $-b\dot{\theta}$ and an external periodic forcing torque $\tau(t) = \tau_0 \cos(\omega t)$:

$$mL^2\ddot{\theta} + bL\dot{\theta} + mgL\sin\theta = \tau_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

Dividing through by $mL^2$:

$$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{b}{mL}\dot{\theta} + \frac{g}{L}\sin\theta = \frac{\tau_0}{mL^2}\cos(\omega t)$$

The right-hand side explicitly contains $t$, making this **non-autonomous**.

## 3.3 Modelling of the Non-Linear Mass-Spring System (Autonomous)

### 3.3.1 System Description

A mass $m$ slides on a frictionless horizontal surface, attached to a wall by a non-linear spring whose restoring force is:

$$F_{\text{spring}} = k_1 x + k_2 x^2 + k_3 x^3$$

The $k_2 x^2$ term is the **asymmetric quadratic non-linearity** (different stiffness in compression vs. extension). The $k_3 x^3$ term is the **symmetric cubic non-linearity** (Duffing-type).

### 3.3.2 Applying Newton's Second Law

With a damping force $-b\dot{x}$ opposing motion:

$$m\ddot{x} = -b\dot{x} - k_1 x - k_2 x^2 - k_3 x^3$$

### 3.3.3 Rearranging to Standard Form

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + k_1 x + k_2 x^2 + k_3 x^3 = 0$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{b}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k_1}{m}x + \frac{k_2}{m}x^2 + \frac{k_3}{m}x^3 = 0$$

### 3.3.4 State-Space Form

Define $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = \dot{x}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= x_2 \\
\dot{x}_2 &= -\frac{k_1}{m}x_1 - \frac{k_2}{m}x_1^2 - \frac{k_3}{m}x_1^3 - \frac{b}{m}x_2
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.5 Equilibrium Points

Setting $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$x_2 = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad k_1 x_1 + k_2 x_1^2 + k_3 x_1^3 = 0$$

Factoring $x_1$:

$$x_1(k_3 x_1^2 + k_2 x_1 + k_1) = 0$$

This gives one trivial equilibrium $x_1 = 0$ and two non-trivial equilibria (real or complex) depending on the discriminant $k_2^2 - 4k_1 k_3$.

## 3.4 Forced Mass-Spring (Non-Autonomous)

With an external time-varying force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + k_1 x + k_2 x^2 + k_3 x^3 = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

This is **non-autonomous** because of the explicit $t$ on the RHS.

## 3.5 Augmentation Trick: Converting Non-Autonomous to Autonomous

For the forced pendulum:

$$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{b}{mL}\dot{\theta} + \frac{g}{L}\sin\theta = \frac{\tau_0}{mL^2}\cos(\omega t)$$

Define an augmented state $x_3 = \omega t$ (a new state variable), so $\dot{x}_3 = \omega$ (constant). Now the system is autonomous in the augmented 3-D state $(x_1, x_2, x_3)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= x_2 \\
\dot{x}_2 &= -\frac{b}{mL}x_2 - \frac{g}{L}\sin(x_1) + \frac{\tau_0}{mL^2}\cos(x_3) \\
\dot{x}_3 &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This augmentation is a **guaranteed KTU exam question** — expect at least 7 marks on it.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Modelling Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete modelling pipeline** from physical system to non-linear state-space, with classification into autonomous vs. non-autonomous at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical System] --> B[Identify Forces and Torques]
    B --> C[Free Body Diagram FBD]
    C --> D[Apply Newton Second Law]
    D --> E{External Time Dependent Forcing}
    E -->|No| F[Non Linear Autonomous ODE]
    E -->|Yes| G[Non Linear Non Autonomous ODE]
    F --> H[Define State Variables x1 x2]
    G --> I[Augment State with time]
    I --> H
    H --> J[State Space Form xdot equals f of x]
    J --> K[Find Equilibrium Points]
    K --> L[Stability Analysis in Module 2]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Simple Pendulum Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Fixed Pivot] -->|Rod Length L| P1[Mass m]
    P1 -->|Restoring Force mg sin theta| P0
    P2[Vertical Downward Reference] -.->|Angle theta| P1
    P3[External Torque tau cos omega t] -.-> P1
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Non-Linear Mass-Spring Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Fixed Wall] -->|Spring k1 x plus k2 x squared plus k3 x cubed| M1[Mass m]
    M1 -->|Viscous Damper b xdot| D1[Damping Element]
    M2[External Force F of t] -.->|Acts on| M1
    M1 -->|Displacement x and velocity xdot| S1[State Vector x1 x2]
```

## 4.4 Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start with ODE] --> S1{Does RHS contain t explicitly}
    S1 -->|No| S2[AUTONOMOUS System]
    S1 -->|Yes| S3[NON AUTONOMOUS System]
    S2 --> S4[Equilibria are time invariant]
    S2 --> S5[Phase portrait is closed orbits or fixed]
    S3 --> S6[Equilibria are quasi steady or non existent]
    S3 --> S7[Phase portrait depends on initial time t0]
    S4 --> S8[Apply Standard Lyapunov Theory]
    S6 --> S9[Apply Time Varying Stability Methods]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between autonomous and non-autonomous systems. Give one example of each from a mechanical system.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Autonomous | Non-Autonomous |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical form | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, t)$ |
| Time on RHS | **Absent** | **Explicitly present** |
| Equilibrium points | **Time-invariant** (true equilibria) | **Time-varying** (quasi-equilibria) |
| Effect of initial time $t_0$ | Trajectory is **unchanged** | Trajectory **depends on $t_0$** |

- **Autonomous example:** $\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g}{L}\sin\theta = 0$ (undamped pendulum).
- **Non-autonomous example:** $\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g}{L}\sin\theta = A\cos(\omega t)$ (forced pendulum).

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the equilibrium points of the undamped simple pendulum $\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g}{L}\sin\theta = 0$ and classify them.**

**Model Answer:**

Setting $\dot{\theta} = 0$ and $\ddot{\theta} = 0$, we get $\sin\theta = 0$, giving:

$$\theta = n\pi, \quad n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$

- $n$ even: **Stable** (downward position, restoring torque).
- $n$ odd: **Unstable** (inverted position, no restoring torque).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a simple pendulum with viscous damping and an external periodic torque, and identify the equilibrium points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the resulting non-autonomous equation into an equivalent autonomous state-space form using the augmentation technique. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The free-body analysis gives:

$$mL^2\ddot{\theta} + bL\dot{\theta} + mgL\sin\theta = \tau_0\cos(\omega t)$$

Dividing by $mL^2$:

$$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{b}{mL}\dot{\theta} + \frac{g}{L}\sin\theta = \frac{\tau_0}{mL^2}\cos(\omega t)$$

Equilibrium analysis: at equilibrium, $\dot{\theta} = 0$ and $\ddot{\theta} = 0$:

$$\frac{g}{L}\sin\theta_{eq} = \frac{\tau_0}{mL^2}\cos(\omega t)$$

$$\sin\theta_{eq} = \frac{\tau_0}{mgL}\cos(\omega t)$$

Because $t$ appears on the RHS, the equilibrium is **time-varying** — these are **quasi-equilibria**, not true equilibria.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Augment the state with $x_3 = \omega t$, so $\dot{x}_3 = \omega$. The augmented autonomous state-space form is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= x_2 \\
\dot{x}_2 &= -\frac{b}{mL}x_2 - \frac{g}{L}\sin(x_1) + \frac{\tau_0}{mL^2}\cos(x_3) \\
\dot{x}_3 &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

The system is now autonomous in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with state vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, x_3]^T$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a mass attached to a non-linear spring with restoring force $F = k_1 x + k_2 x^2 + k_3 x^3$, including viscous damping. Write the system in state-space form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine all equilibrium points of the system in part (a) and discuss the physical significance of the non-linear terms $k_2 x^2$ and $k_3 x^3$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Newton's second law along the direction of motion:

$$m\ddot{x} = -b\dot{x} - k_1 x - k_2 x^2 - k_3 x^3$$

Standard form:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + k_1 x + k_2 x^2 + k_3 x^3 = 0$$

State variables: $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = \dot{x}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= x_2 \\
\dot{x}_2 &= -\frac{k_1}{m}x_1 - \frac{k_2}{m}x_1^2 - \frac{k_3}{m}x_1^3 - \frac{b}{m}x_2
\end{aligned}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

Setting $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$x_2 = 0, \quad k_1 x_1 + k_2 x_1^2 + k_3 x_1^3 = 0$$

Factor:

$$x_1(k_3 x_1^2 + k_2 x_1 + k_1) = 0$$

Trivial equilibrium: $x_1 = 0$. Non-trivial equilibria:

$$x_1 = \frac{-k_2 \pm \sqrt{k_2^2 - 4k_1 k_3}}{2k_3}$$

**Physical significance:**
- $k_2 x^2$: **Asymmetric (quadratic) non-linearity** — produces different restoring force in compression vs. extension (e.g., rubber, geometric stiffening).
- $k_3 x^3$: **Symmetric (cubic, Duffing) non-linearity** — produces equal stiffening/softening on both sides (e.g., beam-bending at large deflections).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $m$ or $mL^2$** at the end of the derivation. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Calling quasi-equilibria "true equilibria"** in forced (non-autonomous) systems. This is a conceptual error worth **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the augmentation equation** $\dot{x}_3 = \omega$ when converting non-autonomous to autonomous. Examiners insist on **all three** state equations.
> 4. **Incorrectly using $\sin\theta \approx \theta$** in the non-linear region (large angle) — this is a **linearisation error**.
> 5. **Not specifying $n \in \mathbb{Z}$** when listing equilibrium points of the pendulum.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Autonomous**: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ — no explicit $t$ on RHS. Equilibria are time-invariant.
- **Non-Autonomous**: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, t)$ — $t$ explicitly on RHS. Equilibria are time-varying or absent.
- **Simple pendulum (autonomous)**: $\ddot{\theta} + \dfrac{g}{L}\sin\theta = 0$ — equilibria at $\theta = n\pi$.
- **Damped pendulum**: add $\dfrac{b}{mL}\dot{\theta}$ term.
- **Forced pendulum (non-autonomous)**: add $\dfrac{\tau_0}{mL^2}\cos(\omega t)$ on RHS.
- **Linear mass-spring**: $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = 0$ — one equilibrium at origin.
- **Non-linear mass-spring**: $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + k_1 x + k_2 x^2 + k_3 x^3 = 0$ — up to 3 equilibria.
- **Quadratic non-linearity $k_2 x^2$**: asymmetric — compression ≠ extension.
- **Cubic non-linearity $k_3 x^3$**: symmetric (Duffing) — stiffening or softening spring.
- **Augmentation trick**: define $x_3 = \omega t$, add $\dot{x}_3 = \omega$ to convert non-autonomous $\to$ autonomous.
- **Physical constants**: $g = 9.81$ m/s², $L$ in m, $m$ in kg.
- **Linearisation**: valid only for $\vert\theta\vert \ll 1$ rad using $\sin\theta \approx \theta$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singular Points - Phase Portraits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Singular Points - Phase Portraits # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Singular Point (Equilibrium / Critical Point) A **Singular Point** of an autonomous non-linear system is a point in the state space where the system remains permanently at rest — i.e., all state derivatives vanish simultaneously. For a second-order autonomous system of the form: $$ \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} f_1(x_1, x_2) \\ f_2(x_1, x_2) \end{bmatrix} $$ a singular point $\mathbf{x}_{sp} = (x_1^{sp}, x_2^{sp})^{T}$ satisfies the algebraic condition: $$ f_1(x_1^{sp}, x_2^{sp}) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad f_2(x_1^{sp}, x_2^{sp}) = 0 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Singular points are also called **Equilibrium Points** or **Critical Points**. They form the *skeleton* of any phase portrait — every trajectory either starts from, ends at, or circulates around these special points. ## 1.2 Phase Portrait A **Phase Portrait** is the geometric family of trajectories traced by system states in the $(x_1, x_2)$ phase plane, with arrows indicating the direction of motion as time $t \to \infty$. The portrait provides a *global* qualitative picture of system behaviour without solving the non-linear differential equation. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ball on a Hill" Picture > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine a small ball rolling on a hilly terrain where height represents potential energy. > - **Top of a hill (unstable node):** The ball teeters at the peak; the slightest push sends it rolling away — *every direction leads to escape*. > - **Bottom of a valley (stable node):** The ball settles into the lowest point; any disturbance returns it home — *all paths converge*. > - **Saddle of a mountain pass (saddle point):** One direction looks like a valley, the perpendicular direction looks like a ridge — the ball is attracted from one side and repelled along the other. > - **Bowl with friction (stable focus):** The ball spirals inward, overshooting the centre repeatedly, like a damped pendulum. > - **Frictionless bowl (centre):** The ball traces a closed circular orbit forever, like a friction-free pendulum. The **sign of the eigenvalues** of the linearized Jacobian matrix at that point determines which "terrain type" the equilibrium behaves like. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Eigenvalues of Jacobian:** $\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\text{tr}(A) \pm \sqrt{\text{tr}(A)^2 - 4\det(A)}}{2}$ - **Stability Boundary (in $\text{tr}$–$\det$ plane):** $\text{tr}(A) = 0$ and $\det(A) = 0$ - **Damping Ratio (for focus):** $\zeta = \frac{-\text{tr}(A)}{2\sqrt{\det(A)}}$ - **Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\det(A)}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Local Linearization Procedure (Hartman–Grobman Theorem Backbone) To classify a singular point $\mathbf{x}_{sp}$ of a non-linear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$, the KTU-accepted procedure is: 1. **Find the singular point** by solving $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$. 2. **Compute the Jacobian matrix** $A = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \bigg\vert_{\mathbf{x}_{sp}}$. 3. **Evaluate eigenvalues** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of $A$. 4. **Classify** the singular point using the table below. 5. **Sketch the phase portrait** using eigenvectors as asymptotic directions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hartman–Grobman Theorem:** Near a *hyperbolic* singular point (Re$(\lambda_i) \neq 0$ for all $i$), the non-linear flow is *topologically equivalent* to the linearized flow. This justifies using linear analysis to classify non-linear equilibria. ## 2.2 The Six Canonical Singular Point Types | # | Type | Eigenvalue Signature | Phase Portrait Behaviour | Stability | |---|------|----------------------|--------------------------|-----------| | 1 | **Stable Node** | $\lambda_1 < \lambda_2 < 0$ (real, distinct, both negative) | Trajectories approach origin along two tangent directions; no rotation | Asymptotically Stable | | 2 | **Unstable Node** | $\lambda_1 > \lambda_2 > 0$ (real, distinct, both positive) | Trajectories leave origin along tangent directions | Unstable | | 3 | **Saddle Point** | $\lambda_1 < 0 < \lambda_2$ (real, opposite signs) | Two stable manifolds (incoming) and two unstable manifolds (outgoing) cross at origin | Unstable | | 4 | **Stable Focus (Spiral Sink)** | $\lambda = \alpha \pm j\beta$ with $\alpha < 0$ | Trajectories spiral inward | Asymptotically Stable | | 5 | **Unstable Focus (Spiral Source)** | $\lambda = \alpha \pm j\beta$ with $\alpha > 0$ | Trajectories spiral outward | Unstable | | 6 | **Centre (Vortex)** | $\lambda = \pm j\omega$ (purely imaginary) | Closed elliptical/circular orbits | Lyapunov Stable (not asymptotically) | > [!NOTE] > **Degenerate Cases** (also tested in KTU): > - **Stable Star Node:** $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 < 0$, single eigenvector — every trajectory is a straight radial line. > - **Unstable Star Node:** $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 > 0$, single eigenvector. > - **Degenerate Node (single zero eigenvalue):** $\det(A) = 0$ — not classifiable by Hartman–Grobman (non-hyperbolic). ## 2.3 Decision Logic in the $\text{tr}(A)$–$\det(A)$ Plane Define $T = \text{tr}(A)$ and $\Delta = \det(A)$. The discriminant of the characteristic polynomial is $T^2 - 4\Delta$. | Condition | Discriminant | Eigenvalue Type | Singular Point | |-----------|--------------|-----------------|----------------| | $T^2 - 4\Delta > 0,\ T < 0,\ \Delta > 0$ | Positive | Real, distinct, both negative | Stable Node | | $T^2 - 4\Delta > 0,\ T > 0,\ \Delta > 0$ | Positive | Real, distinct, both positive | Unstable Node | | $T^2 - 4\Delta > 0,\ \Delta < 0$ | Positive | Real, opposite signs | Saddle Point | | $T^2 - 4\Delta < 0,\ T < 0,\ \Delta > 0$ | Negative | Complex, $\text{Re} < 0$ | Stable Focus | | $T^2 - 4\Delta < 0,\ T > 0,\ \Delta > 0$ | Negative | Complex, $\text{Re} > 0$ | Unstable Focus | | $T^2 - 4\Delta < 0,\ T = 0,\ \Delta > 0$ | Negative | Purely imaginary | Centre | | $\Delta = 0$ | — | At least one zero eigenvalue | Non-hyperbolic (higher-order) | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Application of Phase Portraits | |-------------------|-------------------------------| | **Power Systems** | Transient stability analysis of synchronous generators after faults | | **Aircraft Dynamics** | Identifying stable trim conditions and divergence modes | | **Pendulum & Robotics** | Analysing swing-up and energy-based controllers | | **Chemical Reactors** | Locating multiple steady states in exothermic reactions | | **Biological Systems** | Population dynamics (predator–prey Lotka–Volterra models) | | **Electronic Oscillators** | Van der Pol oscillator — limit cycle behaviour | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Saddle Point Classification Consider the non-linear system: $$ \dot{x}_1 = x_1 - x_1^3 $$ $$ \dot{x}_2 = -x_2 $$ **Step 1: Locate singular points.** Set $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$: $$ x_1 - x_1^3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1(1 - x_1^2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 \in \{-1, 0, 1\} $$ $$ -x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 0 $$ Hence the three singular points are: $$ \mathbf{x}_{sp} \in \{(0,0),\ (1,0),\ (-1,0)\} $$ **Step 2: Compute the Jacobian matrix.** $$ J(x_1,x_2) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 3x_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3: Evaluate $J$ at each singular point and find eigenvalues.** At $(0,0)$: $$ J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = 1,\ \lambda_2 = -1 $$ Eigenvalues of **opposite sign** ⟹ **Saddle Point** (unstable). Eigenvectors: For $\lambda_1 = 1$, $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1, 0)^T$ (unstable manifold). For $\lambda_2 = -1$, $\mathbf{v}_2 = (0, 1)^T$ (stable manifold). At $(1,0)$: $$ J(1,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 3 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \lambda_1 = -2,\ \lambda_2 = -1 \quad \text{(both negative, distinct)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Stable Node} $$ At $(-1,0)$: $$ J(-1,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 3 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Identical to $(1,0)$ ⟹ **Stable Node**. **Step 4: Sketch using the $(T, \Delta)$ plane.** For $(0,0)$: $T = 0$, $\Delta = -1 < 0$ — confirms saddle region. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Stable Focus with Quantitative Damping System: $$ \dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_2 $$ $$ \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 - x_2 $$ **Step 1: Singular point.** $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$ both yield $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 0$. **Step 2: Jacobian (constant, since system is linear).** $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3: Characteristic equation.** $$ \det(\lambda I - A) = \begin{vmatrix} \lambda + 1 & -1 \\ 1 & \lambda + 1 \end{vmatrix} = 0 $$ Expanding using the formula $\det\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = ad - bc$: $$ (\lambda + 1)^2 - (-1)(1) = 0 $$ $$ (\lambda + 1)^2 + 1 = 0 $$ $$ \lambda^2 + 2\lambda + 2 = 0 $$ **Step 4: Solve the quadratic.** $$ \lambda = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 8}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-4}}{2} = -1 \pm j $$ **Step 5: Classify.** - Complex conjugate pair: confirms **focus**. - $\text{Re}(\lambda) = -1 < 0$: confirms **stable focus** (spiral sink). **Step 6: Damping ratio and natural frequency.** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\det(A)} = \sqrt{(-1)(-1) - (1)(-1)} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\ \text{rad/s} $$ $$ \zeta = \frac{-\text{tr}(A)}{2\sqrt{\det(A)}} = \frac{-(-2)}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 $$ Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — trajectories spiral inward. **Step 7: Trajectory equation in phase plane.** Differentiating $\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_2$ and substituting $x_2 = \dot{x}_1 + x_1$: $$ \ddot{x}_1 = -\dot{x}_1 + \dot{x}_2 = -\dot{x}_1 + (-x_1 - x_2) = -\dot{x}_1 - x_1 - (\dot{x}_1 + x_1) $$ $$ \ddot{x}_1 + 2\dot{x}_1 + 2x_1 = 0 $$ Solution: $x_1(t) = e^{-t}\left(C_1 \cos t + C_2 \sin t\right)$ — confirming decaying oscillation, consistent with a **stable focus**. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Quick KTU Cheat) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Decision:}\quad &\text{If } \det(A) < 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Saddle Point} \\ &\text{If } \det(A) > 0 \text{ and } \text{tr}(A)^2 > 4\det(A) \\ &\qquad \text{then sign}(\text{tr}(A)) \text{ decides node stability} \\ &\text{If } \det(A) > 0 \text{ and } \text{tr}(A)^2 < 4\det(A) \\ &\qquad \text{then sign}(\text{tr}(A)) \text{ decides focus stability} \\ &\text{If } \text{tr}(A) = 0 \text{ and } \det(A) > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Centre} \end{aligned} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Phase Portrait Classification Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Find singular point x_sp"] --> B["Compute Jacobian J at x_sp"] B --> C["Compute trace T and determinant Delta"] C --> D{"Delta less than 0?"} D -- "Yes" --> E["SADDLE POINT
Unstable"] D -- "No" --> F{"T equals 0?"} F -- "Yes" --> G{"Delta greater than 0?"} G -- "Yes" --> H["CENTRE
Lyapunov Stable"] G -- "No" --> I["Non-hyperbolic
Higher order test"] F -- "No" --> J{"T squared minus 4 Delta
greater than 0?"} J -- "Yes" --> K{"T less than 0?"} K -- "Yes" --> L["STABLE NODE
Asymptotically Stable"] K -- "No" --> M["UNSTABLE NODE
Unstable"] J -- "No" --> N{"T less than 0?"} N -- "Yes" --> O["STABLE FOCUS
Spiral Sink"] N -- "No" --> P["UNSTABLE FOCUS
Spiral Source"] ``` ## 4.2 Phase Portrait Construction Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Step 1: Identify
non-linear ODEs"] --> S2["Step 2: Locate all
singular points"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Linearize
via Jacobian at each x_sp"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute
eigenvalues"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Classify
node/focus/saddle/centre"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Find eigenvectors
asymptotic directions"] S6 --> S7["Step 7: Draw trajectories
and arrows"] S7 --> S8["Final Phase
Portrait Diagram"] ``` ## 4.3 Eigenvalue-to-Phase-Portrait Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD E1["Eigenvalues lambda 1 and lambda 2"] --> E2{"Real or Complex?"} E2 -- "Real" --> E3{"Same sign?"} E2 -- "Complex" --> E4{"Re of lambda
positive or negative?"} E3 -- "Both negative" --> E5["Stable Node
Both arrows point IN"] E3 -- "Both positive" --> E6["Unstable Node
Both arrows point OUT"] E3 -- "Opposite sign" --> E7["Saddle Point
Two IN two OUT"] E4 -- "Re less than 0" --> E8["Stable Focus
Spiral IN"] E4 -- "Re greater than 0" --> E9["Unstable Focus
Spiral OUT"] E4 -- "Re equals 0" --> E10["Centre
Closed orbits"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a singular point. With a suitable sketch, explain the nature of trajectories near (i) a stable node and (ii) a saddle point.** **Model Answer:** A **singular point** (or equilibrium point) of an autonomous system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ is a state $\mathbf{x}_{sp}$ at which $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{sp}) = \mathbf{0}$, meaning the system remains permanently at rest once placed there. **(i) Stable Node:** Both eigenvalues are real, distinct, and negative ($\lambda_1 < \lambda_2 < 0$). Trajectories approach the singular point along the eigenvector directions without rotation. The origin is **asymptotically stable** — all nearby trajectories converge to it as $t \to \infty$. **(ii) Saddle Point:** Eigenvalues are real with opposite signs ($\lambda_1 < 0 < \lambda_2$). Trajectories approach along the eigenvector associated with the negative eigenvalue (stable manifold) and depart along the eigenvector associated with the positive eigenvalue (unstable manifold). The point is **unstable** — generic perturbations cause escape. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "stable manifold" with the point being stable. In a **saddle**, only a *measure-zero* set of initial conditions (those exactly on the stable eigenvector) converge; the point itself is **unstable**. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. --- ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State the Hartman–Grobman theorem. Why is it important for classifying singular points of non-linear systems?** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** The Hartman–Grobman theorem states that near a **hyperbolic** singular point (one for which all eigenvalues of the Jacobian have non-zero real parts), the non-linear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ is **topologically equivalent** (orbitally conjugate) to its linearization $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ where $A = \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\big\vert_{\mathbf{x}_{sp}}$. **Importance:** 1. It **justifies linearization** — the qualitative phase portrait of the non-linear system near a hyperbolic equilibrium is *the same* as that of the linearized system. 2. It reduces a hard non-linear problem to an **eigenvalue computation** of a $2 \times 2$ matrix. 3. It is valid for **all hyperbolic** points — saddle, node, focus. Only non-hyperbolic points (centres, degenerate cases) require higher-order analysis. > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners award 1 mark for the statement and 2 marks for the engineering significance. --- ## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Choice Q1]` **(a) [7 Marks]** For the system: $$ \dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + x_2(1 - x_1^2) $$ find all singular points, linearize around each, and classify the type. **(b) [7 Marks]** Sketch the phase portrait of the above system, marking all singular points, their stable and unstable manifolds, and the direction of increasing time. --- #### Solution to (a) **Step 1: Find singular points.** From $\dot{x}_1 = x_2 = 0$ we get $x_2 = 0$. Substituting into $\dot{x}_2 = 0$: $$ -x_1 + 0 \cdot (1 - x_1^2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 0 $$ **Single singular point: $\mathbf{x}_{sp} = (0, 0)$.** **Step 2: Compute Jacobian.** $$ f_1 = x_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} = 0,\ \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} = 1 $$ $$ f_2 = -x_1 + x_2 - x_1^2 x_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} = -1 - 2x_1 x_2,\ \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} = 1 - x_1^2 $$ $$ J(x_1, x_2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 - 2x_1 x_2 & 1 - x_1^2 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3: Evaluate at origin.** $$ J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4: Characteristic equation.** $$ \det(\lambda I - A) = \begin{vmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 1 & \lambda - 1 \end{vmatrix} = 0 $$ $$ \lambda(\lambda - 1) - (-1)(1) = 0 $$ $$ \lambda^2 - \lambda + 1 = 0 $$ **Step 5: Eigenvalues.** $$ \lambda = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm j\sqrt{3}}{2} $$ **Step 6: Classify.** $\text{Re}(\lambda) = \frac{1}{2} > 0$ and $\lambda$ is complex ⟹ **Unstable Focus (Spiral Source)**. Eigenvectors: For $\lambda = \frac{1+j\sqrt{3}}{2}$, solving $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = 0$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} -\lambda & 1 \\ -1 & 1 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \lambda \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \frac{1+j\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} $$ The real and imaginary parts give the orientation of the spiral. --- #### Solution to (b) **Sketching instructions for the candidate:** 1. Plot the origin with an **unstable spiral marker** (a swirl with arrows pointing outward, expanding clockwise or counter-clockwise depending on eigenvector rotation). 2. The spiral is **counter-clockwise** because the off-diagonal element $a_{12} = 1 > 0$ in $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$ means motion in the upper half-plane ($x_2 > 0$) drives $x_1$ to increase. 3. All trajectories starting at any initial condition (other than the origin) move **outward** in a counter-clockwise spiral, growing without bound. 4. The non-linear term $x_2(1 - x_1^2)$ for $\vert x_1 \vert > 1$ creates a *non-linear damping* effect — far from origin, trajectories may eventually saturate, but local behaviour near origin remains an unstable focus. **Phase Portrait Description (since text-based):** - Origin: marked with spiral-outward symbol. - Trajectories: counter-clockwise expanding spirals emanating from origin. - No other equilibrium points exist, so trajectories either spiral outward indefinitely or escape to infinity. --- ### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Choice Q2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the system: $$ \dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 - x_2 $$ Determine the type and stability of the singular point at the origin. Compute the damping ratio and natural frequency. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the system is modified to $\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + x_2$ instead, re-classify the singular point.
