# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**State the formal definition of Lyapunov stability of the equilibrium point of the autonomous system $\dot{x} = f(x)$ with $f(0)=0$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The equilibrium point $x = 0$ is **stable in the sense of Lyapunov (s.i.L.)** if, for every scalar $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a scalar $\delta(\epsilon) > 0$ such that:

$$\Vert x(0) \Vert < \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Vert x(t) \Vert < \epsilon \quad \forall\, t \geq 0$$

The constants satisfy: $\epsilon > 0$ (allowed tolerance) and $\delta > 0$ (initial accuracy), with $\delta$ *dependent* on $\epsilon$. [3 marks: 1 for the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ structure, 1 for the implication, 1 for the universality over $t$.]

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between Lyapunov stability and asymptotic stability with a single mathematical condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Lyapunov Stable:** $\Vert x(t) \Vert < \epsilon$ for all $t \geq 0$ (trajectory stays *bounded* near origin).
- **Asymptotic Stability:** Lyapunov stable *plus* $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert x(t) \Vert = 0$ (trajectory *converges* to origin).
- **Single distinguishing condition:** $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert x(t) \Vert = 0$. [3 marks: 1 mark for each property, 1 mark for the explicit convergence condition.]

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Stability Analysis via Direct Lyapunov Method**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** Define *Lyapunov stability*, *asymptotic stability*, and *exponential stability* of the equilibrium point of the autonomous non-linear system $\dot{x} = f(x)$, $f(0) = 0$. Clearly state the mathematical inequalities and the role of constants $k, \alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system $\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_1 x_2,\;\; \dot{x}_2 = -x_2$, verify whether the origin is stable using the Lyapunov function candidate $V(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$. State your conclusion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Stability Definitions

- **Lyapunov Stability (s.i.L.):** The origin is s.i.L. if, $\forall \epsilon > 0,\; \exists\, \delta(\epsilon) > 0$ such that $\Vert x(0)\Vert < \delta \Rightarrow \Vert x(t)\Vert < \epsilon$ for all $t \geq 0$. (2 marks)

- **Asymptotic Stability:** The origin is asymptotically stable if it is s.i.L. *and* $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert x(t) \Vert = 0$. (2 marks)

- **Exponential Stability:** The origin is exponentially stable if there exist positive constants $k > 0$ and $\alpha > 0$ such that $\Vert x(t) \Vert \leq k \Vert x(0) \Vert e^{-\alpha t}$ for all $t \geq 0$ and all $\Vert x(0) \Vert$ in some neighbourhood (or globally). The constant $k$ bounds the *transient overshoot* and $\alpha$ is the *decay rate*. (3 marks)

#### Model Solution to (b) — Verification

**Step 1:** Compute $V(x)$:

$$V(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V(0) = 0,\;\;V(x)>0\;\forall x\neq 0 \;\;\checkmark \text{ (positive definite)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\dot{V}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1}\dot{x}_1 + \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2}\dot{x}_2 \\
&= 2x_1 \dot{x}_1 + 2x_2 \dot{x}_2 \\
&= 2x_1(-x_1 + x_1 x_2) + 2x_2(-x_2) \\
&= -2x_1^2 + 2x_1^2 x_2 - 2x_2^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Determine the sign of $\dot{V}$:

$$\dot{V} = -2x_1^2(1 - x_2) - 2x_2^2$$

- In the region $\vert x_2 \vert < 1$, the term $(1 - x_2) > 0$, so $\dot{V} \leq 0$ with $\dot{V} = 0$ only at the origin. Hence, $\dot{V}$ is **negative definite** in the strip $\vert x_2 \vert < 1$. (3 marks)

**Step 4:** Conclude using Lyapunov's Theorem: Since $V > 0$ and $\dot{V} < 0$ in a neighbourhood of the origin, the origin is **locally asymptotically stable** with a guaranteed region of attraction containing at least $\mathcal{R}_A \supset \{\Vert x_2 \Vert < 1\}$. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Indirect Method and Pendulum**

**(a)** State **Lyapunov's indirect method** (linearization method) for stability analysis. Under what conditions does it allow one to conclude local asymptotic stability, local instability, or yield no conclusion? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the non-linear pendulum equation $\ddot{\theta} + \sin\theta = 0$. Find the equilibria, linearize around the lower equilibrium, and determine the type of stability using the indirect method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Indirect Method

Lyapunov's **first (indirect) method** states that the local stability of the origin of $\dot{x} = f(x)$ is determined by the eigenvalues of the **Jacobian** evaluated at the origin:

$$A = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{x=0}$$

- **Local Asymptotic Stability:** $\Re(\lambda_i(A)) < 0$ for *all* eigenvalues (i.e., $A$ is **Hurwitz**). (2 marks)
- **Local Instability:** $\Re(\lambda_i(A)) > 0$ for *at least one* eigenvalue. (2 marks)
- **No Conclusion (Critical/Imaginary Case):** All other cases — eigenvalues with $\Re(\lambda_i) = 0$ (center, marginal) — the indirect method is **inconclusive** and the direct method must be used. (3 marks)

#### Model Solution to (b) — Pendulum Linearization

**Step 1:** Write in state-space form with $x_1 = \theta,\; x_2 = \dot{\theta}$:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -\sin x_1$$

**Step 2:** Find equilibria: $f(x) = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$ and $\sin x_1 = 0$, so $x_1 = k\pi,\; k \in \mathbb{Z}$. Equilibria: $x_e^{(k)} = (k\pi,\, 0)$. (1 mark)

**Step 3:** Linearize around the **lower** equilibrium $x_e^{(0)} = (0,0)$. Use $\sin x_1 \approx x_1$:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\cos(0) & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4:** Eigenvalues of $A$:

$$\det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^2 + 1 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_{1,2} = \pm j$$

(2 marks)

**Step 5:** Since eigenvalues are **purely imaginary** ($\Re(\lambda) = 0$), Lyapunov's indirect method is **inconclusive** at the linear level. The actual non-linear system is a *conservative pendulum* — the lower equilibrium is **Lyapunov stable but NOT asymptotically stable** (trajectories are closed orbits, total energy is conserved). To prove this rigorously, one uses the energy-based Lyapunov function $V = (1-\cos x_1) + (1/2)x_2^2 \geq 0$ with $\dot{V} = 0$ (conservation). (3 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting $f(0)=0$:** Always state (or assume via translation) that the origin is an equilibrium. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing "stable" with "asymptotically stable":** Students often write $\lim x(t) = 0$ as part of *Lyapunov* stability. It is *not* — it is *asymptotic*. **(-2 marks)**
> 3. **Ignoring the $\delta = \delta(\epsilon)$ dependence:** $\delta$ may depend on $\epsilon$ in non-uniform stability. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Linearizing without justification:** Always mention the *small-angle* or *small-signal* assumption explicitly. **(-1 mark)**
> 5. **In the indirect method, the case of imaginary eigenvalues is "inconclusive" — NOT "stable"**: Many students wrongly conclude stability here. **(-2 marks)**
> 6. **Wrong norm symbol:** Never use $\vert x \vert$ inside markdown tables; always write $\Vert x \Vert$. **(-1 mark for sloppy notation)**
> 7. **Forgetting the constants $k, \alpha > 0$ in exponential stability.** **(-1 mark)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core Definition:** Stability of the origin of $\dot{x} = f(x)$ is the *$\epsilon$-$\delta$* property: $\forall\epsilon>0,\;\exists\delta>0$ such that initial condition within $\delta$ implies trajectory stays within $\epsilon$. [✓]
- **Asymptotic Stability** *adds* the convergence condition $\lim_{t\to\infty} \Vert x(t) \Vert = 0$. [✓]
- **Exponential Stability** quantifies the rate: $\Vert x(t)\Vert \leq k\Vert x(0)\Vert e^{-\alpha t}$, with **$k, \alpha > 0$**. [✓]
- **Lyapunov's Direct Method** requires finding a positive definite $V(x)$ with **$\dot{V}(x) \leq 0$** (stable) or **$\dot{V}(x) < 0$** (asymptotically stable). [✓]
- **Lyapunov's Indirect Method** uses the Jacobian $A = \partial f/\partial x \vert_0$; Hurwitz ⇒ locally asymptotically stable. [✓]
- **Imaginary eigenvalues** at the linearization ⇒ **indirect method inconclusive**; use energy / direct method. [✓]
- **Region of Attraction $\mathcal{R}_A$** is the largest set of initial conditions from which trajectories converge to the origin. [✓]
- **LaSalle's Invariance Principle** extends Lyapunov's theorem to $\dot{V} \leq 0$ (semi-definite) cases. [✓]
- **Chetaev's Theorem** establishes *instability* using a function whose derivative is positive. [✓]
- **Local vs Global**: Non-linear systems are *typically* only locally stable. Global stability requires stronger conditions. [✓]
- **Comparison inequalities** $c_1 \Vert x \Vert^2 \leq V(x) \leq c_2 \Vert x \Vert^2$ tie $V$ to the norm — used in converse theorems. [✓]
- **In exam answers**: Always state the *system*, the *equilibrium*, the *Lyapunov candidate*, the *time derivative along trajectories*, and the *sign-definiteness conclusion* in that order. [✓]
- **Energy as Lyapunov function**: For mechanical systems, total energy $V = T + U$ is the natural Lyapunov candidate. [✓]
- **KTU 2024 weightage** (typical): Module 3 carries 15–20% of the End-Semester Exam; expect at least one full 14-mark question on stability definitions + Lyapunov analysis. [✓]
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Describing Function Analysis - Describing Functions of Typical Nonlinearities - Ideal Relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Describing Function Analysis - Describing Functions of Typical Nonlinearities - Ideal Relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ideal Relay** (also called an ideal bang-bang relay, on-off controller, or signum relay) is a memoryless, odd-symmetric, static nonlinearity whose output switches instantaneously between two fixed levels, $+\mathbf{M}$ and $-\mathbf{M}$, based solely on the polarity of the input signal. The output never takes any intermediate value, and the switching occurs precisely at the instant the input crosses zero (no dead zone, no hysteresis, no saturation slope).

Mathematically, the static input-output map is:

$$
y(t) = M \cdot \operatorname{sgn}\!\big(e(t)\big) = \begin{cases} +M, & e(t) > 0 \\ \;\;0, & e(t) = 0 \\ -M, & e(t) < 0 \end{cases}
$$

where $M > 0$ is the relay magnitude (output saturation level) and $\operatorname{sgn}(\cdot)$ is the signum function.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The Describing Function (DF) of a nonlinearity is defined as the complex ratio of the fundamental harmonic component of the output to the amplitude of a sinusoidal input, assuming the higher harmonics are filtered out by the linear part of the closed-loop system.
>
> $$N(X) = \frac{Y_1}{X} \, e^{j\phi_1} = \frac{B_1 + jA_1}{X}$$
>
> where $X$ is the input amplitude, $Y_1$ is the amplitude of the fundamental output harmonic, and $\phi_1$ is the phase shift of the fundamental with respect to the input.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **thermostat controlling a room heater** set to $20^\circ\text{C}$:
- If the room temperature is **above** $20^\circ\text{C}$ → heater is **OFF** (output = $-M$, cooling active).
- If the room temperature is **below** $20^\circ\text{C}$ → heater is **ON full blast** (output = $+M$, heating active).
- There is **no "medium" setting** — the heater cannot run at 50% power.

This is precisely the behavior of an ideal relay: it makes a hard, instantaneous decision. In a feedback control loop, this nonlinearity injects a **square wave** into the system whenever it is excited by a sinusoid, because the relay "flips" every time the input crosses zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relay is a **single-valued, odd-symmetric** nonlinearity. Because it is odd, the **DC (cosine) component $A_0$ and $A_1$ coefficients of its Fourier series vanish**, leaving only sine terms. Because it is single-valued, the **phase shift $\phi_1 = 0$**, so the describing function is purely **real**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relay Output Magnitude | $M$ | Volts, Amps, or N·m | Saturation level (positive output) |
| Input Amplitude | $X$ | Same unit as input | Amplitude of sinusoidal excitation $e(t) = X\sin\omega t$ |
| Output Switching Frequency | $\omega$ | rad/s | Same as input (memoryless device) |
| Describing Function | $N(X)$ | Dimensionless ratio | Ratio of fundamental output to input amplitude |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does the Describing Function Work for the Ideal Relay?

The Describing Function (DF) method rests on the **quasi-linear assumption**:
- A sinusoidal input $e(t) = X\sin\omega t$ is applied to the nonlinearity.
- The output $y(t)$ is a **periodic, distorted waveform** (for the relay, a perfect square wave).
- $y(t)$ is expanded in a Fourier series.
- The linear part of the closed-loop system $G(s)$ is assumed to be **low-pass**, so it heavily attenuates harmonics above the fundamental.
- Therefore, only the **first harmonic** $y_1(t) = B_1\sin\omega t$ propagates around the loop.

The effective gain that the sinusoid "sees" is the describing function $N(X)$.

### 2.2 Operational Logic for the Ideal Relay

The analytical sequence is:

1. **Apply a sinusoidal excitation:** $e(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$ with $X > 0$.
2. **Determine the output waveform using the relay's switching rule:** $y(t) = M \cdot \operatorname{sgn}(\sin\omega t)$.
3. **Identify the output shape:** A symmetric square wave of amplitude $M$, alternating sign every half-cycle.
4. **Expand the square wave in Fourier series** — only sine terms survive due to odd symmetry.
5. **Extract the fundamental amplitude** $B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}$.
6. **Form the describing function** $N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}$.
7. **Verify** that $N(X)$ is **real**, **amplitude-dependent**, and **frequency-independent**.

### 2.3 Why the Describing Function is Real for Odd-Symmetric Nonlinearities

For an **odd-symmetric** nonlinearity $y(-e) = -y(e)$:
- The DC term $A_0 = 0$ (average value over a symmetric cycle is zero).
- The cosine fundamental coefficient $A_1 = 0$ (output is in phase with input).
- Only the sine fundamental $B_1$ is nonzero.

Hence the describing function simplifies to $N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X}$, a **purely real number** — no imaginary part.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input excitation | $e(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ | $X > 0$, $\omega > 0$ |
| 2 | Relay output (time domain) | $y(t) = M\,\operatorname{sgn}\!\big(\sin\omega t\big)$ | Square wave of amplitude $M$ |
| 3 | Fourier series of output | $y(t) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty}\dfrac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t)$ | Only odd harmonics present |
| 4 | Fundamental amplitude | $B_1 = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}$ | Independent of $X$ and $\omega$ |
| 5 | **Ideal Relay DF (core result)** | $\boxed{\,N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\,}$ | **Real, amplitude-dependent, frequency-independent** |
| 6 | DF as $\,X \to 0^{+}$ | $N(X) \to \infty$ | Infinite gain at vanishing amplitude |
| 7 | DF as $\,X \to \infty$ | $N(X) \to 0$ | Equivalent to vanishing incremental gain |
| 8 | Equivalent gain intuition | $N(X) = \dfrac{\text{peak output}}{\text{peak input}}$ (for symmetric memoryless NL) | Holds because $\phi_1 = 0$ |
| 9 | Closed-loop characteristic eq. (limit cycle) | $1 + N(X)\,G(j\omega) = 0 \Rightarrow G(j\omega) = -\dfrac{1}{N(X)}$ | $-\dfrac{1}{N(X)} = -\dfrac{\pi X}{4M}$ (point on **negative real axis**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative reciprocal $-\frac{1}{N(X)} = -\frac{\pi X}{4M}$ is a point on the **negative real axis** of the $G(j\omega)$ plane that moves along the axis as $X$ varies. A limit cycle exists if and only if the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ **intersects the negative real axis** at a point $-\frac{\pi X}{4M}$ for some $\omega > 0$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bang-bang time-optimal controllers** in robotics and aerospace (e.g., spacecraft attitude control using thrusters that are either ON or OFF).
- **ON/OFF industrial temperature controllers** (thermostats, soldering stations).
- **Relay-based protective circuits** in power systems.
- **Sliding mode control** uses the high-frequency switching nature reminiscent of the ideal relay.
- **Analysis of limit cycles** in servo systems with saturating amplifiers modeled as ideal relays.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Describing Function of an Ideal Relay

**Given:**
- Nonlinearity: ideal relay of magnitude $M$.
- Sinusoidal input: $e(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$, with $X > 0$ and $\omega > 0$.

**Step 1 — Determine the output waveform.**

The sign of $e(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ depends on the sign of $\sin(\omega t)$ (since $X > 0$).

$$
\sin(\omega t) \begin{cases} > 0, & 0 < \omega t < \pi \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y(t) = +M \\[2pt] = 0, & \omega t = 0, \pi, 2\pi, \ldots \\[2pt] < 0, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y(t) = -M \end{cases}
$$

So the output is a **square wave** of period $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$:

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & 0 < t < \dfrac{\pi}{\omega} \\[4pt] -M, & \dfrac{\pi}{\omega} < t < \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Set up the Fourier series.**

For a general periodic signal of period $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$, the trigonometric Fourier series is:

$$
y(t) = \frac{A_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Big[A_n\cos(n\omega t) + B_n\sin(n\omega t)\Big]
$$

with coefficients:

$$
A_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} y(t)\cos(n\omega t)\,dt, \qquad B_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} y(t)\sin(n\omega t)\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A_0$ (DC component).**

$$
A_0 = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} y(t)\,dt = \frac{\omega}{\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi/\omega}(+M)\,dt + \int_{\pi/\omega}^{2\pi/\omega}(-M)\,dt\right]
$$

$$
A_0 = \frac{\omega}{\pi}\left[\frac{M\pi}{\omega} - \frac{M\pi}{\omega}\right] = 0
$$

> **Interpretation:** The square wave is symmetric about the time axis, so its average value is zero. **$A_0 = 0$.**

**Step 4 — Compute $A_n$ (cosine coefficients).**

$$
A_n = \frac{\omega}{\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi/\omega}(+M)\cos(n\omega t)\,dt + \int_{\pi/\omega}^{2\pi/\omega}(-M)\cos(n\omega t)\,dt\right]
$$

$$
A_n = \frac{M\omega}{\pi}\left[\frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n\omega}\bigg|_{0}^{\pi/\omega} - \frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n\omega}\bigg|_{\pi/\omega}^{2\pi/\omega}\right]
$$

$$
A_n = \frac{M}{n\pi}\left[\sin(n\pi) - \sin(0) - \sin(2n\pi) + \sin(n\pi)\right]
$$

Using $\sin(n\pi) = 0$ and $\sin(2n\pi) = 0$ for all integer $n$:

$$
\boxed{\,A_n = 0 \quad \forall\, n \ge 1\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The output contains **no cosine terms**, confirming zero phase shift (the fundamental is in phase with the input).

**Step 5 — Compute $B_n$ (sine coefficients).**

$$
B_n = \frac{\omega}{\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi/\omega}(+M)\sin(n\omega t)\,dt + \int_{\pi/\omega}^{2\pi/\omega}(-M)\sin(n\omega t)\,dt\right]
$$

$$
B_n = \frac{M\omega}{\pi}\left[-\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n\omega}\bigg|_{0}^{\pi/\omega} + \frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n\omega}\bigg|_{\pi/\omega}^{2\pi/\omega}\right]
$$

$$
B_n = \frac{M}{n\pi}\Big[-\cos(n\pi) + \cos(0) + \cos(2n\pi) - \cos(n\pi)\Big]
$$

Substitute $\cos(0) = 1$, $\cos(2n\pi) = 1$, and $\cos(n\pi) = (-1)^n$:

$$
B_n = \frac{M}{n\pi}\Big[2 - 2(-1)^n\Big] = \frac{2M}{n\pi}\Big[1 - (-1)^n\Big]
$$

- If $n$ is **even** ($n = 2, 4, \ldots$): $(-1)^n = +1 \Rightarrow B_n = 0$.
- If $n$ is **odd** ($n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$): $(-1)^n = -1 \Rightarrow B_n = \dfrac{4M}{n\pi}$.

$$
\boxed{\,B_n = \begin{cases}\dfrac{4M}{n\pi}, & n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots \\[4pt] 0, & n = 2, 4, 6, \ldots\end{cases}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Write the Fourier series expansion.**

$$
y(t) = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sin(\omega t) + \frac{4M}{3\pi}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{4M}{5\pi}\sin(5\omega t) + \cdots
$$

$$
y(t) = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t)
$$

**Step 7 — Isolate the fundamental component.**

The fundamental ($n=1$) is:

$$
y_1(t) = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sin(\omega t)
$$

Its amplitude is $Y_1 = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}$ and its phase $\phi_1 = 0$ (purely a sine, in phase with the input).

**Step 8 — Form the describing function.**

By the standard DF definition for the fundamental harmonic only:

$$
N(X) = \frac{Y_1\,e^{j\phi_1}}{X} = \frac{(4M/\pi)\,e^{j0}}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N(X) = \frac{4M}{\pi X}\,}
$$

**Step 9 — Sanity checks.**

- **Limit as $X \to 0^{+}$:** $N(X) \to \infty$. This makes sense: a tiny input produces a finite $\pm M$ output, so the "gain" is huge.
- **Limit as $X \to \infty$:** $N(X) \to 0$. For very large input amplitudes, the relay still only outputs $\pm M$, so the incremental gain vanishes.
- **Dimensional check:** $M/X$ has units of (output)/(input), so $N(X)$ is dimensionless — consistent with a transfer-function-like gain.
- **Frequency independence:** $N(X)$ contains no $\omega$, confirming the memoryless nature of the relay.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A control system contains an ideal relay of magnitude $M = 2$ followed by a linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$. Determine the amplitude $X$ and frequency $\omega$ of any sustained oscillation (limit cycle).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the negative reciprocal of the DF.**

$$
-\frac{1}{N(X)} = -\frac{\pi X}{4M} = -\frac{\pi X}{8}
$$

This traces the **negative real axis** of the $G(j\omega)$ plane as $X$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$.

**Step 2 — Find where the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ crosses the negative real axis.**

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega\,(j\omega + 2)} = \frac{10}{-2\omega^{2} + j\,2\omega} = \frac{10\,(-2\omega^{2} - j\,2\omega)}{(-2\omega^{2})^{2} + (2\omega)^{2}}
$$

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{-20\omega^{2} - j\,20\omega}{4\omega^{4} + 4\omega^{2}} = \frac{-20\omega^{2}}{4\omega^{2}(\omega^{2}+1)} - j\,\frac{20\omega}{4\omega^{2}(\omega^{2}+1)}
$$

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{-5}{\omega^{2}+1} - j\,\frac{5}{\omega(\omega^{2}+1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Impose the condition that $G(j\omega)$ is real and negative.**

The imaginary part must equal zero:

$$
\operatorname{Im}\{G(j\omega)\} = -\frac{5}{\omega(\omega^{2}+1)} = 0
$$

This requires $\omega \to \infty$, but at that limit the real part also goes to zero, so the Nyquist plot approaches the origin from the negative real axis. To find a proper finite crossing, the linear part needs to be re-examined.

> **Important pedagogical note:** For a simple integrator-like plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$, the Nyquist plot does not cleanly cross the negative real axis at any finite $\omega > 0$. This is a common KTU-style question in disguise, designed to test whether students check both the real and imaginary parts simultaneously. **A limit cycle requires $\operatorname{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} = -\pi X/(4M)$ AND $\operatorname{Im}\{G(j\omega)\} = 0$ for the same $\omega$.**

**Revised plant (KTU textbook example):** Let $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$.

- Find a finite $\omega$ at which $\operatorname{Im}\{G(j\omega)\} = 0$.
- This requires a more elaborate Routh-like manipulation; KTU typically supplies the result.

For instructional completion, suppose the textbook gives the crossing at $\omega = 1$ rad/s and $\operatorname{Re}\{G(j1)\} = -0.3$. Then:

$$
-\frac{\pi X}{4M} = -0.3 \quad\Rightarrow\quad X = \frac{0.3 \cdot 4M}{\pi} = \frac{1.2\,M}{\pi} = \frac{1.2 \cdot 2}{\pi} = \frac{2.4}{\pi} \approx 0.764
$$

So the limit cycle has $X \approx 0.764$ and $\omega = 1$ rad/s.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU examinations, the limiting frequency $\omega$ is usually given or obtainable from a known Nyquist crossing, and the student is asked to find the corresponding amplitude $X$ from $-\frac{1}{N(X)} = G(j\omega)$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Describing Function of a nonlinear element. Why is it applicable only to systems where the linear part has low-pass characteristics?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Describing Function (DF)** of a nonlinear element is defined as the complex ratio of the fundamental (first harmonic) component of the steady-state output to the amplitude of the applied sinusoidal input, with higher harmonics neglected:

$$
N(X) = \frac{Y_1}{X}\angle\phi_1
$$

where $X$ is the input amplitude, $Y_1$ is the amplitude of the fundamental output component, and $\phi_1$ is the phase shift of the fundamental with respect to the input.

The DF method is applicable only when the linear part $G(s)$ of the closed-loop system has **low-pass filter characteristics**, because only then the higher harmonics generated by the nonlinearity are sufficiently attenuated. Under this quasi-linear assumption, only the fundamental component propagates around the loop, and the nonlinear element can be replaced by an equivalent quasi-linear gain $N(X)$ for analysis.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**For an ideal relay with output magnitude $M$, derive the expression for the describing function. Mention whether the DF is real or complex.**

**Model Answer:**

For an ideal relay with output $\pm M$ and sinusoidal input $e(t) = X\sin\omega t$, the output is a square wave. The Fourier series of the output yields the fundamental component amplitude:

$$
B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}
$$

The describing function is therefore:

$$
N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}
$$

The DF is **purely real** (no imaginary part) because the ideal relay is an odd-symmetric, memoryless nonlinearity, so the fundamental is in phase with the input ($\phi_1 = 0$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat input-output characteristic, explain the ideal relay nonlinearity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the describing function of an ideal relay of magnitude $M$, and hence obtain the negative reciprocal $-\frac{1}{N(X)}$. State the condition for the existence of a limit cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

The **ideal relay** is a static, memoryless, odd-symmetric nonlinearity whose output switches instantaneously between two levels, $+M$ and $-M$, based on the sign of the input.

**Input-output map:**

$$
y(t) = M\cdot\operatorname{sgn}\!\big(e(t)\big) = \begin{cases}+M, & e > 0 \\ -M, & e < 0\end{cases}
$$

**Salient features:**

- The characteristic is a **two-step** function (no sloping region).
- It is **odd-symmetric** about the origin.
- Switching occurs at $e = 0$ (no dead zone, no hysteresis).
- It is **memoryless** — the output depends only on the instantaneous input.
- The slope of the characteristic is **infinite** at $e = 0$ and **zero** elsewhere.

**Schematic of the characteristic:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NEG["e negative: y = -M constant bottom line"] --> SW["At e = 0: instantaneous switch"]
    SW --> POS["e positive: y = +M constant top line"]
```

**Real-world examples:** ON/OFF thermostats, bang-bang controllers, space thrusters, electromagnetic relays.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

For sinusoidal input $e(t) = X\sin\omega t$, the output is:

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases}+M, & 0 < \omega t < \pi \\ -M, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi\end{cases}
$$

The Fourier series of this square wave (as derived in Section 3) is:

$$
y(t) = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t)
$$

Fundamental component: $y_1(t) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}\sin\omega t$, so $B_1 = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}$.

**Describing function:**

$$
N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}
$$

**Negative reciprocal:**

$$
-\frac{1}{N(X)} = -\frac{\pi X}{4M}
$$

This is a point on the **negative real axis** of the $G(j\omega)$ plane. As $X$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$, $-\frac{1}{N(X)}$ traces the entire negative real axis from $0$ to $-\infty$.

**Limit cycle condition:**

A sustained oscillation (limit cycle) exists if and only if the Nyquist plot $G(j\omega)$ intersects the negative real axis at some point $-a$ ($a > 0$) for a real frequency $\omega = \omega^\star$:

$$
G(j\omega^\star) = -\frac{1}{N(X^\star)} = -\frac{\pi X^\star}{4M}
$$

The amplitude is then:

$$
X^\star = -\frac{4M}{\pi}\,\operatorname{Re}\!\big\{G(j\omega^\star)\big\}
$$

provided $\operatorname{Re}\{G(j\omega^\star)\} < 0$ and $\operatorname{Im}\{G(j\omega^\star)\} = 0$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** List any **four** common nonlinearities encountered in control systems. For each, state whether its describing function is real or complex, and whether it is amplitude- and/or frequency-dependent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ideal relay with magnitude $M = 5$ is in cascade with a linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ in a unity feedback loop. The Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ crosses the negative real axis at the point $-0.5$ for $\omega = 2$ rad/s. Predict the amplitude and frequency of the limit cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

| # | Nonlinearity | Real / Complex | Amplitude-Dependent? | Frequency-Dependent? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Relay | Real | Yes ($N = 4M/\pi X$) | No |
| 2 | Saturation (Sat) | Real | Yes (piecewise) | No |
| 3 | Dead Zone (DZ) | Real | Yes (piecewise) | No |
| 4 | Relay with Hysteresis (Relay + Dead Zone) | Complex | Yes | No (but phase depends on hysteresis width) |
| 5 | Quantization | Real | Yes (piecewise, with jumps) | No |
| 6 | Coulomb Friction | Real | Yes (constant magnitude) | No |
| 7 | Backlash (Play) | Complex | Yes | No (phase depends on backlash width) |

> **Note for KTU:** Memoryless nonlinearities produce **real** describing functions; nonlinearities with **memory** (hysteresis, backlash) produce **complex** DFs.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Ideal relay: $M = 5$.
- Plant: $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$.
- Nyquist crossing: $G(j2) = -0.5$ (real, negative).

**Step 1 — Write the negative reciprocal of the DF.**

$$
-\frac{1}{N(X)} = -\frac{\pi X}{4M} = -\frac{\pi X}{20}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the limit cycle condition.**

At the crossing, $G(j\omega^\star) = -\dfrac{1}{N(X^\star)}$:

$$
-0.5 = -\frac{\pi X^\star}{20}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $X^\star$.**

$$
X^\star = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{\pi} = \frac{10}{\pi} \approx 3.183
$$

**Step 4 — State the frequency.**

The crossing occurs at $\omega^\star = 2$ rad/s.

**Result:** The system exhibits a **sustained limit cycle** with amplitude $\boxed{X^\star \approx 3.183}$ and frequency $\boxed{\omega^\star = 2 \text{ rad/s}}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $A_0$:** Students often write $A_0 \ne 0$ for the relay — wrong; the average of a symmetric square wave is **zero**. *(-1 mark)*
> 2. **Conflating DF for relay with hysteresis and ideal relay:** Hysteresis introduces a phase shift, making $N(X)$ complex. The **ideal** relay DF is **always real**. *(-2 marks)*
> 3. **Including $\omega$ in the DF expression:** The ideal relay is memoryless, so $N(X)$ is **frequency-independent**. *(-1 mark)*
> 4. **Sign error in the negative reciprocal:** It is $-\dfrac{1}{N(X)}$, not $+\dfrac{1}{N(X)}$. Getting this wrong flips the entire limit cycle conclusion. *(-2 marks)*
> 5. **Not verifying both real AND imaginary parts of $G(j\omega)$:** A limit cycle needs $\operatorname{Im}\{G(j\omega^\star)\} = 0$ AND $\operatorname{Re}\{G(j\omega^\star)\} = -\frac{\pi X^\star}{4M}$ at the *same* $\omega^\star$. *(-1 mark)*
> 6. **Confusing the input amplitude $X$ with the input peak $\hat{e}$:** They are the same, but students sometimes use $X$ as the RMS value. The DF is defined with the **peak** amplitude. *(-1 mark)*
> 7. **Dropping the factor of 4 in the square wave's fundamental:** The correct first-harmonic amplitude is $\dfrac{4M}{\pi}$, not $\dfrac{2M}{\pi}$ (which is the half-wave-rectified value). *(-2 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Ideal Relay Describing Function**

- [ ] **Definition:** The ideal relay is a two-level, memoryless, odd-symmetric nonlinearity: $y = M\operatorname{sgn}(e)$.
- [ ] **Sinusoidal input:** $e(t) = X\sin\omega t$ produces a **square wave** output of amplitude $M$ and period $2\pi/\omega$.
- [ ] **Fourier series of the output:** $y(t) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}\sum_{n\text{ odd}}\dfrac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}$ — only **odd harmonics**, only **sine terms**.
- [ ] **Fundamental amplitude:** $B_1 = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}$ (independent of $X$ and $\omega$).
- [ ] **Describing function:** $\boxed{N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}}$ — **real, amplitude-dependent, frequency-independent**.
- [ ] **Negative reciprocal:** $-\dfrac{1}{N(X)} = -\dfrac{\pi X}{4M}$ — lies on the **negative real axis**, traced as $X$ goes from $0$ to $\infty$.
- [ ] **Limit cycle condition:** $G(j\omega^\star) = -\dfrac{1}{N(X^\star)}$ with $\operatorname{Im}\{G(j\omega^\star)\} = 0$ and $\operatorname{Re}\{G(j\omega^\star)\} < 0$.
- [ ] **Amplitude formula:** $X^\star = -\dfrac{4M}{\pi}\operatorname{Re}\{G(j\omega^\star)\}$.
- [ ] **Asymptotic behavior:** $N(X) \to \infty$ as $X \to 0^{+}$; $N(X) \to 0$ as $X \to \infty$.
- [ ] **DF phase:** $\phi_1 = 0$ for the ideal relay (unlike the relay-with-hysteresis, where $\phi_1 \ne 0$).
- [ ] **Key assumption:** The linear part $G(s)$ must be sufficiently low-pass to filter out the 3rd, 5th, ... harmonics.
- [ ] **Why "ideal"?** No dead zone, no saturation slope, no hysteresis — pure two-level switching.
- [ ] **Common confusion:** The output $y(t)$ is **not** a sinusoid; the DF replaces the nonlinearity with a **gain that produces only the fundamental** as if it were a sinusoid.
- [ ] **Stability intuition:** If $-\frac{1}{N(X)}$ lies **inside** the encirclements of $G(j\omega)$, the limit cycle is **unstable**; if **outside**, the limit cycle is **stable** (use the Loeb criterion).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relay with Deadzone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relay with Deadzone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Relay with Deadzone** (also termed a **three-position relay** or **relay with dead-band**) is a static non-linear element whose output is a discontinuous, piecewise-constant function of the input error signal, characterized by a central region of insensitivity termed the *deadzone*.

Mathematically, the static input–output characteristic is described by:

$$
y(t) =
\begin{cases}
+M, & e(t) > \Delta \\
0, & \vert e(t) \vert \leq \Delta \\
-M, & e(t) < -\Delta
\end{cases}
$$

where:
- $M > 0$ is the constant relay output magnitude.
- $\Delta > 0$ is the **half-width of the deadzone**.
- $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$ is the actuating error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** This topic falls under *"Analysis of common non-linearities using the Describing Function (DF) method and Phase-plane method."* Relay with deadzone is a **symmetric, single-valued, position-independent non-linearity** and is inherently *memoryless* and *time-invariant*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Light Switch with Stuck Contacts"

Imagine a wall-mounted light switch in an old house. The switch has a small physical "slack" — you have to push it at least **2 mm** to the right before the light turns on, and at least **2 mm** to the left before it turns off. If your finger pushes it less than 2 mm in either direction, **nothing happens** — the light stays in its current state (the "deadzone").

- The **light ON state** = $+M$ (relay saturated positive).
- The **light OFF state** = $-M$ (relay saturated negative).
- The **2 mm slack zone** = $\pm \Delta$ (deadzone where output is $0$).

This mechanical insensitivity is precisely the engineering motivation for the deadzone: to **reject noise** and **avoid chattering** caused by small oscillations around the equilibrium point.

## 1.3 Engineering Motivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why introduce a deadzone?**
> 1. **Noise immunity:** Small sensor noise (e.g., thermistor jitter) will not trigger actuator switching.
> 2. **Chatter suppression:** Prevents rapid ON/OFF cycling near setpoint in on-off thermostats, hydraulic valves, and bang-bang servos.
> 3. **Component protection:** Limits mechanical wear in relays, contactors, and solenoid valves.
> 4. **Steady-state error tolerance:** A well-designed deadzone trades off a small steady-state error for reduced limit-cycle amplitude.

The deadzone half-width is typically a fixed design parameter, often expressed in **per cent of the input span** (e.g., $\Delta = 2\% \, E_{FS}$ where $E_{FS}$ is full-scale error).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristic Graph

The relay with deadzone partitions the input space into three discrete regions:

| Region | Condition | Output | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Positive saturation | $e > \Delta$ | $y = +M$ | Actuator fully energized in one direction |
| **Deadzone** | $\vert e \vert \leq \Delta$ | $y = 0$ | Actuator receives no command — coasting |
| Negative saturation | $e < -\Delta$ | $y = -M$ | Actuator fully energized in opposite direction |

The output is **discontinuous** at $e = \pm \Delta$ (jump discontinuities of magnitude $M$), and **identically zero** within the central band. This makes the element a **discontinuous non-linearity** (category: *piecewise-constant / relay-type*).

## 2.2 Classification of the Non-Linearity

| Property | Classification |
|---|---|
| Symmetry | Odd-symmetric about origin |
| Memory | Memoryless (static) |
| Slope | Slope = 0 inside; discontinuous at boundaries |
| Time-variance | Time-invariant |
| Type | **Discontinuous**, **non-linear**, **piecewise-constant** |
| Hysteresis? | **No** (single-valued) — distinguishes it from relay with hysteresis |

## 2.3 Describing Function Concept (Why it works)

The Describing Function (DF) is the **first-harmonic approximation** of the non-linear element's response to a sinusoidal input. For a memoryless, odd-symmetric non-linearity driven by $e(t) = E \sin(\omega t)$, the output $y(t)$ is an **odd, periodic, half-wave symmetric** function of $\omega t$. Therefore, the fundamental component lies entirely in the **sine quadrature**, and the DF reduces to a **real-valued function of amplitude** $E$ alone:

$$
N(E) = \frac{B_1}{E}, \qquad B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\tau) \sin(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

where $\tau = \omega t$ is the normalized phase.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static output | $y = M \, \text{sgn}(e)$ if $\vert e\vert > \Delta$, else $0$ | Dimensionless (normalized form) |
| 2 | Describing function | $N(E) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi E} \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{E}\right)^{2}}$ | $E > \Delta$ (otherwise $N=0$) |
| 3 | DF when $E \leq \Delta$ | $N(E) = 0$ | Insensitive region |
| 4 | Limiting form $E \to \infty$ | $N(E) \to \dfrac{4M}{\pi E}$ | Recovers ideal relay DF (no deadzone) |
| 5 | Limiting form $E \to \Delta^{+}$ | $N(E) \to 0$ | Deadzone completely suppresses fundamental |
| 6 | Maximum DF | $N_{\max} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}\, M}{\pi \Delta} \approx \dfrac{1.1026\, M}{\Delta}$ | Occurs at $E = \dfrac{2\Delta}{\sqrt{3}}$ |
| 7 | Self-sustained oscillation | $G(j\omega) = -\dfrac{1}{N(E)}$ | Closed-loop characteristic equation |
| 8 | Equivalent gain (slope sense) | $k_{eq} = \dfrac{dy}{de}\bigg\vert_{\text{rms sense}}$ | $\propto N(E)$ for sinusoidal test |
| 9 | Phase-plane switching lines | $e = +\Delta, \quad e = -\Delta$ | Vertical lines in $(e, \dot{e})$ plane |
| 10 | Steady-state error bound | $\vert e_{ss}\vert \leq \Delta$ | Forced by deadzone (no integrator in NL block) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - In **hydraulic servo-valve positioners**, deadzone prevents valve hunting.
> - In **HVAC thermostats**, deadzone is essential to prevent compressor short-cycling.
> - In **missile autopilot bang-bang control**, deadzone-shaped switching improves fuel efficiency.
> - In **sliding-mode controllers**, the deadzone forms the *boundary layer* for chattering reduction.

## 2.5 Logical Steps in Solving a DF Problem (KTU Approach)

1. **Identify the non-linearity** — confirm it's a single-valued, memoryless relay with deadzone (no hysteresis).
2. **Apply a sinusoidal test signal** $e(t) = E \sin(\omega t)$.
3. **Find switching instants** $\omega t_1 = \sin^{-1}(\Delta / E)$.
4. **Sketch the output waveform** — show $+M$ pulse, zero gaps, and $-M$ pulse over one period.
5. **Compute the first Fourier sine coefficient** $B_1$ by integrating the product $y \sin(\omega t)$.
6. **Form the DF** $N(E) = B_1 / E$.
7. **Plot $-1/N(E)$** versus $E$ on the complex plane (it lies on the negative real axis).
8. **Superimpose the Nyquist plot** of $G(j\omega)$ and locate intersections — these are *candidate limit cycles*.
9. **Test stability of each limit cycle** using the *encirclement / perturbation* criterion.
10. **Conclude** — report amplitude $E^{*}$ and frequency $\omega^{*}$ of any stable limit cycle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Describing Function $N(E)$

### Step 1 — Apply the test sinusoid

We inject a sinusoidal error signal:

$$
e(t) = E \sin(\omega t), \qquad E > 0
$$

The relay's static characteristic determines when the output switches. The switching angle $\omega t_1$ corresponds to the instant when $e(t)$ first exceeds $+\Delta$:

$$
E \sin(\omega t_1) = \Delta
$$

Solving for the switching angle (using the principal branch in $[0, \pi/2]$):

$$
\omega t_1 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right) \quad \text{for } E > \Delta
$$

### Step 2 — Construct the output waveform $y(t)$

Tracking the input trajectory over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$:

- From $\omega t = 0$ to $\omega t_1$: $e < \Delta$ → output $y = 0$.
- From $\omega t_1$ to $\pi - \omega t_1$: $e > \Delta$ → output $y = +M$.
- From $\pi - \omega t_1$ to $\pi + \omega t_1$: $\vert e \vert \leq \Delta$ → output $y = 0$.
- From $\pi + \omega t_1$ to $2\pi - \omega t_1$: $e < -\Delta$ → output $y = -M$.
- From $2\pi - \omega t_1$ to $2\pi$: returns to $y = 0$ and the pattern repeats.

### Step 3 — Compute the fundamental Fourier coefficient $B_1$

For a memoryless, odd-symmetric non-linearity, only the $B_1$ coefficient is non-zero. Using the standard Fourier series form with $\tau = \omega t$:

$$
B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\tau) \sin(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

By half-wave symmetry ($y(\tau + \pi) = -y(\tau)$), this simplifies to:

$$
B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} y(\tau) \sin(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

Substituting the piecewise output:

$$
B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\omega t_1} (0) \sin(\tau) \, d\tau + \int_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1} (M) \sin(\tau) \, d\tau \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral explicitly

The middle integral evaluates as follows:

$$
\int_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1} M \sin(\tau) \, d\tau = M \left[ -\cos(\tau) \right]_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1}
$$

$$
= M \left[ -\cos(\pi - \omega t_1) - (-\cos(\omega t_1)) \right]
$$

$$
= M \left[ \cos(\omega t_1) + \cos(\omega t_1) \right] \quad \text{(since } \cos(\pi - x) = -\cos(x)\text{)}
$$

$$
= 2M \cos(\omega t_1)
$$

### Step 5 — Form the final expression for $B_1$

$$
B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot 2M \cos(\omega t_1) = \frac{4M}{\pi} \cos(\omega t_1)
$$

Substituting $\cos(\omega t_1) = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2(\omega t_1)} = \sqrt{1 - (\Delta / E)^2}$:

$$
B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi} \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^{2}}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Describing Function

$$
N(E) = \frac{B_1}{E} = \frac{4M}{\pi E} \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^{2}}, \qquad E > \Delta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This DF is **purely real** and **positive** — the relay with deadzone introduces **no phase shift** to the fundamental component (it only modifies the *gain*). This is a key exam-relevant property.

## 3.2 Limit Cycle Condition (Harmonic Balance)

For a unity-feedback closed loop with linear plant $G(s)$ and the relay non-linearity $N(E)$ in the forward path, the describing-function approximation reduces the loop equation to:

$$
1 + N(E) \, G(j\omega) = 0
$$

Rearranging to expose the negative reciprocal:

$$
G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(E)} = -\frac{\pi E}{4M \sqrt{1 - \left(\Delta / E\right)^{2}}}
$$

The RHS is a **negative real number** whose magnitude varies from $0$ (as $E \to \Delta^+$) to $\infty$ (as $E \to \infty$), reaching a **minimum negative value** at $E = 2\Delta/\sqrt{3}$:

$$
\left| -\frac{1}{N(E)} \right|_{\min} = -\frac{\pi \Delta}{2\sqrt{3}\,M}
$$

A stable limit cycle exists if and only if the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ **intersects the negative real axis** at a point whose magnitude is **at least** $\pi \Delta / (2\sqrt{3}\,M)$ away from the origin.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Limit Cycle Computation

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A unity-feedback system has linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$ preceded by a relay with deadzone having $M = 1$ and $\Delta = 0.2$. Predict the amplitude and frequency of any self-sustained oscillation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Compute the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ and find its intersection with the negative real axis.

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega + 1)(j\omega + 2)} = \frac{10}{-3\omega^2 + j\omega(2 - \omega^2)}
$$

Rationalizing the denominator:

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{10 \left[ -3\omega^2 - j\omega(2 - \omega^2) \right]}{9\omega^4 + \omega^2(2 - \omega^2)^2}
$$

For the imaginary part to vanish, we need $2 - \omega^2 = 0$, giving:

$$
\omega^* = \sqrt{2} \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2** — Evaluate $G(j\omega^*)$:

$$
G(j\sqrt{2}) = \frac{10}{-3(2) + 0j} = \frac{10}{-6} = -\frac{5}{3} \approx -1.667
$$

**Step 3** — Apply the harmonic balance condition:

$$
-\frac{1}{N(E^*)} = -\frac{5}{3}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{N(E^*)} = \frac{5}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N(E^*) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6
$$

**Step 4** — Solve for $E^*$ from the DF equation:

$$
\frac{4M}{\pi E^*} \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E^*}\right)^{2}} = 0.6
$$

With $M = 1, \Delta = 0.2$:

$$
\frac{4}{\pi E^*} \sqrt{1 - \frac{0.04}{(E^*)^2}} = 0.6
$$

Letting $x = E^*$ and squaring both sides:

$$
\frac{16}{\pi^2 x^2} \left( 1 - \frac{0.04}{x^2} \right) = 0.36
$$

Let $u = x^2$:

$$
\frac{16}{\pi^2 u} \left( 1 - \frac{0.04}{u} \right) = 0.36
$$

$$
\frac{16}{\pi^2} \left( \frac{1}{u} - \frac{0.04}{u^2} \right) = 0.36
$$

Multiplying by $u^2$:

$$
\frac{16}{\pi^2} (u - 0.04) = 0.36 \, u^2
$$

Let $K = 16/\pi^2 \approx 1.6211$:

$$
1.6211 \, u - 0.0648 = 0.36 \, u^2
$$

$$
0.36 u^2 - 1.6211 u + 0.0648 = 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$
u = \frac{1.6211 \pm \sqrt{(1.6211)^2 - 4(0.36)(0.0648)}}{2(0.36)}
$$

$$
u = \frac{1.6211 \pm \sqrt{2.6280 - 0.0933}}{0.72} = \frac{1.6211 \pm \sqrt{2.5347}}{0.72}
$$

$$
u = \frac{1.6211 \pm 1.5921}{0.72}
$$

Two roots:

- $u_1 = (1.6211 + 1.5921)/0.72 = 3.2132/0.72 = 4.463 \Rightarrow x_1 = 2.113$
- $u_2 = (1.6211 - 1.5921)/0.72 = 0.0290/0.72 = 0.0403 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0.2007 \approx \Delta$

**Step 5** — Stability check using perturbation:

- $E_1 = 2.113 > \Delta$ (valid). Perturbing $E \uparrow$: $N \downarrow \Rightarrow 1/(N) \uparrow$; the operating point moves to a value where $\vert G \vert < 1/N$, so the system is **stable** and oscillations decay.
- $E_2 = 0.2007 \approx \Delta$ (unstable — just at the deadzone boundary).

> **Conclusion:** No stable self-sustained oscillation is predicted for this specific parameter set, because the plant's critical gain $5/3$ exceeds the maximum inverse DF $1/N_{\max} = \pi\Delta/(2\sqrt{3}M) = \pi(0.2)/(2\sqrt{3}) \approx 0.1814$. The deadzone is "wide enough" to suppress limit cycling.

## 3.4 Phase-Plane Analysis for a Second-Order Plant

Consider a relay with deadzone driving a double-integrator-like plant:

$$
\ddot{e} + a \dot{e} = u(t)
$$

where $u(t) = M, 0, -M$ depending on the region. In the phase plane $(e, \dot{e})$:

- The **switching lines** $e = +\Delta$ and $e = -\Delta$ partition the plane into three vertical strips.
- In the central strip ($\vert e \vert \leq \Delta$): $u = 0$ → trajectory follows the natural unforced system dynamics.
- In the right strip ($e > \Delta$): $u = +M$ → trajectory bends with net "acceleration" $+M$.
- In the left strip ($e < -\Delta$): $u = -M$ → trajectory bends with net "acceleration" $-M$.

If the plant has damping, trajectories within the deadzone spiral toward the equilibrium region; if the plant is undamped, they may oscillate perpetually — leading to a **dead-zone-induced limit cycle** that resembles a *trapezoidal trajectory* in the phase plane.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Closed-Loop System

This diagram maps how the relay with deadzone interfaces with the linear plant and the unity feedback loop, and shows the describing-function analysis pathway.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reference Input r]:::input
    SUM[Comparator Summing Junction]:::node
    ERR[Error Signal e of t]:::sig
    NL[Relay with Deadzone N of E]:::nlblock
    U[Relay Output u of t plus or minus M or 0]:::sig
    PLANT[Linear Plant G of s]:::plant
    OUT[System Output c of t]:::output
    FB[Unity Feedback Path]:::fb
    DF[Describing Function Calculator]:::df
    NYQ[Nyquist Plot of G of j omega]:::plot
    NR[Negative Reciprocal of 1 over N of E]:::plot
    LC[Limit Cycle Detection and Stability Test]:::node

    R --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR
    ERR --> NL
    NL --> U
    U --> PLANT
    PLANT --> OUT
    OUT --> FB
    FB --> SUM

    NL -.-> DF
    PLANT -.-> NYQ
    DF -.-> NR
    NYQ --> LC
    NR --> LC

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef nlblock fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#274e13,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#4d0099,color:#000
    classDef df fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#994d00,color:#000
    classDef plot fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Static Characteristic and Output Waveform Mapping

This diagram shows the input–output mapping and the resulting waveforms for a sinusoidal excitation — a critical visual aid for deriving the describing function.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Static Characteristic y versus e]:::chart
    B[Sinusoidal Input e of t = E sin omega t]:::sig
    C[Switching Angle omega t1 = arcsin Delta over E]:::node
    D[Output Waveform y of t]:::sig
    E[Positive Pulse of magnitude M between omega t1 and pi minus omega t1]:::pulse
    F[Deadzone Gap of duration 2 omega t1]:::gap
    G[Negative Pulse of magnitude minus M between pi plus omega t1 and 2 pi minus omega t1]:::pulse
    H[Fourier Sine Coefficient B1 Computation]:::df
    I[Describing Function N of E equals 4M over pi E times sqrt of 1 minus Delta over E squared]:::result

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F
    D --> G
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I

    classDef chart fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef pulse fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#274e13,color:#000
    classDef gap fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#4d0099,color:#000
    classDef df fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#994d00,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#ccff99,stroke:#336600,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Phase-Plane Partition and Trajectory Sketch

This block-level schematic illustrates the partition of the phase plane by the switching lines and the typical trajectory flow for a damped second-order plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Phase Plane Coordinates e on horizontal, e dot on vertical]:::plane
    L1[Switching Line e equals plus Delta, vertical boundary]:::line
    L2[Switching Line e equals minus Delta, vertical boundary]:::line
    R1[Region e greater than plus Delta, u equals plus M, accelerated right]:::region
    C[Region absolute value of e less than or equal to Delta, u equals 0, coasting band]:::region
    R2[Region e less than minus Delta, u equals minus M, accelerated left]:::region
    T1[Trajectory in R1 bends rightward toward switching line]:::traj
    T2[Trajectory in C glides along natural dynamics toward equilibrium]:::traj
    T3[Trajectory in R2 bends leftward toward switching line]:::traj
    LC[Possible Closed Orbit encircling deadzone, limit cycle]:::orbit

    P --> L1
    P --> L2
    L1 --> R1
    L2 --> R2
    L1 --> C
    L2 --> C
    R1 --> T1
    C --> T2
    R2 --> T3
    T1 --> LC
    T2 --> LC
    T3 --> LC

    classDef plane fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333333,color:#000
    classDef line fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef region fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef traj fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#274e13,color:#000
    classDef orbit fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#994d00,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Describing Function Magnitude vs Amplitude — Graphical Insight

The following block-diagram summarises the three critical regions of the $N(E)$ curve that the examiner expects you to mark on a sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Region 1, E less than Delta, N equals 0, dead silence zone]:::r1
    X2[Region 2, Delta less than E less than 2 Delta over sqrt 3, N rises sharply]:::r2
    X3[Region 3, E greater than 2 Delta over sqrt 3, N decays as 4M over pi E]:::r3
    PEAK[Peak Value N max equals 2 sqrt 3 M over pi Delta at E equals 2 Delta over sqrt 3]:::peak
    IDEAL[Asymptote, ideal relay DF 4M over pi E as Delta approaches 0]:::asym

    X1 --> X2
    X2 --> X3
    X2 --> PEAK
    X3 --> IDEAL

    classDef r1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef r2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef r3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#274e13,color:#000
    classDef peak fill:#ccff99,stroke:#336600,color:#000
    classDef asym fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#4d0099,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define *relay with deadzone*. Sketch its static input–output characteristic and mention one practical situation where it is deliberately introduced.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *relay with deadzone* is a static, discontinuous, memoryless non-linear element whose output takes values $+M$, $0$, or $-M$ depending on whether the error $e(t)$ lies above $+\Delta$, inside the band $\vert e\vert \leq \Delta$, or below $-\Delta$, respectively.

The static characteristic consists of a horizontal segment of zero output spanning $[-\Delta, +\Delta]$ on the input axis, flanked by two step jumps of magnitude $M$ at the boundaries. **Practical example:** A room thermostat with a 1°C deadzone prevents the compressor from short-cycling when the room temperature oscillates around the setpoint.

*(Marking split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sketch, 1 mark for practical use.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Why does the describing function of a relay with deadzone vanish for input amplitudes $E \leq \Delta$? What is the physical meaning?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For $E \leq \Delta$, the sinusoid never crosses the deadzone boundaries, so the relay output remains identically zero throughout the entire cycle. Since the fundamental Fourier coefficient $B_1$ of an all-zero output is zero, $N(E) = 0/E = 0$. Physically, this means the deadzone completely *masks* small input signals — the system becomes insensitive to errors smaller than $\Delta$, and the closed loop effectively behaves as an **open-loop plant** for those perturbations.

*(Marking split: 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for physical interpretation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

### Question A — Describing Function and Limit Cycle Analysis

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the describing function of a relay with deadzone having output levels $\pm M$ and deadzone half-width $\Delta$. Sketch $N(E)$ versus $E$ and clearly indicate the limiting cases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A unity-feedback system has linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$ preceded by a relay with deadzone ($M = 2$, $\Delta = 0.5$). Determine the value of $K$ above which a stable limit cycle can exist, and compute the corresponding amplitude and frequency.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Step-by-step derivation (7 marks):**

1. Apply test signal $e(t) = E\sin(\omega t)$ with $E > \Delta$. *(0.5 mark)*
2. Switching angle $\omega t_1 = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/E)$. *(1 mark)*
3. Output waveform construction: $+M$ pulse of width $\pi - 2\omega t_1$, two zero-gaps, $-M$ pulse. *(1.5 marks)*
4. Compute $B_1 = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - (\Delta/E)^2}$. *(2 marks)*
5. Form $N(E) = B_1/E = \dfrac{4M}{\pi E}\sqrt{1 - (\Delta/E)^2}$. *(1 mark)*
6. Sketch with limits: $N \to 0$ as $E \to \Delta^+$, peak at $E = 2\Delta/\sqrt{3}$, decay as $4M/(\pi E)$ for large $E$. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Step-by-step solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1** — Nyquist intersection with negative real axis:

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+4)} = \frac{K}{j\omega(-\omega^2 + 5j\omega + 4)}
$$

$$
= \frac{K}{-4\omega^2 + j\omega(4 - \omega^2)} \cdot \frac{1}{j}
$$

After rationalization, the imaginary part vanishes when $4 - \omega^2 = 0$, giving:

$$
\omega^* = 2 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2** — Evaluate at $\omega^* = 2$:

$$
G(j2) = \frac{K}{-4(4) + 0j} = -\frac{K}{16}
$$

**Step 3** — For a limit cycle to exist, the Nyquist plot must reach the segment occupied by $-1/N(E)$ on the negative real axis. The minimum (most negative) value of $-1/N(E)$ is:

$$
\left|-\frac{1}{N(E)}\right|_{\min} = \frac{\pi \Delta}{2\sqrt{3}\,M} = \frac{\pi(0.5)}{2\sqrt{3}(2)} = \frac{\pi}{8\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.2268
$$

The Nyquist plot must cross the real axis at a point **more negative** than this, i.e. $\vert G(j2)\vert = K/16 > 0.2268$, giving:

$$
K > 16 \times 0.2268 \approx 3.629
$$

**Step 4** — For $K = 4$ (a typical case), set $K/16 = 1/N(E^*)$:

$$
\frac{4}{16} = 0.25 = \frac{1}{N(E^*)} \Rightarrow N(E^*) = 4
$$

$$
\frac{4M}{\pi E^*}\sqrt{1 - (\Delta/E^*)^2} = 4
$$

With $M = 2, \Delta = 0.5$:

$$
\frac{8}{\pi E^*}\sqrt{1 - \frac{0.25}{(E^*)^2}} = 4
$$

$$
\sqrt{1 - \frac{0.25}{(E^*)^2}} = \frac{\pi E^*}{2}
$$

Squaring and letting $u = (E^*)^2$:

$$
1 - \frac{0.25}{u} = \frac{\pi^2 u}{4}
$$

$$
\frac{\pi^2}{4} u^2 - u + 0.25 = 0
$$

$$
2.467 u^2 - u + 0.25 = 0
$$

$$
u = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 2.467}}{4.934} = \frac{1 \pm j\sqrt{1.467}}{4.934}
$$

The discriminant is negative → no real solution for $K = 4$. Recheck: for $K = 4$, $-1/N$ would need to equal $0.25$, meaning $1/N = 0.25$, i.e. $N = 4$. But $N_{\max} = 2\sqrt{3}M/(\pi \Delta) = 2\sqrt{3}(2)/(\pi \cdot 0.5) \approx 4.41$, so $N = 4$ is achievable. The correct algebraic path is:

$$
\frac{2}{\pi E^*} \sqrt{1 - 0.25/(E^*)^2} = 1
$$

$$
\frac{4}{\pi^2 (E^*)^2} \left(1 - \frac{0.25}{(E^*)^2}\right) = 1
$$

$$
1 - \frac{0.25}{(E^*)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 (E^*)^2}{4}
$$

With $u = (E^*)^2$: $\pi^2 u^2/4 - u + 0.25 = 0 \Rightarrow 2.467 u^2 - u + 0.25 = 0$ — same equation; recompute discriminant:

$$
\Delta_{disc} = 1 - 4(2.467)(0.25) = 1 - 2.467 = -1.467
$$

Negative → **no real solution** at $K = 4$. This means $K = 4$ yields *no intersection*. The minimum $K$ is therefore the value at which the Nyquist locus **just touches** the minimum of $-1/N(E)$:

$$
K_{\min} = 16 \times \frac{\pi}{8\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 3.628
$$

For $K > K_{\min}$, the equation admits real solutions. Take $K = 5$:

$$
\frac{K}{16} = \frac{5}{16} = 0.3125 = \frac{1}{N(E^*)}
$$

$$
N(E^*) = 3.2
$$

By numerical solution of the DF equation with $M = 2, \Delta = 0.5$, the two valid amplitudes are $E^* \approx 0.82$ and $E^* \approx 0.95$, with the larger one being the **stable** limit cycle by the perturbation test.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $E > \Delta$ condition.** If you obtain a root $E^* \leq \Delta$, discard it — it is non-physical.
> 2. **Not performing the stability test.** A limit cycle is only "real" if it is *stable*. Always apply the perturbation argument.
> 3. **Mixing up $K_{\min}$ and $K_{\max}$.** There is no $K_{\max}$ for the existence of a limit cycle; the condition is $K > K_{\min}$.
> 4. **Missing the half-wave symmetry step.** The integral from $0$ to $\pi$ alone gives the correct $B_1$ only because of half-wave symmetry — state this explicitly.
> 5. **Sign errors in rationalizing** $G(j\omega)$ are the single most common source of arithmetic mistakes.

---

### Question B — Phase-Plane Analysis and Steady-State Error

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the phase-plane trajectories of the system $\ddot{c} + \dot{c} = u(t)$ where $u(t)$ is the output of a relay with deadzone ($M = 1$, $\Delta = 0.3$) controlling the position $c$ to track a step reference of magnitude $2$. Discuss the nature of the response.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A relay with deadzone ($M = 3$, $\Delta = 0.5$) is in cascade with a linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s+2}$ in a unity-feedback loop. Using the describing function method, comment on the existence of limit cycles and the resulting steady-state error.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Phase-plane analysis (7 marks):**

1. Define error $e = r - c$, so $\dot{e} = -\dot{c}$, $\ddot{e} = -\ddot{c}$. *(0.5 mark)*
2. Plant equation becomes $\ddot{e} + \dot{e} = -u(t)$. The switching lines are $e = \pm 0.3$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Region 1 ($e > 0.3$):** $u = +1 \Rightarrow \ddot{e} + \dot{e} = -1$. The phase trajectory has a slope $\dot{e}/(d\dot{e}/de) = \dot{e}/(-1 - \dot{e})$. Equilibrium at $\dot{e} = -1$. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Region 2 ($\vert e \vert \leq 0.3$):** $u = 0 \Rightarrow \ddot{e} + \dot{e} = 0$. Trajectories decay exponentially: $\dot{e}(t) = \dot{e}_0 \, e^{-t}$. Equilibrium at $\dot{e} = 0$. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Region 3 ($e < -0.3$):** $u = -1 \Rightarrow \ddot{e} + \dot{e} = +1$. Symmetric to Region 1. *(1.5 marks)*
6. **Response description:** The trajectory spirals towards the deadzone strip, enters it with $\dot{e} \neq 0$ (unless perfectly aligned), glides with exponential decay, and eventually settles within $\vert e \vert \leq 0.3$. **Steady-state error bound:** $\vert e_{ss} \vert \leq 0.3$. *(1 mark)*

**(b) DF analysis (7 marks):**

1. Linear plant $G(s) = 2/(s+2)$ is a **first-order lag** with DC gain $G(0) = 1$ and corner frequency $\omega_c = 2$ rad/s. *(1 mark)*
2. Frequency response: $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{2}{2 + j\omega} = \dfrac{2(2 - j\omega)}{4 + \omega^2}$. *(1 mark)*
3. Negative reciprocal locus: $-1/N(E)$ is purely real and negative, ranging from $0$ to $-\infty$ as $E$ decreases from $\infty$ to $\Delta^+$, with minimum magnitude $\pi\Delta/(2\sqrt{3}M) = \pi(0.5)/(2\sqrt{3}(3)) \approx 0.1512$. *(1.5 marks)*
4. The Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ lies in the **fourth quadrant** for $\omega > 0$, starting at $+1$ on the real axis and spiraling to origin. It **never crosses the negative real axis**. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Conclusion:** No intersection exists → **no limit cycle**. The system reaches a steady state within the deadzone. The steady-state error satisfies $\vert e_{ss} \vert \leq \Delta = 0.5$. *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes in this type of question:**
> - Drawing the Nyquist plot on the *wrong side* of the imaginary axis (first-order stable plant always has Nyquist in the right half of the s-plane for $\omega > 0$).
> - Forgetting to **state** the bound on steady-state error explicitly.
> - Confusing the negative reciprocal with the *negative inverse* — they are the same here only because $N$ is real, but the notation matters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Relay with Deadzone**

- **Definition:** Output $\in \{+M, 0, -M\}$; zero output band for $\vert e\vert \leq \Delta$.
- **Odd-symmetric, memoryless, time-invariant, single-valued, discontinuous non-linearity.**
- **Switching angles** for sinusoidal input: $\omega t_1 = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/E)$, with $E > \Delta$.
- **Master equation:**

$$
N(E) = \frac{4M}{\pi E}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^{2}}, \quad E > \Delta; \quad N(E) = 0, \quad E \leq \Delta
$$

- **Peak DF:** $N_{\max} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}\,M}{\pi \Delta}$ at $E = \dfrac{2\Delta}{\sqrt{3}}$.
- **Minimum $-1/N$:** $\dfrac{\pi \Delta}{2\sqrt{3}\,M}$ (most negative point of the negative reciprocal locus).
- **Limit cycle condition:** $G(j\omega) = -1/N(E)$ — graphically the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ must intersect the negative real axis at a point more negative than $-\pi\Delta/(2\sqrt{3}M)$.
- **Stability test:** Perturb $E$ above and below the candidate $E^*$; the direction of return determines stability.
- **Phase plane:** Switching lines at $e = \pm\Delta$ partition the plane into three strips; trajectories in the central strip follow unforced plant dynamics.
- **Steady-state error bound:** $\vert e_{ss} \vert \leq \Delta$ for a stable closed loop with this element.
- **Real-world uses:** Thermostats, hydraulic valves, bang-bang missile control, sliding-mode boundary layers, sensor noise filters.
- **Common pitfalls:** (1) Including the root $E \leq \Delta$ as a valid limit cycle, (2) Forgetting the stability test, (3) Drawing the negative reciprocal locus on the positive real axis, (4) Sign errors in rationalising $G(j\omega)$.
- **Distinguish carefully:**
  - *Relay with deadzone* (no hysteresis) vs. *relay with hysteresis* (two-valued at the boundary).
  - *Memoryless deadzone* (this element) vs. *backlash* (multi-valued, with memory).
  - *Describing function* (approximate, sinusoidal) vs. *phase plane* (exact, for 2nd order).

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"No phase shift from a real DF — the relay with deadzone only modulates gain."* Use this line in your answer to bag a quick conceptual mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Deadzone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple Deadzone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Simple Deadzone** (also called a **Dead Band** or **Neutral Zone**) is a static, memoryless, odd-symmetric nonlinearity in which the output remains **exactly zero** when the input magnitude lies within a threshold interval $[-d, +d]$, and then increases linearly (with slope $K$) once the input magnitude exceeds this threshold.

The defining mathematical relation for a **simple (symmetric) deadzone** of slope $K$ and half-width $d$ is:

$$
y(t) \;=\; \mathcal{D}\big[u(t)\big] \;=\;
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } \lvert u(t)\rvert \le d \\[4pt]
K\,\big[u(t) - d\,\mathrm{sgn}(u(t))\big], & \text{if } \lvert u(t)\rvert > d
\end{cases}
$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = input to the nonlinear element,
- $y(t)$ = output of the nonlinear element,
- $d$ = **half-width of the dead zone** (the input must exceed this magnitude to produce any output),
- $K$ = **linear (post-deadzone) slope** with units of (output unit) / (input unit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, deadzone is treated under *Static Nonlinearities* and analysed via the **Describing Function (DF)** method for predicting limit cycles. Students must clearly distinguish it from **relay (on-off)** and **saturation** nonlinearities — deadzone produces **zero output in a central band**, whereas saturation *limits* the output asymptotically and a relay jumps abruptly between two levels.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a stiff, rusty steering wheel on an old car.**
> When you turn the wheel a little (say less than 5° to the left or right), **nothing happens to the wheels** — the linkage is too loose / there is too much friction. Only after you rotate the wheel **past that "dead" angle** does the car actually start turning. After that threshold, the wheels respond **proportionally** to how much further you turn the wheel.

This everyday example is precisely what engineers call a **deadzone nonlinearity**:

| Real-World System | Input (Cause) | Dead-Zone Width | Output (Effect) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rusty steering wheel | Hand torque on wheel | Slack in linkage | Car wheel angle |
| Hydraulic servo valve | Spool displacement | Valve overlap | Fluid flow |
| DC motor under static friction | Armature voltage | Coulomb friction | Shaft rotation |
| Diode (small-signal regime) | Forward voltage | Cut-in voltage (~0.6 V) | Forward current |

In every case, the system **ignores** small stimuli and **responds linearly** only once a critical threshold is crossed.

---

## 1.3 Key Characteristics (Board-Exam Essentials)

- **Odd symmetric:** $\mathcal{D}(-u) = -\mathcal{D}(u)$ — the curve is symmetric about the origin.
- **Multi-valued at the break-points:** at $u = \pm d$, the output is conventionally taken as $0$.
- **Memoryless / Static:** output at any instant depends only on the present input, not on past values.
- **Single-valued in each branch:** once $\lvert u\rvert > d$, the $y$–$u$ relation is linear and invertible.
- **Contains no energy storage** — it is a pure algebraic nonlinearity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The deadzone is the **simplest, most frequently-occurring nonlinearity** in mechanical actuation systems. The KTU examiner expects you to **sketch it, write its equation, and derive its describing function** — these three tasks almost always co-appear in ESE questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

A deadzone element behaves like a **two-stage signal conditioner**:

1. **Stage 1 — Threshold Gating:** Compare $\lvert u(t)\rvert$ with the threshold $d$.
   - If $\lvert u(t)\rvert \le d$ → **gate is OFF**, force $y = 0$.
   - If $\lvert u(t)\rvert > d$ → **gate is ON**, proceed to Stage 2.

2. **Stage 2 — Linear Scaling:** Subtract the threshold magnitude (with correct sign) and multiply by slope $K$.
   - $y = K(u - d)$ for $u > +d$.
   - $y = K(u + d)$ for $u < -d$.

3. **Result:** A piecewise-linear, odd-symmetric, $S$-shaped (offset) characteristic centred at the origin.

The deadzone can be **mathematically modelled as a linear element of slope $K$ in series with a subtraction of the "lost" input** within the dead band. Equivalently:

$$
y(t) \;=\; K\,u(t) \;-\; \mathcal{D}_{\text{rect}}(u(t))
$$

where $\mathcal{D}_{\text{rect}}$ is the deadzone output interpreted as a *correction term*. This decomposition is useful when deriving the **Describing Function**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol / Expression | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dead-zone half-width | $d$ | Input units; $d > 0$ |
| 2 | Linear slope | $K$ | Output / Input |
| 3 | Input–output relation (1) | $y = 0$ | $\lvert u\rvert \le d$ |
| 4 | Input–output relation (2) | $y = K(u - d\,\text{sgn}\,u)$ | $\lvert u\rvert > d$ |
| 5 | Dead-zone break frequency | $\psi = \sin^{-1}(d/X)$ | $X > d$, radians |
| 6 | **Describing Function (real)** | $N(X) = K - \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\left[\psi + \dfrac{d}{X}\cos\psi\right]$ | $X > d$ |
| 7 | Describing Function (degenerate) | $N(X) = K$ | $X \to \infty$ |
| 8 | Describing Function (threshold) | $N(X) = 0$ | $X \le d$ |
| 9 | Phase of $N(X)$ | $0°$ (purely real) | Always |
| 10 | Odd-symmetry property | $N(X)$ real-valued | Confirms no phase lag |
| 11 | Equivalent gain (small signal) | $K_{\text{eq}} \to 0$ | $X \approx d$ |
| 12 | Equivalent gain (large signal) | $K_{\text{eq}} \to K$ | $X \gg d$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight for KTU:** Because the deadzone is **odd-symmetric**, its Describing Function is **purely real** (no imaginary part). Therefore, when seeking limit cycles via $-1/N(X)$ on the Nyquist plane, you only need to plot a **point on the negative real axis**, not a curve.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Deadzone Manifestation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic / pneumatic valves | Spool overlap (valve overlap) | Loss of low-flow resolution |
| DC motor drives | Coulomb + static friction | Steady-state error; stick-slip |
| Robotic joint actuators | Backlash in gear trains | Positional inaccuracy, chattering |
| Process control valves | Seat friction | Hysteresis in flow loop |
| Analog comparators | Threshold offset | Quantisation-like effect |
| Piezoelectric actuators | Hysteresis + creep | Tracking error in nano-positioning |

Understanding deadzone is essential for **compensator design** — a well-tuned controller must provide enough excitation to **escape the dead band** during transients, while not chattering the actuator in steady state.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation of the Describing Function for Deadzone

## 3.1 Setup and Assumption

Assume the input to the deadzone is a pure sinusoid:

$$
u(t) \;=\; X\sin(\omega t), \qquad X > 0
$$

For a meaningful describing-function analysis we require the input amplitude to **exceed the dead-zone half-width**:

$$
X > d
$$

Define the **break angle** $\psi$ as the value of $\omega t$ at which $\lvert u\rvert = d$:

$$
X\sin\psi = d \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\psi \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{X}\right)}, \qquad 0 < \psi < \dfrac{\pi}{2}
$$

## 3.2 Output Waveform in One Quarter Period

Within one positive quarter-cycle $0 \le \omega t \le \pi/2$, the output of the deadzone is:

$$
y(t) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
0, & 0 \le \omega t \le \psi \\[4pt]
K\bigl(X\sin(\omega t) - d\bigr), & \psi \le \omega t \le \dfrac{\pi}{2}
\end{cases}
$$

For the remaining half-cycle the waveform mirrors with negative sign (odd symmetry). The complete output is therefore a **clipped sinusoid** — sinusoid with the central portion removed and then reconnected linearly outside the dead band.

## 3.3 Describing Function Definition

The (single-tone) Describing Function is the complex ratio of the fundamental Fourier component of $y(t)$ to the input sinusoid:

$$
N(X) \;=\; \frac{Y_1}{X}\,e^{j\phi_1}, \qquad
\text{where } y(t) \approx Y_1\sin(\omega t + \phi_1)
$$

For an **odd-symmetric** nonlinearity, the output Fourier series contains only **odd sine harmonics**, so $Y_1\cos\phi_1 = 0$ and:

$$
N(X) \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi X}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\tau)\sin(\tau)\,d\tau, \qquad \tau \equiv \omega t
$$

Using the quarter-wave symmetry of the output:

$$
N(X) \;=\; \frac{4}{\pi X}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} y(\tau)\sin(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

## 3.4 Substitution of the Output Expression

$$
N(X) \;=\; \frac{4}{\pi X}\!\int_{\psi}^{\pi/2}\! K\bigl(X\sin\tau - d\bigr)\sin\tau \,d\tau
$$

Split the integral into two pieces:

$$
N(X) \;=\; \frac{4K}{\pi}\!\int_{\psi}^{\pi/2}\!\sin^{2}\tau \,d\tau \;-\; \frac{4Kd}{\pi X}\!\int_{\psi}^{\pi/2}\!\sin\tau \,d\tau
$$

## 3.5 Evaluating the Two Integrals

**Integral A:**

$$
\int_{\psi}^{\pi/2}\!\sin^{2}\tau \,d\tau \;=\; \int_{\psi}^{\pi/2}\!\frac{1 - \cos 2\tau}{2}\,d\tau
$$

$$
= \left[\frac{\tau}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\tau}{4}\right]_{\psi}^{\pi/2}
$$

$$
= \left(\frac{\pi}{4} - 0\right) - \left(\frac{\psi}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\psi}{4}\right)
$$

$$
= \frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\psi}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\psi}{4}
$$

**Integral B:**

$$
\int_{\psi}^{\pi/2}\!\sin\tau \,d\tau \;=\; \left[-\cos\tau\right]_{\psi}^{\pi/2}
$$

$$
= -\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) + \cos\psi
$$

$$
= 0 + \cos\psi \;=\; \cos\psi
$$

## 3.6 Combining the Results

Substituting back:

$$
N(X) \;=\; \frac{4K}{\pi}\!\left[\frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\psi}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\psi}{4}\right] - \frac{4Kd}{\pi X}\cos\psi
$$

$$
= K - \frac{2K\psi}{\pi} + \frac{K\sin 2\psi}{\pi} - \frac{4Kd\cos\psi}{\pi X}
$$

Apply the double-angle identity $\sin 2\psi = 2\sin\psi\cos\psi$ and recall $\sin\psi = d/X$:

$$
\frac{K\sin 2\psi}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{2K\sin\psi\cos\psi}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{2K(d/X)\cos\psi}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{2Kd\cos\psi}{\pi X}
$$

Therefore:

$$
N(X) \;=\; K - \frac{2K\psi}{\pi} + \frac{2Kd\cos\psi}{\pi X} - \frac{4Kd\cos\psi}{\pi X}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N(X) \;=\; K \;-\; \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\psi + \frac{d}{X}\cos\psi\right],\qquad \psi = \sin^{-1}\!\frac{d}{X},\; X > d\,}
$$

## 3.7 Limiting Behaviour (Verification)

**Case 1 — Very large input amplitude ($X \to \infty$):**
$\psi \to 0$ and $d/X \to 0$, so $N(X) \to K$. The deadzone becomes invisible; the element behaves like a pure linear gain.

**Case 2 — Input just above threshold ($X \to d^{+}$):**
$\psi \to \pi/2$, $\cos\psi \to 0$, so $N(X) \to K - \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{2} = 0$. The equivalent gain collapses — physically, the sinusoid spends most of its time inside the dead band.

**Case 3 — Input below threshold ($X \le d$):**
The output is identically zero, so $N(X) = 0$.

This monotonic rise from $0$ to $K$ as $X$ increases is the **hallmark signature** of a deadzone describing function.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Deadzone Input–Output Characteristic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputAxis["Input u(t)"]
        direction LR
        negD["u = -d"]
        zero["u = 0"]
        posD["u = +d"]
    end

    subgraph OutputCurve["Deadzone Output y = D(u)"]
        direction LR
        seg1["Dead region: y = 0 for |u| <= d"]
        seg2["Linear rise: y = K(u - d) for u > d"]
        seg3["Linear fall: y = K(u + d) for u < -d"]
    end

    negD --> seg3
    zero --> seg1
    posD --> seg2

    seg3 --- noteA["Slope K (negative branch)"]
    seg1 --- noteB["Flat segment of width 2d"]
    seg2 --- noteC["Slope K (positive branch)"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Deadzone

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inNode["Input u(t)"] --> absComp["Absolute value |u|"]
    absComp --> compNode{"Is |u| greater than d?"}

    compNode -->|NO| gateOff["Output gate = 0"]
    compNode -->|YES| gateOn["Output gate = 1"]

    gateOn --> subtractor["Subtract d sgn u from u"]
    subtractor --> gainBlock["Multiply by slope K"]
    gainBlock --> outSum

    gateOff --> outSum["Summing junction"]

    inNode --> sgnBlock["sgn(u)"]
    sgnBlock --> subtractor

    inNode --> outSum

    outSum --> outNode["Output y(t) = D(u)"]
```

## 4.3 Describing-Function Analysis Loop (Limit Cycle Search)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Reference r(t)"] --> E["Error e(t)"]
    E --> G["Linear plant G(s)"]
    G --> Y["Plant output y(t)"]
    Y --> FBN["Feedback node"]
    FBN --> N["Deadzone DF: N(X)"]
    N --> E

    noteOL["Open-loop TF = N(X) * G(jw)"]
    G -.- noteOL
    N -.- noteOL

    noteLC["Limit cycle if G(jw) = -1/N(X)"]
    noteOL -.- noteLC
```
