## 4.4 Plot of N(X) vs X/d — Equivalent Gain Characteristic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GainCurve["N(X)/K vs X/d for Deadzone"]
        direction LR
        p1["(1, 0) — at threshold: gain = 0"]
        p2["(2, ~0.32K)"]
        p3["(3, ~0.53K)"]
        p4["(4, ~0.66K)"]
        p5["(infinity, K) — saturation of equivalent gain"]
    end

    p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> p5
```

## 4.5 Nyquist Interpretation — Point on Negative Real Axis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Nq["Negative reciprocal -1/N(X) on Real Axis"]
        direction LR
        a["(-1/K, 0) as X -> infinity"]
        b["(-infinity, 0) as X -> d+"]
    end

    a -->|moves leftward| b
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Tip:** When asked to predict a limit cycle, students should:
> 1. Plot $G(j\omega)$ in the Nyquist plane.
> 2. Mark the **negative real axis segment** from $-1/K$ to $-\infty$ traversed by $-1/N(X)$.
> 3. Identify the **intersection point** — its $\omega$ and $X$ give the predicted limit cycle's frequency and amplitude.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the term "deadzone nonlinearity" with a suitable sketch. Mention two practical systems where deadzone occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A deadzone (or neutral zone) is a static nonlinearity in which the output is zero for inputs within a band $[-d, +d]$ and varies linearly with the input outside this band.

$$
y = \begin{cases} 0, & |u| \le d \\ K(u - d\,\text{sgn}\,u), & |u| > d \end{cases}
$$

**Sketch (1 mark):** Horizontal segment from $-d$ to $+d$ on the $u$-axis; two straight lines of slope $K$ outside this band, forming an odd-symmetric broken characteristic.

**Two practical examples (1 mark):**
1. Hydraulic servo valve with spool overlap (input = spool displacement, output = fluid flow).
2. DC motor with Coulomb friction (input = voltage, output = shaft velocity).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the describing function of a deadzone nonlinearity with slope $K$ and dead-zone half-width $d$. Why is the describing function purely real?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**DF expression (2 marks):**

$$
N(X) = K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\psi + \frac{d}{X}\cos\psi\right], \qquad \psi = \sin^{-1}(d/X), \quad X > d
$$

**Reason for purely real DF (1 mark):** Deadzone is an **odd-symmetric** nonlinearity ($y(-u) = -y(u)$). Its output Fourier expansion contains only **sine terms**, so the in-phase (cosine) Fourier coefficient $B_1 = 0$, leaving only the quadrature (sine) coefficient — hence $N(X)$ has zero phase.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the **Describing Function** $N(X)$ of a simple deadzone nonlinearity with slope $K$ and half-width $d$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the unity-feedback system shown, the linear plant is

$$
G(s) = \frac{10}{s\,(s+1)\,(s+5)}
$$

A deadzone with $K = 1$, $d = 1$ precedes $G(s)$. Using the Nyquist criterion and the $-1/N(X)$ locus, **determine whether a limit cycle exists, and if so, find its amplitude $X$ and frequency $\omega$**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

The detailed derivation given in **SECTION 3** above is the complete solution. For board-exam brevity, list the key steps:

1. **Input assumption (½ mark):** $u(t) = X\sin\omega t$ with $X > d$.
2. **Break-angle definition (1 mark):** $\psi = \sin^{-1}(d/X)$.
3. **Output waveform in one quarter-period (1 mark):** $y = 0$ for $0 \le \omega t \le \psi$; $y = K(X\sin\omega t - d)$ for $\psi \le \omega t \le \pi/2$.
4. **DF formula using quarter-wave symmetry (1 mark):**

$$
N(X) = \frac{4}{\pi X}\int_{\psi}^{\pi/2} K\bigl(X\sin\tau - d\bigr)\sin\tau\,d\tau
$$

5. **Evaluation of the two integrals (2 marks):**

$$
\int_{\psi}^{\pi/2}\sin^{2}\tau\,d\tau = \frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\psi}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\psi}{4}, \quad \int_{\psi}^{\pi/2}\sin\tau\,d\tau = \cos\psi
$$

6. **Final simplification using $\sin 2\psi = 2(d/X)\cos\psi$ (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{N(X) = K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\psi + \frac{d}{X}\cos\psi\right],\quad \psi = \sin^{-1}(d/X)}
$$

7. **Limiting cases (½ mark):** $N \to 0$ as $X \to d^{+}$ and $N \to K$ as $X \to \infty$.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ (2 marks):**

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega\,(j\omega + 1)\,(j\omega + 5)}
$$

Find the **point where $G(j\omega)$ crosses the negative real axis** (set imaginary part to zero):

After rationalisation,

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{10\bigl[-6\omega^{2} - j\omega(5 - \omega^{2})\bigr]}{\omega^{2}(\omega^{2}+1)(\omega^{2}+25)}
$$

Imaginary part vanishes when $5 - \omega^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$ rad/s.

At this frequency:

$$
\text{Re}\,G(j\sqrt{5}) = \frac{10\bigl(-6 \cdot 5\bigr)}{5 \cdot 6 \cdot 30} = \frac{-300}{900} = -\frac{1}{3}
$$

So the Nyquist plot crosses the **negative real axis at $-1/3$**.

**Step 2 — Locus of $-1/N(X)$ (2 marks):**

For $K = 1$:

$$
-\frac{1}{N(X)} = -\frac{1}{1 - \frac{2}{\pi}\!\left[\psi + \frac{1}{X}\cos\psi\right]}
$$

As $X \to 1^{+}$ (threshold): $N \to 0 \Rightarrow -1/N \to -\infty$.
As $X \to \infty$: $N \to 1 \Rightarrow -1/N \to -1$.

So the $-1/N(X)$ locus sweeps the negative real axis from $-\infty$ to $-1$.

**Step 3 — Intersection check (2 marks):**

The Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis at $-1/3$. Since $-1/3$ lies **outside** the interval $(-\infty, -1)$ … wait — $-1/3$ is to the right of $-1$.

> **Re-check:** The $-1/N(X)$ locus lies on $(-\infty, -1]$, while the Nyquist point is at $-1/3$. Since $-1/3 > -1$, the two loci **do not intersect**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**

**No limit cycle exists** for this specific choice of $K$ and $d$. To create a limit cycle, one would need to **increase $d$ or reduce $K$** so that the $-1/N(X)$ locus extends past $-1/3$, OR modify $G(s)$ so its crossing point lies left of $-1$.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** Sketch the input–output characteristic of a deadzone with half-width $d = 2$ and slope $K = 3$. Mark the break points and indicate the equations of the three segments. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** A unity-feedback nonlinear system has a deadzone $K = 2$, $d = 1.5$ followed by a linear plant

$$
G(s) = \frac{K_p}{s\,(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{4}{s\,(0.5s + 1)}
$$

Using the **Describing Function method**, determine the **amplitude $X$ and frequency $\omega$** of any self-sustained oscillation. **(11 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 3 marks

**Sketch (2 marks):** Plot the horizontal segment from $u = -2$ to $u = +2$ at $y = 0$. From $(+2, 0)$, draw a line of slope $3$ to the right: $y = 3(u - 2)$. From $(-2, 0)$, draw a line of slope $3$ to the left: $y = 3(u + 2)$.

**Equations (1 mark):**

$$
y = \begin{cases} 0, & |u| \le 2 \\ 3(u - 2), & u > 2 \\ 3(u + 2), & u < -2 \end{cases}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 11 marks

**Step 1 — Describing Function of deadzone (1 mark):**

$$
N(X) = K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\psi + \frac{d}{X}\cos\psi\right] = 2 - \frac{4}{\pi}\!\left[\psi + \frac{1.5}{X}\cos\psi\right], \quad \psi = \sin^{-1}(1.5/X)
$$

The $-1/N(X)$ locus lies on the negative real axis, ranging from $-1/2$ (as $X \to \infty$) to $-\infty$ (as $X \to 1.5^{+}$).

**Step 2 — Frequency response of the linear plant (2 marks):**

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{4}{j\omega(0.5j\omega + 1)} = \frac{4}{j\omega - 0.5\omega^{2} + 1} = \frac{4}{(1 - 0.5\omega^{2}) + j\omega}
$$

Rationalise:

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{4\bigl[(1 - 0.5\omega^{2}) - j\omega\bigr]}{(1 - 0.5\omega^{2})^{2} + \omega^{2}}
$$

Real and imaginary parts:

$$
\text{Re}\,G(j\omega) = \frac{4(1 - 0.5\omega^{2})}{(1 - 0.5\omega^{2})^{2} + \omega^{2}}, \quad \text{Im}\,G(j\omega) = \frac{-4\omega}{(1 - 0.5\omega^{2})^{2} + \omega^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Phase-crossover frequency (where $\text{Im} = 0$) (2 marks):**

For a Type-1 second-order plant of the form $K_p/[s(\tau s + 1)]$, the phase is **$-90°$ at $\omega = 0$** and **$-180°$ as $\omega \to \infty$**, crossing $-180°$ at $\omega = \infty$. Hence the Nyquist plot does **not** cross the negative real axis at a finite frequency (the imaginary part is always negative for finite $\omega > 0$).

This means there is **no finite-frequency intersection** of $G(j\omega)$ with the negative real axis.

**Step 4 — Re-examine the limit-cycle equation (2 marks):**

The describing-function oscillation condition is:

$$
G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(X)}
$$

Because $G(j\omega)$ has **negative imaginary part** for all $\omega > 0$ (it is a Type-1 plant), and $-1/N(X)$ is **purely real**, equality requires the imaginary part of $G(j\omega)$ to vanish — which only happens at $\omega = 0$ or $\omega \to \infty$, neither of which is a physical oscillation.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (2 marks):**

**No finite-amplitude limit cycle exists** for this configuration. This is a known result: deadzone alone, in cascade with a stable minimum-phase plant of relative degree $\ge 1$, generally **cannot sustain a self-oscillation** unless the plant has **conditional stability** (phase dipping below $-180°$ at finite frequency).

> **Engineering interpretation (2 marks):** The deadzone introduces an "amplitude-dependent attenuation" — at small amplitudes the effective loop gain $K_p \cdot N(X)$ drops below the critical value needed to encircle $-1$, so the system remains stable. Only an **integrator plant with phase loss** (e.g., a plant with time delay or right-half-plane zero) could combine with deadzone to produce a limit cycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check $X > d$:** Many students substitute a numerical value of $X$ without confirming it exceeds $d$. If $X \le d$, $N(X) = 0$ and the analysis is invalid.
> 2. **Mixing up the two integral limits:** The standard error is writing $\int_{0}^{\psi}$ instead of $\int_{\psi}^{\pi/2}$ in the DF integral — this leads to a **sign error** in $N(X)$.
> 3. **Plotting $-1/N(X)$ as a curve instead of a point:** Because deadzone is odd-symmetric, $-1/N(X)$ is a **single point moving on the negative real axis** — *not* a curve in the complex plane.
> 4. **Using $K_{\text{eq}} = K$ for small-signal analysis:** Inside the deadzone the effective gain drops to zero; assuming full gain $K$ leads to over-estimation of system response and missed stability issues.
> 5. **Skipping the limit-cycle intersection argument:** A complete solution must explicitly state *whether* $G(j\omega)$ intersects $-1/N(X)$, *and at what frequency and amplitude*.
> 6. **Wrong units in $d$ and $X$:** $d$ must be in the same units as the input to the nonlinearity; mixing radian and degree scales for $\psi$ loses a mark immediately.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Simple Deadzone**

- **Definition (static, odd, memoryless):** Output is **zero** for $\lvert u\rvert \le d$ and **linear** with slope $K$ for $\lvert u\rvert > d$.
- **Two parameters:** half-width $d$ and slope $K$.
- **Three-equation piecewise definition** — must be memorised.
- **Odd symmetry** $\Rightarrow$ **DF is purely real** (no phase shift, no imaginary part).
- **Describing Function formula (board-exam essential):**

$$
N(X) = K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{d}{X} + \frac{d}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{d^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right], \quad X > d
$$

- **Asymptotic behaviour:** $N(X) \to 0$ as $X \to d^{+}$; $N(X) \to K$ as $X \to \infty$.
- **Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ + real-axis locus $-1/N(X)$** is the complete limit-cycle search toolkit.
- **Limit-cycle existence requires intersection** between $G(j\omega)$ and $-1/N(X)$; if no intersection, **no limit cycle**.
- **Equivalent gain $K_{\text{eq}} = N(X)$** is amplitude-dependent; this is the key insight that distinguishes nonlinear from linear analysis.
- **Practical examples:** valve overlap, Coulomb friction, gear backlash, diode cut-in voltage — mention at least two in any definition question.
- **Sketch must show:** horizontal segment across the dead band with two straight lines of slope $K$ extending outward, odd-symmetric about the origin.
- **Common KTU mistake:** plotting the deadzone characteristic with non-zero output at $u = 0$. The defining feature is **$y = 0$ exactly at $u = 0$** (and across the central band).
- **Compensation hint (advanced):** Deadzone can be cancelled using an **inverse deadzone** pre-compensator — feed-forward the missing threshold to "fill the gap."
- **Compare-and-contrast table for exam:**

| Nonlinearity | Output at origin | Output outside band | DF phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deadzone** | 0 (flat) | Linear | Real |
| Saturation | Linear | Asymptotic limit | Real |
| Relay | $\pm M$ | $\pm M$ | Real |
| Backlash | Multi-valued (loop) | Linear | **Complex** |
| Hysteresis | Multi-valued | Linear | **Complex** |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saturation or Limiter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Saturation or Limiter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of **Non-Linear Control Systems (NCS)** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a **Saturation Non-Linearity** (also called a **Limiter**) is defined as a *memoryless, static, single-valued, odd-symmetric non-linearity* in which the output signal maintains a linear proportional relationship with the input signal over a bounded operating region, but is constrained to fixed amplitude levels once the input exceeds prescribed thresholds.

Mathematically, for an input signal $u(t)$ and output signal $m(t)$:

$$
m(t) =
\begin{cases}
M, & u(t) > a \\[4pt]
K \cdot u(t), & \vert u(t) \vert \leq a \\[4pt]
-M, & u(t) < -a
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $K$ = **linear-region slope (gain)**
- $a$ = **linear range threshold (break-point input)**
- $M = K \cdot a$ = **saturation output level (clipping amplitude)**
- $\pm a$ = **dead-band linear limits**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Saturation is the *most frequently encountered non-linearity* in real-world engineering actuators. In the Board Examination, you will be expected to (i) sketch the input-output characteristic, (ii) derive its **Describing Function (DF)**, and (iii) apply the **Extended Nyquist / Kochenburger Criterion** to predict limit cycles in closed-loop systems containing a saturation block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Odd-Symmetry Property:** Because $m(-u) = -m(u)$, the saturation element produces **only odd harmonics** ($1^{st}, 3^{rd}, 5^{th}, \ldots$) when excited by a pure sinusoid — a property that simplifies the Describing Function analysis considerably.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain-English Analogy — The "Garden-Hose Pressure Regulator" Imagine** you are watering your garden with a hose connected to a municipal water tap.

- **Linear Region:** When you turn the tap handle a *little*, the water pressure (output) rises proportionally to how much you turn (input). The relationship is a clean straight line.
- **Saturation Region:** But the tap has a *physical stop*. Even if you wrench the handle beyond a certain angle, the valve cannot open further. The water pressure is now **pinned at its maximum possible value** — no matter how hard you twist, it cannot exceed that ceiling.

The **Saturation Non-Linearity is exactly this valve behavior** in mathematical form. Inside the linear range, the system obeys Hooke's law (input $\propto$ output). Outside, the output is "clamped" at the saturation level $M$.

**Why does it matter in Control Engineering?** Every physical actuator — DC motors (limited by armature voltage / current), hydraulic valves (limited by spool travel), op-amps (limited by supply rail $\pm V_{CC}$), and power electronic converters (PWM duty-cycle bounded by $0$ to $1$) — exhibits saturation. Ignoring it leads to the **"linear-design fallacy"** where a controller designed via Bode/Nyquist methods becomes unstable or sluggish when the plant actually hits its physical limits.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics for Saturation:**
> - **Linear Range:** $\pm a$ (units of input — Volts, Amps, rad, m, etc.)
> - **Saturation Level:** $\pm M = \pm K \cdot a$
> - **Saturation Ratio:** $\eta = \dfrac{a}{A}$ where $A$ is the sinusoidal input amplitude; the describing function depends only on this dimensionless ratio.
> - **Equivalent Gain:** $N(A)$ — frequency-independent (saturation has no dynamics, only amplitude-dependent gain).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The saturation element operates on **three distinct input regimes**. The control engineer must understand all three because the *region of operation* determines whether the closed-loop system is in the linear regime (and hence amenable to Bode/Nyquist analysis) or the non-linear regime (where Describing Function methods become mandatory).

### Step 1 — Identify the Input Magnitude

Given an excitation $u(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ at the saturation input, compute the **peak amplitude** $A$ and compare it against the linear threshold $a$.

- If $A \le a$ : the system operates **entirely within the linear band**. The non-linearity is transparent (invisible). Output is $m(t) = K \cdot A \sin(\omega t)$.
- If $A > a$ : the input **clips** the non-linearity twice per cycle. The output is a **truncated sinusoid** with flat tops/bottoms.

### Step 2 — Compute the Crossover Angle $\alpha$

The crossover angle $\alpha$ is defined as the phase angle at which the sinusoid first exceeds the linear range:

$$
\alpha = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right), \qquad A \ge a
$$

This angle is the **linchpin** of the entire Describing Function derivation — it tells you exactly where the clipping begins within one quarter-cycle.

### Step 3 — Decompose the Output into Segments

For $A > a$, the output in one quarter-period $[0, \pi/2]$ is piecewise:

$$
m(t) =
\begin{cases}
K A \sin(\omega t), & 0 \le \omega t \le \alpha \\[4pt]
M = K a, & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi/2
\end{cases}
$$

The remaining quadrants are reconstructed using the **odd-symmetry** of the non-linearity: $m(t + \pi/\omega) = -m(t)$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Fundamental Fourier Coefficient

The **Describing Function** $N(A)$ is defined as the ratio of the fundamental component of the output to the input phasor:

$$
N(A) \triangleq \frac{B_1}{A} \angle \phi_1
$$

For odd-symmetric, memoryless non-linearities, the higher harmonics are odd-only, and the phase shift $\phi_1 = 0$. Hence $N(A)$ is **real-valued** (no phase lag), and depends *only* on amplitude $A$, not on frequency $\omega$.

$$
N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} = \frac{1}{\pi A} \int_{0}^{2\pi} m(\tau) \sin(\tau) \, d\tau, \qquad \tau = \omega t
$$

### Step 5 — Boundary Behavior

- **As $A \to a^+$ (just entering saturation):** $N(A) \to K$ (continuous matching with the linear regime).
- **As $A \to \infty$ (deep saturation):** $N(A) \to \dfrac{2K}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{a}{A} \to 0$. The non-linearity behaves as an **attenuator** that kills the loop gain — a key insight for **anti-windup** design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The Describing Function $N(A)$ is a **monotonically decreasing** function of $A$ in the saturation regime. This monotonicity guarantees a **unique limit cycle** (if it exists) for a given linear plant — a critical conclusion that examiners love to test.

---

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Manifestation of Saturation |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Control-surface deflection limited by actuator stroke and rate limits |
| **Process Control** | Valve positioners (0–100% opening) clamped by physical travel |
| **Power Electronics** | PWM duty-ratio bounded in $[0, 1]$; inverter output limited by DC bus voltage |
| **Robotics** | Joint torques bounded by motor torque ratings |
| **Automotive** | Throttle-by-wire pedal maps; brake-pressure modulators |
| **Biomedical** | Drug-delivery infusion pumps with minimum/maximum dosage clamps |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the markdown table below, all absolute-value and conditional expressions use the LaTeX commands `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol `\|` to prevent breakage of the markdown table renderer.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Valid Regime / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input-Output Characteristic | $m = \begin{cases} M, & u > a \\ K u, & \vert u \vert \le a \\ -M, & u < -a \end{cases}$ | All real $u$ |
| 2 | Crossover Angle | $\alpha = \arcsin(a/A)$ | $A \ge a$ |
| 3 | Saturation Ratio | $\eta = a/A$ | $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 4 | Describing Function (Linear) | $N(A) = K$ | $A \le a$ |
| 5 | Describing Function (Saturation) | $N(A) = \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\arcsin(\eta) + \eta\sqrt{1-\eta^{2}}\right]$ | $A \ge a$ |
| 6 | Equivalent Gain at Limit | $\displaystyle \lim_{A \to \infty} N(A) = 0$ | Deep saturation |
| 7 | Saturation Output Level | $M = K a$ | Engineering units |
| 8 | Odd-Harmonic Content | $B_{2k} = 0, \ \forall k \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ | Odd symmetry |
| 9 | Phase Shift | $\phi_{1} = 0$ | Real-valued $N(A)$ |
| 10 | Limit Cycle Condition (Kochenburger) | $1 + N(A_{lc}) \cdot G(j\omega_{lc}) = 0$ | Closed-loop |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Describing Function of Saturation

We derive $N(A)$ for the saturation non-linearity in full detail. This is a **high-weight derivation** (often 7–10 marks) in KTU Board Examinations.

### Setup

Let the input to the non-linearity be a pure sinusoid:

$$
u(t) = A \sin(\omega t)
$$

Assume **$A > a$** so that clipping occurs. The output waveform $m(t)$ is shown in Fig. 1 of SECTION 4 and is described, over one half-cycle $\omega t \in [0, \pi]$, by:

$$
m(\tau) =
\begin{cases}
K A \sin(\tau), & 0 \le \tau \le \alpha \\[4pt]
K a = M, & \alpha \le \tau \le \pi - \alpha \\[4pt]
K A \sin(\tau), & \pi - \alpha \le \tau \le \pi
\end{cases}
$$

where $\tau = \omega t$ and $\alpha = \arcsin(a/A)$.

By **odd-symmetry** ($m(\tau + \pi) = -m(\tau)$), the second half of the period is simply the negation of the first half, so it suffices to integrate over $[0, \pi]$.

---

### Step A — Write the Fundamental Fourier Coefficient

For a memoryless non-linearity with odd symmetry, the **fundamental sine coefficient** $B_1$ is:

$$
B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} m(\tau) \sin(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

The factor of $2/\pi$ (not $1/\pi$) arises because we integrate over half the period and exploit the half-wave odd symmetry that doubles the contribution.

---

### Step B — Split the Integral at the Crossover Angles

Substituting the piecewise expression for $m(\tau)$:

$$
B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\alpha} (K A \sin\tau)\sin\tau \, d\tau + \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} (M)\sin\tau \, d\tau + \int_{\pi - \alpha}^{\pi} (K A \sin\tau)\sin\tau \, d\tau \right]
$$

$$
B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi} \left[ K A \int_{0}^{\alpha} \sin^{2}\tau \, d\tau + M \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \sin\tau \, d\tau + K A \int_{\pi - \alpha}^{\pi} \sin^{2}\tau \, d\tau \right]
$$

---

### Step C — Evaluate the Sine-Squared Integrals

Use the half-angle identity $\sin^{2}\tau = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\tau)}{2}$:

$$
\int \sin^{2}\tau \, d\tau = \frac{\tau}{2} - \frac{\sin(2\tau)}{4} + C
$$

Therefore:

$$
\int_{0}^{\alpha} \sin^{2}\tau \, d\tau = \left[\frac{\tau}{2} - \frac{\sin(2\tau)}{4}\right]_{0}^{\alpha} = \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{4}
$$

By symmetry of $\sin^{2}\tau$ about $\tau = \pi/2$:

$$
\int_{\pi - \alpha}^{\pi} \sin^{2}\tau \, d\tau = \int_{0}^{\alpha} \sin^{2}\tau \, d\tau = \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{4}
$$

**Sum of the two equal sine-squared integrals:**

$$
\int_{0}^{\alpha} \sin^{2}\tau \, d\tau + \int_{\pi - \alpha}^{\pi} \sin^{2}\tau \, d\tau = \alpha - \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}
$$

---

### Step D — Evaluate the Middle (Clipped) Integral

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} \sin\tau \, d\tau = \left[-\cos\tau\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} = -\cos(\pi - \alpha) + \cos(\alpha)
$$

Using $\cos(\pi - \alpha) = -\cos\alpha$:

$$
= -(-\cos\alpha) + \cos\alpha = 2\cos\alpha
$$

---

### Step E — Assemble $B_1$

$$
B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\left[K A \left(\alpha - \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right) + M \cdot 2\cos\alpha\right]
$$

Apply the double-angle identity $\sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha$:

$$
B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\left[K A \alpha - K A \sin\alpha\cos\alpha + 2 M \cos\alpha\right]
$$

Substitute $M = K a$:

$$
B_1 = \frac{2 K}{\pi}\left[A \alpha - A \sin\alpha\cos\alpha + 2 a \cos\alpha\right]
$$

---

### Step F — Apply the Geometric Constraint $\sin\alpha = a/A$

We have $\sin\alpha = a/A$, so $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1 - (a/A)^{2}}$. Substituting:

$$
B_1 = \frac{2 K}{\pi}\left[A \arcsin\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) - A \cdot \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{A^{2}}} + 2 a \sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{A^{2}}}\right]
$$

$$
B_1 = \frac{2 K}{\pi}\left[A \arcsin\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) - a\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{A^{2}}} + 2 a \sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{A^{2}}}\right]
$$

$$
B_1 = \frac{2 K}{\pi}\left[A \arcsin\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) + a\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{A^{2}}}\right]
$$

---

### Step G — Form the Describing Function $N(A) = B_1 / A$

$$
N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} = \frac{2 K}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) + \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^{2}}{A^{2}}}\right]
$$

Defining $\eta = a/A$:

$$
\boxed{\,N(A) = \frac{2 K}{\pi}\left[\arcsin(\eta) + \eta\sqrt{1-\eta^{2}}\right], \qquad \eta = \frac{a}{A},\ A \ge a\,}
$$

And in the linear regime:

$$
\boxed{\,N(A) = K,\qquad A \le a\,}
$$

---

### Step H — Sanity Checks

**Check 1 — Continuity at $A = a$ ($\eta = 1$):**

$$
N(a) = \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\arcsin(1) + 1 \cdot \sqrt{0}\right] = \frac{2K}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = K \quad \checkmark
$$

**Check 2 — Deep-saturation limit $A \to \infty$ ($\eta \to 0$):**

$$
N(\infty) = \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[0 + 0\right] = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Check 3 — Monotonicity:** Differentiating with respect to $\eta$ shows $dN/d\eta < 0$, confirming $N(A)$ is monotonically decreasing for $A \ge a$. This guarantees **at most one limit cycle** in a closed-loop system.

---

## 3.2 Limit Cycle Prediction (Kochenburger's Criterion)

If the saturation element sits in a unity-feedback loop with linear plant $G(s)$, a **limit cycle** of amplitude $A_{lc}$ and frequency $\omega_{lc}$ satisfies:

$$
G(j\omega_{lc}) = -\frac{1}{N(A_{lc})}
$$

Graphical procedure (KF-board favorite):

1. Plot the **negative reciprocal** $-1/N(A)$ on the Nichols/G Nyquist plot. For saturation, $-1/N(A)$ lies on the **negative real axis** between $-1/K$ (at $\eta = 1$) and $-\infty$ (as $\eta \to 0$).
2. Plot $G(j\omega)$.
3. The **intersection** gives the limit-cycle parameters $(A_{lc}, \omega_{lc})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability of Limit Cycle:** If the intersection occurs with the locus $G(j\omega)$ crossing $-1/N(A)$ from "inside" to "outside" as $\omega$ increases, the limit cycle is **stable**. Otherwise it is unstable.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Input-Output Characteristic of Saturation

The static non-linearity is best represented by a Cartesian plot. Since a Mermaid block cannot render a true function plot, the following **functional architecture flow** captures the regime-decision logic that mathematically *defines* the saturation curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputNode["INPUT u of t"] --> decider{"ABS u LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO a"}
    decider -- YES --> linearPath["LINEAR BRANCH m of t EQUALS K times u"]
    decider -- NO --> signCheck{"SIGN OF u"}
    signCheck -- POSITIVE --> upperSat["UPPER SATURATION m of t EQUALS PLUS M"]
    signCheck -- NEGATIVE --> lowerSat["LOWER SATURATION m of t EQUALS MINUS M"]
    linearPath --> outputNode["OUTPUT m of t"]
    upperSat --> outputNode
    lowerSat --> outputNode
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#E0F4FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px
    classDef terminal fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    class decider,signCheck decision
    class linearPath,upperSat,lowerSat branch
    class inputNode,outputNode terminal
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The decision diamonds implement the piecewise regions of the saturation function. The two flat-cap terminals ($+M$, $-M$) correspond to the horizontal asymptotes of the input-output characteristic. The linear branch corresponds to the central slope-$K$ segment.

---

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Block Diagram with Saturation in the Forward Path

A canonical configuration for analyzing limit cycles arising from actuator saturation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refNode["REFERENCE INPUT r of t"] --> sumNode["SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    sumNode -- "ERROR e of t" --> ctrlNode["LINEAR COMPENSATOR Gc of s"]
    ctrlNode --> satNode["SATURATION NONLINEARITY N of A"]
    satNode --> plantNode["LINEAR PLANT G of s"]
    plantNode --> yNode["PLANT OUTPUT y of t"]
    yNode -- "FEEDBACK" --> sumNode
    classDef ref fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctrl fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sat fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px
    classDef plant fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px
    class refNode,yNode ref
    class ctrlNode ctrl
    class satNode sat
    class plantNode sumNode plant
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading guide:** The saturation block (highlighted in orange) is the *only* non-linear element. Once the input amplitude $A$ to this block exceeds $a$, the closed-loop dynamics depart from the linear Nyquist prediction. The **Describing Function** of this single block replaces the entire non-linear subsystem when the linear plant acts as a low-pass filter (attenuating higher harmonics).
