## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define the describing function of a nonlinear element. State the two conditions under which the describing function analysis yields accurate results for a closed-loop nonlinear control system.*

**Model Answer:**

The **describing function (DF)** of a nonlinear element is defined as the complex ratio of the fundamental harmonic component of the periodic output $y(t)$ to the input sinusoid $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, treated as a quasi-linear gain:

$$N(A,\omega) = \frac{B_1 + jA_1}{A}$$

The two conditions for **accuracy** are:

1. **Filtering Hypothesis:** The linear subsystem $G(s)$ must strongly attenuate all higher harmonics, i.e., $\vert G(jn\omega)\vert \ll \vert G(j\omega)\vert$ for $n \geq 2$. This ensures only the fundamental survives the loop.

2. **Low Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** The nonlinearity must produce an output whose harmonic content beyond the fundamental is small relative to the fundamental, i.e., $\text{THD} \ll 1$ (typically $\text{THD} < 10\%$ is desired).

When both hold, the higher-harmonic terms in the closed-loop characteristic equation become negligible, and $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$ accurately predicts the system's limit-cycle behavior.

*(Examiner's note: A common mistake is to list only one condition — students must explicitly mention BOTH filtering AND low THD for full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supple]**
*Explain the concept of the filtering hypothesis in describing function analysis. Why is it critical for the accuracy of limit-cycle predictions?*

**Model Answer:**

The **filtering hypothesis** states that the linear plant $G(s)$ in a closed-loop nonlinear system must behave as a sufficiently strong low-pass filter, so that the higher harmonics (generated by the nonlinearity) are heavily attenuated before they propagate back to the nonlinearity's input.

Mathematically:

$$\left|\frac{G(jn\omega)}{G(j\omega)}\right| \ll 1 \quad \text{for all } n \geq 2$$

**Why it is critical for accuracy:**

- The describing function method assumes the input to the nonlinear element is a pure sinusoid. If $G(s)$ passes higher harmonics with appreciable gain, this assumption is violated, and the nonlinear element responds to a *distorted* waveform rather than a pure sine.
- The harmonic balance equation $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$ is derived by *retaining only the fundamental* in the loop. If the second harmonic transmission $\vert G(j2\omega)\vert$ is not negligible, the residual term $G(j2\omega)N_2(A)$ introduces a systematic error in the predicted amplitude and frequency of the limit cycle.
- A useful rule of thumb: $\vert G(j2\omega)/G(j\omega)\vert < 0.1$ is often required for accuracy within ~10%, which is the standard KTU benchmark.

If the filtering hypothesis fails (e.g., $G(s)$ has a resonant peak near $2\omega$), the DF prediction can be qualitatively wrong — predicting a stable limit cycle where the actual system is stable, or vice versa.

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **Question A OR Question B**.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**(a)** *Derive the describing function $N(A)$ of an ideal relay with output magnitudes $\pm M$. Compute the total harmonic distortion (THD) of the relay output. **[7 Marks]***

**(b)** *For a unity-feedback system with an ideal relay (output $\pm 1$) in cascade with linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$, use the describing function method to predict the limit cycle. Verify the filtering hypothesis and estimate the accuracy of your prediction. **[7 Marks]***

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Relay output:** For input $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, $A > 0$, the relay output is $y(t) = M\,\text{sgn}[\sin(\omega t)]$, a symmetric square wave.

**Step 2 — Fourier series of square wave:**

$$y(t) = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Identify fundamental:** $B_1 = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}$, $A_1 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Describing function:**

$$\boxed{N(A) = \frac{4M}{\pi A}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute higher-harmonic power:**

$$\sum_{n=3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\!\left(\frac{4M}{n\pi}\right)^{\!2} = \frac{16M^2}{\pi^2}\!\left(\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1\right)$$

**Step 6 — Compute THD:**

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{(16M^2/\pi^2)(\pi^2/8 - 1)}}{4M/\pi} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2/8 - 1}{1}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 8}{8}}$$

Numerically:

$$\text{THD} = \sqrt{\frac{9.8696 - 8}{8}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.8696}{8}} = \sqrt{0.2337} \approx 0.4834$$

$$\boxed{\text{THD} \approx 48.34\%}$$

This very high THD means **the ideal relay is the "worst case" nonlinearity** for DF analysis — extreme filtering is required.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Harmonic balance equation:**

$$1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(A)} = -\frac{\pi A}{4M}$$

With $M = 1$:

$$G(j\omega) = -\frac{\pi A}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $G(j\omega)$ for the given plant:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+5)} = \frac{10}{j\omega(5 - \omega^2 + j6\omega)}$$

$$= \frac{10}{-6\omega^2 + j\omega(5-\omega^2)}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10\left[-6\omega^2 - j\omega(5-\omega^2)\right]}{36\omega^4 + \omega^2(5-\omega^2)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate imaginary parts to zero:**

For the harmonic balance to be satisfied, the imaginary part of $G(j\omega)$ must vanish (since the RHS $-\pi A/4$ is purely real):

$$\omega(5 - \omega^2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega^2 = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega^* = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Equate real parts to solve for amplitude:**

Real part of $G(j\omega)$ at $\omega = \sqrt{5}$:

$$\text{Re}[G(j\sqrt{5})] = \frac{10 \cdot (-6 \cdot 5)}{36 \cdot 25 + 5 \cdot 0} = \frac{-300}{900} = -\frac{1}{3}$$

Setting $-\pi A^*/4 = -1/3$:

$$A^* = \frac{4}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244$$

**Step 5 — Predicted limit cycle:**

$$\boxed{A^* \approx 0.424, \quad \omega^* \approx 2.236 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify filtering hypothesis:**

At $\omega = \sqrt{5}$:

$$|G(j\omega^*)| = \left|\frac{1}{3}\right| = 0.333$$

At $2\omega^* = 2\sqrt{5}$:

$$G(j2\sqrt{5}) = \frac{10}{j2\sqrt{5}(j2\sqrt{5}+1)(j2\sqrt{5}+5)}$$

Compute $\omega^2 = 20$:

$$|G(j2\sqrt{5})| = \frac{10}{2\sqrt{5}\sqrt{20 + 1}\sqrt{20 + 25}} = \frac{10}{2\sqrt{5}\sqrt{21}\sqrt{45}} \approx \frac{10}{4.472 \cdot 4.583 \cdot 6.708} \approx 0.0726$$

Filter ratio:

$$\frac{|G(j2\omega^*)|}{|G(j\omega^*)|} = \frac{0.0726}{0.333} \approx 0.218$$

**Step 7 — Accuracy estimate:**

$$\varepsilon \approx \frac{|G(j2\omega^*)|}{|G(j\omega^*)|} \times \text{THD} \approx 0.218 \times 0.4834 \approx 0.105 \;\;(\approx 10.5\%)$$

Since the filter ratio exceeds the recommended 0.1 and the THD is very high (48.34%), the prediction accuracy is **only marginal**, with about 10% expected error in amplitude.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Derive the describing function of a saturation nonlinearity having slope $k$ in the linear region $\vert x\vert \leq a$ and slope $k_2$ (where $k_2 < k$) in the saturated region $\vert x\vert > a$. Identify the conditions under which this DF becomes accurate. **[7 Marks]***

**(b)** *A unity-feedback system has saturation nonlinearity with $k = 1$, $k_2 = 0.2$, $a = 1$, cascaded with linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{5}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$. Apply DF analysis to find the limit cycle, and assess accuracy using THD and the filter ratio. **[7 Marks]***

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — Output waveform:** For input $x = A\sin(\omega t)$, $A > a$, define the break-point angle $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(a/A)$. The output is:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} kA\sin(\omega t), & 0 \leq \omega t \leq \alpha \\ ka + k_2[A\sin(\omega t) - a], & \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \alpha \\ ka + k_2[A\sin(\omega t) + a], & \pi - \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi + \alpha \\ kA\sin(\omega t), & \pi + \alpha \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $B_1$ (odd symmetry ⇒ $A_1 = 0$):**

$$B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\!\int_{0}^{\pi} y(\theta)\sin\theta\,d\theta$$

By symmetry, decompose into four quarter-periods and use the structure. The standard result for the saturation DF is:

$$\begin{aligned} B_1 &= \frac{4}{\pi}\!\left[\int_{0}^{\alpha} kA\sin^2\theta\,d\theta + \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\!\left[ka + k_2(A\sin\theta - a)\right]\sin\theta\,d\theta\right] \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate integrals:**

First integral: $\int_{0}^{\alpha}\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = \dfrac{\alpha}{2} - \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}$.

Second integral: let $I_2 = \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin\theta\,d\theta = \cos\alpha$ and $I_3 = \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = \dfrac{\pi/2 - \alpha}{2} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}$.

Combining:

$$B_1 = \frac{4A}{\pi}\!\left[k\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}\right) + k_2\!\left(\frac{\pi/2 - \alpha}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}\right) + (k - k_2)\!\left(\cos\alpha - \frac{a}{A}\cos\alpha\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using $\sin 2\alpha = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha = 2(a/A)\sqrt{1-(a/A)^2}$:**

After substantial algebraic simplification (refer to standard texts, e.g., Atherton, *Nonlinear Control Engineering*), the **standard DF for saturation** emerges:

$$\boxed{\,N(A) = k_2 + \frac{2(k - k_2)}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{a}{A} + \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^{\!2}}\right]\,}$$

**Step 5 — Conditions for accuracy:**

1. The plant $G(s)$ must be strictly low-pass: $|G(jn\omega)| \ll |G(j\omega)|$ for $n \geq 2$.
2. Saturation has **moderate THD** (typically 5–15%), much smaller than ideal relay's 48%.
3. The amplitude $A$ must satisfy $A > a$ (otherwise the nonlinearity operates in the linear region and DF is trivial).
4. The slope ratio $k_2/k$ must be small for mild saturation; large $k_2/k$ makes the nonlinearity less "sharp" and DF more accurate.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Harmonic balance:** $G(j\omega) = -\dfrac{1}{N(A)}$.

**Step 2 — Plant frequency response:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{5}{j\omega(j\omega+2)(j\omega+4)}$$

Compute denominator magnitude: $\omega \cdot \sqrt{\omega^2 + 4} \cdot \sqrt{\omega^2 + 16}$.

For imaginary part to vanish, we need $G(j\omega)$ real and negative, which requires:

$$\omega^2 = 2 \cdot 4 / (\text{or some cancellation}) \to \text{specific value}$$

Actually for plant $G(s) = K/(s(s+a)(s+b))$, the imaginary part of $G(j\omega)$ is:

$$\text{Im}[G(j\omega)] = -\frac{K(a + b)}{\omega^2(a+b)^2 + (\omega^2 - ab)^2}$$

Wait, let's compute directly:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{5}{j\omega(-\omega^2 + j(a+b)\omega + ab)}$$

Multiplying top and bottom by $-j\omega$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{-5j\omega \cdot [-\omega^2 - j(a+b)\omega + ab]}{[\omega^2 + (a+b)^2\omega^2][\text{...}]}$$

For $a = 2$, $b = 4$: $ab = 8$, $a+b = 6$.

The imaginary part vanishes when the real part of $G$ dominates. Set Im$(G) = 0$:

For $G(s) = K/[s(s^2 + 6s + 8)]$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{5}{j\omega(8 - \omega^2 + j6\omega)} = \frac{5}{j8\omega - j\omega^3 - 6\omega^2} = \frac{5}{-6\omega^2 + j\omega(8-\omega^2)}$$

Rationalizing:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{5[-6\omega^2 - j\omega(8-\omega^2)]}{36\omega^4 + \omega^2(8-\omega^2)^2}$$

Imaginary part vanishes when $8 - \omega^2 = 0$, i.e., $\omega^2 = 8$, $\omega^* = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828$ rad/s.

**Step 3 — Real part at $\omega = 2\sqrt{2}$:**

$$\text{Re}[G(j\omega^*)] = \frac{5 \cdot (-6 \cdot 8)}{36 \cdot 64 + 0} = \frac{-240}{2304} = -\frac{5}{48} \approx -0.1042$$

**Step 4 — Solve for amplitude:**

$$\frac{-1}{N(A^*)} = -\frac{5}{48} \;\Rightarrow\; N(A^*) = \frac{48}{5} = 9.6$$

Substituting into the saturation DF with $k = 1$, $k_2 = 0.2$, $a = 1$:

$$9.6 = 0.2 + \frac{2(0.8)}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{1}{A^*} + \frac{1}{A^*}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{A^{*2}}}\right]$$

$$9.4 = \frac{1.6}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{1}{A^*} + \frac{1}{A^*}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{A^{*2}}}\right]$$

$$\frac{1}{A^*}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{A^{*2}}} + \sin^{-1}\!\frac{1}{A^*} \approx 18.46$$

This requires solving numerically. Let $u = 1/A^*$:

$$f(u) = u\sqrt{1-u^2} + \sin^{-1}(u) - 18.46 = 0$$

For $u \to 0$ (i.e., $A^* \to \infty$): $f(0) = 0 + 0 - 18.46 = -18.46$.
For $u = 0.05$ ($A^* = 20$): $f(0.05) \approx 0.05 \cdot 0.9987 + 0.05 - 18.46 \approx -18.36$.
For $u = 0.1$: $f(0.1) \approx 0.0995 + 0.1002 - 18.46 \approx -18.26$.

This suggests the requested $N(A^*) = 9.6$ is **unachievable** with the given saturation parameters because $N(A) \leq k = 1$ for all $A$. Therefore, **no limit cycle exists** for this configuration — the system is either stable or unstable without oscillation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often blindly apply the harmonic balance formula without checking whether the required $N(A^*)$ value is **physically realizable** for the given nonlinearity. For saturation with $k = 1$, the maximum DF is $N(A \to a^+) = k = 1$, so any $\vert 1/G(j\omega)\vert > 1$ would indicate *no limit cycle*. Always check feasibility!

**Step 5 — Alternative interpretation:** If the question intends the relay (i.e., $k_2 \to \infty$, saturation becomes relay), redo with $N(A) = 4M/(\pi A)$. Then with $M = 1$:

$$\frac{4}{\pi A^*} = \frac{5}{48} \;\Rightarrow\; A^* = \frac{4 \cdot 48}{5\pi} = \frac{192}{5\pi} \approx 12.22$$

**Step 6 — Filter check:**

At $\omega = 2\sqrt{2}$: $|G(j\omega^*)| = 5/48 \approx 0.1042$.

At $2\omega^* = 4\sqrt{2}$: $|G(j4\sqrt{2})| = \dfrac{5}{4\sqrt{2}\sqrt{32+4}\sqrt{32+16}} = \dfrac{5}{5.657 \cdot 6.0 \cdot 6.928} \approx \dfrac{5}{235.2} \approx 0.0213$.

Filter ratio: $\dfrac{0.0213}{0.1042} \approx 0.204$.

**Step 7 — Accuracy verdict:** Filter ratio of ~20% with relay THD of 48% yields $\varepsilon \approx 9.8\%$ — borderline accuracy.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DF Accuracy Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the imaginary part $A_1$** of the Fourier coefficient — many students compute only $B_1$ and lose 1–2 marks when the nonlinearity has phase lag (hysteresis, Coulomb friction).
> 2. **Skipping the THD computation** — even when the question does not explicitly ask for THD, computing it is *expected* to demonstrate accuracy awareness. Marks are awarded in Q(b) for an "accuracy estimate."
> 3. **Not verifying the filtering hypothesis** — without this check, the prediction is incomplete. Examiners specifically look for the ratio $\vert G(j2\omega)/G(j\omega)\vert$.
> 4. **Reporting amplitude only without frequency** — limit cycles have *two* characteristics: amplitude $A^*$ and frequency $\omega^*$. Both must be reported.
> 5. **Using the wrong formula** for saturation DF — students sometimes use the dead-zone formula by mistake. Memorize both formulas separately.
> 6. **Ignoring feasibility** — always check whether the DF value $N(A^*)$ required by harmonic balance lies within the achievable range of the nonlinearity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Accuracy of Describing Function Analysis**

**Core Definition:**
- DF is the **fundamental-component ratio** $N(A) = (B_1 + jA_1)/A$, treating the nonlinearity as a quasi-linear gain.

**Two Conditions for Accuracy:**
- **Filtering hypothesis:** $|G(jn\omega)| \ll |G(j\omega)|$ for $n \geq 2$.
- **Low THD:** Higher harmonics are negligible in the nonlinear output.

**Quantitative Metrics:**
- **THD** quantifies nonlinear distortion; ideal relay has ~48.34% (the worst case).
- **Filter ratio** $\vert G(j2\omega)/G(j\omega)\vert < 0.1$ ensures accuracy within ~10%.
- **Error bound:** $\varepsilon \approx \dfrac{\vert G(j2\omega)\vert}{\vert G(j\omega)\vert} \cdot \text{THD}$.

**Key DFs to Memorize:**
- Ideal Relay: $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$.
- Saturation: $N(A) = k_2 + \dfrac{2(k-k_2)}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{a}{A} + \dfrac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-(a/A)^2}\right]$.
- Dead-Zone: $N(A) = k - \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{d}{A} + \dfrac{d}{A}\sqrt{1-(d/A)^2}\right]$.
- Hysteresis Relay: Complex DF with $-j(h/A)$ imaginary part.

**Harmonic Balance:**
- $1 + N(A^*)G(j\omega^*) = 0$ yields *two equations* (real and imaginary parts) for *two unknowns* ($A^*$, $\omega^*$).

**Engineering Judgment:**
- Sharp nonlinearities (relays) → poor DF accuracy → require stronger filtering.
- Mild nonlinearities (small saturation) → good DF accuracy → relaxed filtering requirements.
- Always check **feasibility** of the predicted $A^*$ (e.g., $A^* > a$ for saturation to engage).

**KTU-Specific Tips:**
- In 14-mark questions, allocate ~3 marks specifically for accuracy discussion (THD + filter check).
- In 3-mark questions, mention *both* conditions and the THD filter criterion.
- Memorize the THD of ideal relay (~48.34%) — it is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Stability from Describing Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT742 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relative Stability from Describing Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **Describing Function (DF)** framework, the stability of a nonlinear system is assessed by examining the relative geometric positioning of the linear plant's Nyquist locus $G(j\omega)$ and the **Negative Inverse Describing Function (NIDF)** locus $-1/N(A)$ in the complex plane. 

**Relative Stability** quantifies *how stable* a nonlinear closed-loop system is — not merely whether a limit cycle exists, but the *degree of robustness* the system possesses against parameter variations and external disturbances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:**
> For a system exhibiting a stable limit cycle of amplitude $A_0$ and frequency $\omega_0$, the *relative stability* is defined as the **proximity measure** (distance, gain ratio, or phase margin equivalent) between the operating point on $G(j\omega)$ and the corresponding point $-1/N(A_0)$ on the NIDF locus.

Mathematically, if the limit cycle condition is satisfied:

$$G(j\omega_0) \cdot N(A_0) = -1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad G(j\omega_0) = -\frac{1}{N(A_0)}$$

then the **Relative Stability Index (RSI)** can be expressed as any of the equivalent metrics below:

$$\boxed{RSI = d = \left\vert G(j\omega) + \frac{1}{N(A)} \right\vert \quad \text{at the operating point}}$$

A *larger* value of $d$ indicates *higher* relative stability (greater robustness margin).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cliff and the Fence"

Imagine a man walking on a narrow ridge representing the system's equilibrium. The **edge of the cliff** is the boundary of instability, where any tiny perturbation sends him tumbling down (i.e., the system diverges).

- In a **linear system**, this cliff edge is the single point $-1 + j0$ on the Nyquist plot.
- In a **nonlinear system**, the cliff edge is no longer a single point but a **whole curve** — the NIDF locus $-1/N(A)$ — whose exact location depends on the amplitude $A$ of oscillation.

The **relative stability** is simply *how far* the linear locus $G(j\omega)$ is from this moving cliff edge at the operating amplitude. Just as a hiker prefers a wider trail, an engineer prefers a system whose $G(j\omega)$ stays comfortably away from $-1/N(A)$ for all expected operating amplitudes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics used:**
> - **Equivalent Gain Margin (EGM):** Dimensionless ratio
> - **Equivalent Phase Margin (EPM):** Measured in **degrees**
> - **Stability Index $d$:** Distance on the complex plane (units depend on system)

---

### 1.3 Why Linear Stability Theory Fails Here

In a **strictly linear** system, the Nyquist criterion reduces to a *binary* question: does $G(j\omega)$ encircle $-1 + j0$? Either it does (unstable) or it does not (stable).

For a **nonlinear** system analyzed via the DF method, the situation is richer:

1. The "critical point" $-1 + j0$ is replaced by a **locus** $-1/N(A)$ parameterised by amplitude $A$.
2. Different amplitudes of disturbance can yield **different** stability conclusions.
3. The system may exhibit **stable limit cycles**, which are neither fully stable nor unstable — they are *bounded oscillations* of specific amplitude.

This calls for a **quantitative** rather than *binary* measure of stability — exactly what **Relative Stability from DF** delivers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Limit Cycle Stability — Kochenburger's Criterion

Before discussing relative stability, we must first establish whether a limit cycle is **stable** (self-correcting) or **unstable** (self-amplifying). The **Kochenburger criterion** provides a graphical test:

- A limit cycle is **stable** if a small increase in amplitude $A$ pushes the system into a region where it tends to decrease $A$, and vice versa.
- Geometrically: at the intersection of $G(j\omega)$ and $-1/N(A)$, examine the direction in which $-1/N(A)$ moves as $A$ increases.

**Operational Rule (exam-favorite):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kochenburger's Stability Rule:**
> - If $-1/N(A)$ locus moves from an **unstable region into a stable region** of the linear system as $A$ increases → the limit cycle is **stable**.
> - If $-1/N(A)$ locus moves from a **stable region into an unstable region** as $A$ increases → the limit cycle is **unstable**.

The "unstable region" is the set of points encircled by $G(j\omega)$ according to the standard Nyquist rule.

### 2.2 Stability Margins for Nonlinear Systems

Once a limit cycle is confirmed stable, we quantify robustness through **two key analogs** of linear stability margins:

#### (a) Equivalent Gain Margin (EGM)

Computed at the **phase-crossover frequency** $\omega_{pc}$ (where $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$):

$$GM_{eq} = \frac{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}{\vert 1/N(A_{op}) \vert} = \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \cdot \vert N(A_{op}) \vert$$

where $A_{op}$ is the amplitude at which the system is being operated.

- $GM_{eq} > 1$ → operating point is in the stable region (relative stability exists).
- $GM_{eq} = 1$ → operating point is on the boundary of stability (limit cycle).
- $GM_{eq} < 1$ → operating point is in the unstable region.

#### (b) Equivalent Phase Margin (EPM)

Computed at the **gain-crossover frequency** $\omega_{gc}$ (where $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$):

$$PM_{eq} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) + \angle N(A_{op})$$

A positive $PM_{eq}$ indicates stable operation; its magnitude measures the robustness.

### 2.3 The Gelb–Vander Velde Approach (Stability Index)

**Gelb and Vander Velde** proposed a unified single-number measure:

$$SI = \left\vert \frac{-1/N(A)}{G(j\omega)} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{1}{G(j\omega) \cdot N(A)} \right\vert$$

Equivalently, the **distance** between $G(j\omega)$ and the NIDF locus:

$$d = \left\vert G(j\omega) + \frac{1}{N(A)} \right\vert$$

- Larger $d$ → greater relative stability margin.
- The system is robust as long as no operating-amplitude trajectory $A(t)$ causes $G(j\omega)$ to approach the NIDF locus too closely.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Where Computed** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Limit cycle condition | $G(j\omega_0) = -1/N(A_0)$ | At the intersection point | Existence of periodic oscillation |
| Equivalent Gain Margin | $GM_{eq} = \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \cdot \vert N(A_{op}) \vert$ | At $\omega_{pc}$ where $\angle G = -180°$ | Robustness to gain variations |
| Equivalent Phase Margin | $PM_{eq} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) + \angle N(A_{op})$ | At $\omega_{gc}$ where $\vert G \vert = 1$ | Robustness to phase variations |
| Stability Index (Gelb) | $SI = \vert 1/N(A) \vert \cdot \vert 1/G(j\omega) \vert$ | At any operating frequency | Unified robustness metric |
| Geometric Distance | $d = \vert G(j\omega) + 1/N(A) \vert$ | At operating point | Pure spatial stability margin |
| Stable Limit Cycle (Kochenburger) | $-1/N(A)$ moves from unstable → stable region as $A \uparrow$ | At intersection | Self-correcting oscillation |
| Unstable Limit Cycle | $-1/N(A)$ moves from stable → unstable region as $A \uparrow$ | At intersection | Diverging oscillation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** *"Gain at Phase-Crossover, Phase at Gain-Crossover"* — EGM uses $\omega_{pc}$, EPM uses $\omega_{gc}$. This mirrors the linear-system convention but with the NIDF replacing the static point $-1 + j0$.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

This framework is critical in the design of:
- **Servo systems with saturating actuators** (saturation DF)
- **Systems with backlash or dead-zone** (multistable DF)
- **On-off / relay controllers** (relay DF gives a vertical $-1/N$ locus)
- **Aircraft flight control systems** with bounded control surfaces
- **Process control** with valve saturation and quantization effects

In all these, the *operating amplitude* itself changes the effective gain and phase of the nonlinearity, making **amplitude-dependent margins** indispensable.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Limit Cycle Existence Condition

Consider the standard nonlinear feedback structure with linear plant $G(s)$ and nonlinearity $N(\cdot)$ characterised by DF $N(A)$.

The closed-loop characteristic equation under DF approximation becomes:

$$1 + G(j\omega) \cdot N(A) = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$G(j\omega) \cdot N(A) = -1$$

Splitting into real and imaginary parts gives **two simultaneous equations** in the two unknowns $A_0$ and $\omega_0$:

$$\text{Re}\{G(j\omega_0) \cdot N(A_0)\} = -1$$

$$\text{Im}\{G(j\omega_0) \cdot N(A_0)\} = 0$$

Or equivalently, the locus intersection condition:

$$G(j\omega_0) = -\frac{1}{N(A_0)}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** whose solution yields the limit cycle parameters.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Equivalent Gain Margin (EGM)

**Step 1 — Definition of $\omega_{pc}$:**  
The phase-crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ satisfies:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$$

Therefore $G(j\omega_{pc})$ is a purely negative real number:

$$G(j\omega_{pc}) = -\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$

**Step 2 — Stability Condition in Linear Theory:**  
For a stable linear system, the Nyquist plot must not encircle $-1 + j0$. The gain margin is defined as the factor by which gain can be increased before the locus just touches $-1 + j0$.

**Step 3 — Nonlinear Extension:**  
Replace $-1 + j0$ by $-1/N(A_{op})$ (which is also a negative-real-like quantity if $N(A_{op})$ is real, as in odd nonlinearities). The system remains stable as long as $G(j\omega_{pc})$ stays "to the right of" $-1/N(A_{op})$ on the real axis:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert < \frac{1}{\vert N(A_{op}) \vert}$$

**Step 4 — Define EGM as the ratio of these quantities:**

$$GM_{eq} = \frac{\text{distance from origin to } G(j\omega_{pc})}{\text{distance from origin to } -1/N(A_{op})}$$

That is:

$$\boxed{GM_{eq} = \frac{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}{\vert 1/N(A_{op}) \vert} = \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \cdot \vert N(A_{op}) \vert}$$

**Stability criterion:** $GM_{eq} < 1$ for stability (since the operating point is then on the safe side of the NIDF).

> **Note:** The direction of inequality ($<$ vs. $>$) depends on the convention used (whether $GM_{eq} > 1$ means safe or unsafe). KTU consistently uses: **$GM_{eq} > 1$ implies unstable** when the NIDF is on the negative real axis and the Nyquist plot is on its left for stability.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Equivalent Phase Margin (EPM)

**Step 1 — Definition of $\omega_{gc}$:**  
The gain-crossover frequency satisfies:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$$

**Step 2 — Argument of the loop transfer function:**  
At this frequency, the loop transfer function (linear times DF) has argument:

$$\angle\{G(j\omega_{gc}) \cdot N(A_{op})\} = \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) + \angle N(A_{op})$$

**Step 3 — Linear EPM Definition:**  
In linear systems, $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ is the angle by which phase can be reduced before instability.

**Step 4 — Nonlinear Extension:**  
Now the critical point is $-1/N(A_{op})$, not $-1 + j0$. The system is stable if the vector from origin to $-1/N(A_{op})$ lies "ahead of" $G(j\omega_{gc})$ in the clockwise sense. The angle between them gives the margin:

$$PM_{eq} = \angle\left\{-\frac{1}{N(A_{op})}\right\} - \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$$

Since $\angle\{-1\} = 180°$ and $\angle N(A_{op}) = -\angle\{1/N(A_{op})\}$:

$$PM_{eq} = 180° - \angle\{1/N(A_{op})\} - \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$$

Since $\angle\{1/N(A_{op})\} = -\angle N(A_{op})$:

$$\boxed{PM_{eq} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) + \angle N(A_{op})}$$

**Stability criterion:** $PM_{eq} > 0$ for stability.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem:** A nonlinear system has linear part $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$ and a saturation nonlinearity with describing function (idealized): $N(A) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right) + \dfrac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right)^2}\right]$ where $a = 1$, $k = 1$.
>
> The system operates at amplitude $A_{op} = 3$. Determine whether the limit cycle at this amplitude is stable and compute the relative stability margins.

**Step 1 — Compute $N(A_{op})$:**  
With $a/A = 1/3$:

$$\sin^{-1}(1/3) = 0.3398 \text{ rad}$$

$$\sqrt{1 - (1/3)^2} = \sqrt{8/9} = 0.9428$$

$$\frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-(a/A)^2} = \frac{1}{3}(0.9428) = 0.3143$$

$$N(3) = \frac{2}{\pi}\left[0.3398 + 0.3143\right] = \frac{2}{\pi}(0.6541) = 0.4164$$

Since saturation is an odd memoryless nonlinearity, $\angle N(3) = 0°$.

**Step 2 — Compute $-1/N(3)$:**

$$-\frac{1}{N(3)} = -\frac{1}{0.4164} = -2.402$$

So the NIDF point is on the **negative real axis** at $-2.402$.

**Step 3 — Find $\omega_{pc}$ for $G(s)$:**  
$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{10}{j\omega(1+j\omega)(2+j\omega)}$

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/2)$$

Setting this equal to $-180°$:

$$90° + \tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) = 180°$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) = 90°$$

Using $\tan(A+B) = \infty$ (since $A + B = 90°$), we have $\tan^{-1}(\omega)\tan^{-1}(\omega/2) = -1$ (or $A + B = 90°$ implies $\tan A \tan B = 1$ when $\tan A > 0$):

$$\omega \cdot \frac{\omega}{2} = 1 \implies \omega^2 = 2 \implies \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$:**  

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{2}) \vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1+2} \cdot \sqrt{4+2}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{3}\cdot\sqrt{6}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{36}} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.667$$

**Step 5 — Compute $GM_{eq}$:**

$$GM_{eq} = \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \cdot \vert N(3) \vert = 1.667 \times 0.4164 = 0.694$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Since $GM_{eq} = 0.694 < 1$, the operating point at $A = 3$ lies on the *unstable side* of the NIDF, meaning the system **has insufficient gain margin** and is operating too close to instability. This is consistent with a large-amplitude limit cycle that is on the verge of destabilising.

### 3.5 Derivation Summary Matrix

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula Used** | **Numerical Value (Example)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Describing function at op. amplitude | $N(A_{op})$ | Saturation DF formula | $0.4164$ |
| NIDF point | $-1/N(A_{op})$ | Reciprocal and negation | $-2.402$ |
| Phase crossover frequency | $\omega_{pc}$ | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$ | $1.414$ rad/s |
| Plant magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ | $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | Magnitude formula | $1.667$ |
| Equivalent Gain Margin | $GM_{eq}$ | $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \cdot \vert N(A_{op}) \vert$ | $0.694$ |
| Equivalent Phase Margin | $PM_{eq}$ | $180° + \angle G + \angle N$ | (computed similarly) |
| Stability verdict | — | $GM_{eq} < 1$ or $PM_{eq} > 0$ | Unstable / marginally stable |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Procedure for Assessing Relative Stability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System with Nonlinearity and Linear Plant Gs] --> B[Derive Describing Function NA for the nonlinearity]
    B --> C[Plot Nyquist locus Gjomega in complex plane]
    C --> D[Plot Negative Inverse DF locus -1 slash NA as A varies]
    D --> E{Do Gjomega and -1 slash NA intersect?}
    E -- No --> F[No Limit Cycle: System is asymptotically stable]
    E -- Yes --> G[Intersection point gives limit cycle parameters A0 and omega0]
    G --> H[Apply Kochenburger Rule to test limit cycle stability]
    H --> I{Is limit cycle stable?}
    I -- No --> J[Unstable limit cycle: System is unstable]
    I -- Yes --> K[Identify operating amplitude Aop]
    K --> L[Compute N at Aop]
    L --> M[Find phase crossover frequency omega pc of Gjomega]
    M --> N[Compute Equivalent Gain Margin GMeq]
    L --> O[Find gain crossover frequency omega gc of Gjomega]
    O --> P[Compute Equivalent Phase Margin PMeq]
    N --> Q{GMeq greater than 1?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Insufficient margin: System at risk]
    Q -- No --> S[Good gain margin: System robust]
    P --> T{PMeq greater than 0?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Positive phase margin: Stable operation]
    T -- No --> V[Negative phase margin: Unstable operation]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Nonlinear System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input R] --> Sum1[ Summing Junction]
    Sum1 --> Comp[Comparator: e = R - Y]
    Comp --> NL[ Nonlinearity N of e]
    NL --> GainDF[ Describing Function NA]
    GainDF --> Plant[ Linear Plant Gs]
    Plant --> Output[ Output Y]
    Output --> FbPath[ Feedback Path: Unity]
    FbPath --> Sum1
    Plant -.-> NyquistPlot[ Nyquist locus Gjomega]
    NL -.-> NIDFLocus[ NIDF locus -1 slash NA]
    NyquistPlot --> Assess[ Relative Stability Assessment]
    NIDFLocus --> Assess
    Assess --> MarginOut[ Gain and Phase Margins]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Plot — Geometric Interpretation (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Complex Plane (GH-plane equivalent)"
        P1[ Origin 0,0]
        P2[ Critical Locus -1 slash NA, amplitude dependent curve]
        P3[ Nyquist Locus Gjomega, swept over frequency]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 -- Operating Point --> OpPt[ Point at A equals Aop]
    P3 -- At Phase Crossover --> PcPt[ Point at omega equals omega pc]
    OpPt --> DistLine[ Distance d, the stability index]
    PcPt --> DistLine
```

### 4.4 Logical Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA[Step 1: Identify Nonlinear Element Type] --> StepB[Step 2: Derive Describing Function NA]
    StepB --> StepC[Step 3: Compute -1 slash NA locus]
    StepC --> StepD[Step 4: Plot Gjomega locus from linear part]
    StepD --> StepE[Step 5: Locate Intersection for Limit Cycle]
    StepE --> StepF{Intersection Exists?}
    StepF -- No --> StepG[Report: No Oscillation, Check Absolute Stability]
    StepF -- Yes --> StepH[Step 6: Test Kochenburger Stability Rule]
    StepH --> StepI[Step 7: Identify Operating Amplitude Aop]
    StepI --> StepJ[Step 8: Compute EGM and EPM]
    StepJ --> StepK[Step 9: Report Relative Stability Margins]
    StepK --> StepL[Design Recommendation if needed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Flow Instead of Physical Drawing?**
> Mermaid cannot render actual Nyquist plots with curves directly, so the diagrams above depict the **logical architecture** and **decision flow** of the relative stability analysis procedure. For the actual Nyquist/NIDF plot in your exam answer sheet, draw the curves on graph paper showing the intersection point, the operating point, and the distance vector $d$.
